description.description: Bunny is the nickname of Harry Manders, Raffles's school friend and accomplice, used throughout the Raffles stories from The Ides of March in The Amateur Cracksman onward.
Dr. Hill Barton is the false identity Watson assumes at Holmes's instruction in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client from The Case-Book, presenting himself as an expert on Chinese pottery.
Escott is the plumber's identity Holmes adopts in The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton to court Milverton's housemaid Agatha and gain intelligence about the blackmailer's house.

Killer Evans / James Winter / Morecroft is the triple alias of the American criminal John Garrideb's antagonist in The Adventure of the Three Garridebs from The Case-Book, sought for a Chicago murder.
The Amateur Cracksman is the epithet applied to A. J. Raffles as the title of E. W. Hornung's first Raffles collection, reflecting his status as a skilled gentlemanly burglar.

Sigerson is the alias under which Sherlock Holmes travels in Tibet and elsewhere during the Great Hiatus after Reichenbach, revealed to Watson in The Adventure of the Empty House in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
Arthur Conan Doyle, the British author who created Sherlock Holmes and Dr. Watson, writing the complete canonical Holmes stories from A Study in Scarlet through The Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes.

E.W. Hornung, the British author who created the gentleman-burglar A.J. Raffles and his narrator Bunny Manders across four books: The Amateur Cracksman, Raffles: Further Adventures, A Thief in the Night, and Mr. Justice Raffles.
G. K. Chesterton is the English author who created Father Brown, writing The Innocence of Father Brown (1911), The Wisdom of Father Brown (1914), and The Secret of Father Brown (1927).

Maurice Leblanc, the French author who created the gentleman-burglar Arsene Lupin, writing the series novels including The Extraordinary Adventures, The Blonde Lady, The Hollow Needle, and The Crystal Stopper.

R. Austin Freeman, the British author who created the medico-legal detective Dr. John Thorndyke, writing The Red Thumb Mark, John Thorndyke's Cases, The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke, and The Vanishing Man.

The Agra Treasure Mystery, the central case of The Sign of the Four in which Holmes and Watson are engaged by Mary Morstan to investigate mysterious annual pearls she has received, uncovering the story of the Agra Treasure and Jonathan Small.

The Arrest on La Provence is the case opening The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin, in which a wireless message warns first-cabin passengers that a blonde man with a wounded right forearm travelling as 'R.' is Arsène Lupin.

The Disappearance of John Bellingham is the central case of The Vanishing Man (The Eye of Osiris) by R. Austin Freeman, in which Bellingham vanishes from the Queen Square museum and his solicitor Jellicoe conceals an accidental death by hiding the body.
The Birlstone Murder Case is the locked-room mystery at Birlstone Manor that Holmes investigates in The Valley of Fear, ultimately revealed as an elaborate deception staged by John Douglas.

The Black Pearl Murder is the case in The Extraordinary Adventures in which Victor Danègre murders the Countess Zalti and steals her celebrated black pearl, later manipulated by Lupin who acquires evidence of the crime.
The Blonde Lady Case, the mystery in Maurice Leblanc's novel involving the fair-haired Clotilde Destange who aided Lupin in a series of Parisian thefts and vanished after his second arrest.

The Death of Edith Grant (Railway Accident) is the case in John Thorndyke's Cases known as The Blue Sequin, in which Thorndyke determines that Miss Grant's death was caused by a steer's horn projecting from a cattle-truck at a railway siding.
The Bruce-Partington Plans Theft, the case in His Last Bow in which Holmes and Mycroft investigate the murder of Arthur Cadogan West and the theft of secret submarine plans by the spy Hugo Oberstein.

The Charmerace Burglary is the Lupin case in Arsène Lupin (the novel) in which Lupin, posing as the Duke of Charmerace, arranges the theft of the château's motor-cars as the opening stage of his elaborate plan.
The Criminologists' Club Case is the Raffles story from A Thief in the Night in which Raffles is invited to dine with the very criminologists who study him and takes the opportunity to rob them.

The Devil's Foot Poisoning, the case in His Last Bow set in Cornwall in which Brenda Tregennis is found dead and her brothers insane, traced to a combustion poison derived from the Devil's Foot Root, deployed by Mortimer Tregennis and avenged by Dr. Sterndale.
The Murder of Enoch Drebber is the central crime in A Study in Scarlet, a poisoning committed by Jefferson Hope in a Brixton house, with the cryptic word RACHE written in blood at the scene.

The Dying Detective Poisoning Plot, the scheme in His Last Bow in which Holmes feigns illness caused by a poison from an ivory box to trap the Sumatran planter Culverton Smith into confessing the murder of his nephew Victor Savage.

The Escape from the Santé is the Lupin case from The Extraordinary Adventures in which Arsène Lupin escapes from the Prison de la Santé in Paris while supposedly still imprisoned, under the nose of Ganimard and the entire police.

The Garcia Murder (Wisteria Lodge), the case in His Last Bow in which Aloysius Garcia of Wisteria Lodge near Esher is found dead, revealing a conspiracy against the exiled San Pedro dictator Don Juan Murillo, investigated by Inspector Baynes and Holmes.
The Gift of the Emperor Case is the Raffles adventure from The Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles steals an emperor's pearl given as a diplomatic gift.

The Gilbert and Vaucheray Case is the crisis of The Crystal Stopper in which Lupin's accomplices Gilbert and Vaucheray are arrested for the murder at the Villa Marie-Thérèse, sentenced to death, and can only be saved if Lupin recovers the crystal stopper.

The Murder of Alfred Hartridge is the case in John Thorndyke's Cases in which Thorndyke and Jervis establish that the victim was killed by a dagger fired as a projectile from a rifle across thirty yards, with the engraving 'TRADITORE' on the aluminium guard.

The Murder of Charles Hearn by Morphia Poisoning, a case in John Thorndyke's Cases in which Dr. Thorndyke deduces that Hearn's body was transported by boat to Sundersley Gap and that the murder involved three men including Arthur Jezzard.

The Secret of the Hollow Needle is the central case of The Hollow Needle, in which Isidore Beautrelet uncovers that Arsène Lupin guards the greatest historic secret in France — the treasure of the Kings hidden in the cliffs at Étretat.

The Hornby Diamond Theft and Fingerprint Forgery, the case in The Red Thumb Mark in which diamonds worth around 25,000 pounds were stolen from John Hornby's safe and the blame falsely laid on Reuben Hornby through a forged thumb-print made by Walter Hornby.

The Hound of the Baskervilles Case, Holmes's investigation into the supernatural threat to the Baskerville family on Dartmoor, involving the murder of Sir Charles and the attempted murder of Sir Henry by the naturalist Jack Stapleton.
The Ides of March Case is the opening story of The Amateur Cracksman in which a desperate Bunny arrives at Raffles's Albany rooms and is recruited into his first burglary.

The Imbert Safe Affair is the Lupin exploit from The Extraordinary Adventures in which Arsène Lupin empties the Imbert family safe only to discover the securities inside are worthless counterfeit bonds.

The Jewish Lamp Case, the second episode of The Blonde Lady in which Holmlock Shears is hired to recover a stolen ancient lamp from the d'Imblevalle house on the Rue Murillo in Paris, solving the theft but finding that Lupin had already taken what he truly sought.

The Lady Frances Carfax Disappearance, a case in His Last Bow in which Holmes tracks the missing wealthy widow Lady Frances Carfax, kidnapped by the criminal Henry Peters (Holy Peters) and his wife who attempted to bury her alive.

The Löwe Burglary by Japanese Criminals is the case in John Thorndyke's Cases known as The Anthropologist at Large, in which Japanese art works worth £8,000–£12,000 are stolen from Isaac Löwe's Piccadilly house, Thorndyke deducing the burglar's origin from a brachycephalic hat.

The Malaquis Castle Burglary is the Lupin case in The Extraordinary Adventures in which Lupin sends Baron de Cahorn a letter warning of the date he will rob Château de Malaquis and then makes good on the threat despite all precautions.

The Mandarin Pearl Suicide Plot, a case in John Thorndyke's Cases in which Dr. Thorndyke exposes the scheme of Captain Raggerton and Alfred Calverley's confederate to drive a victim to suicide through a fabricated story about a mandarin's cursed pearl.

The Murder of Minna Adler (deep-sea sand case) is the Thorndyke case in John Thorndyke's Cases in which hair planted in the victim's hand to mislead investigators reveals the crime was premeditated, with deep-sea sand from Turkey providing a key trace.
Mr. Justice Raffles vs Dan Levy is the case in the novel of that name in which Raffles places himself above the law to punish the moneylender Levy, acting as a self-appointed judge against extortion.

The Murder of Oscar Brodski is the case in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke in which Thorndyke and Jervis investigate the death of a diamond merchant, whose murder by Silas Hickler is exposed through meticulous trace evidence.

The Piccadilly Jewel Robbery (Moabite Cipher Case) is the case in John Thorndyke's Cases in which a hidden message activated by water reveals the cipher 'THE PICKERDILLEY STUF IS UP THE CHIMBLY 416 WARDOUR ST 2ND FLOUR BACK', locating the jewel loot.
The Theft of the Queen's Necklace is the Lupin case in The Extraordinary Adventures in which Arsène Lupin steals the historic necklace from the cabinet of Count de Dreux-Soubise.

The Red Circle Murder, a case in His Last Bow in which Holmes assists Leverton of the Pinkerton agency to locate the fugitive Gennaro Lucca after his wife seeks help, culminating in the discovery of the dead Giuseppe Gorgiano.

The Seven of Hearts Mystery is a Lupin case from The Extraordinary Adventures in which a series of seven-of-hearts playing cards arrive as cryptic warnings, drawing Leblanc himself into the orbit of Lupin's schemes.

The murder of Joseph Stangerson, stabbed to the heart at Halliday's Private Hotel near Euston Station with the word RACHE written in blood above him, investigated by Inspector Lestrade and connected to Jefferson Hope's vendetta.

The Thibermesnil Burglary is the Lupin case in The Extraordinary Adventures in which Lupin burgles Château de Thibermesnil and escapes before Sherlock Holmes (Herlock Sholmès) can arrive at the scene.

The Von Bork German Spy Operation, the espionage network depicted in His Last Bow in which Holmes, working under the alias Altamont for two years, infiltrates and dismantles Von Bork's ring of British naval intelligence gathering on the eve of World War One.
A Bad Night, a story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles and Bunny encounter young Medlicott during an operation that goes wrong, testing Bunny's nerve and loyalty.

A Trap to Catch a Cracksman, a story in A Thief in the Night in which the escaped convict Crawshay and the Irish criminal Barney Maguire conspire to catch Raffles in a counter-trap that Bunny must help navigate.
Out of Paradise, a story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles and Bunny become entangled with Lord Lochmaben in an adventure that blends criminal enterprise with unexpected moral complications.
The Chest of Silver, a story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles and Bunny target a chest of silver connected to the City and Suburban Bank, navigating the elaborate risks of the operation.

The Criminologists' Club, a story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles and Bunny attend a dinner of the Earl of Thornaby's crime-enthusiast club and Raffles commits a theft under the noses of professional criminologists.
The Field of Phillipi, a story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles and Bunny encounter Nipper Nasmyth in circumstances that recall Bunny's past dealings and test their partnership.
The Raffles Relics is a story in A Thief in the Night in which Bunny views the collection of Raffles's burglar tools held in the Black Museum at Scotland Yard after Raffles's death.
The Rest Cure, a story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles and Bunny cross paths with Colonel Crutchley during what was meant to be a period of quiet recovery from their criminal activities.

The Spoils of Sacrilege, a story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles and Bunny commit a church robbery alongside the character Guillemard, raising moral questions about desecrating sacred spaces.
A Case of Premeditation is a story in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke involving the elimination of Mr. Pratt through identity fraud to claim an inheritance, investigated by Thorndyke and Jervis.
Part I of "A Case of Premeditation" in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke ("The Elimination of Mr. Pratt"), in which the mysterious disappearance of a man named Pratt begins a case of planned murder.

The Echo of a Mutiny is a story in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke in which Thorndyke deduces from freshwater diatoms in a victim's lung tissue that the drowning occurred in a river, not in the Thames as staged.
Part I of "The Echo of a Mutiny" in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke ("Death on the Girdler"), set on the Thames estuary where a body is discovered and Thorndyke investigates its origins.

The Case of Oscar Brodski, the opening story of The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke in which Thorndyke and Jervis reconstruct a railway murder from physical evidence, including grit embedded in the victim's welt, deducing that the killer walked the track after the crime.
Part I of "The Case of Oscar Brodski" in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke, in which a diamond merchant is found dead on the railway and Thorndyke begins his forensic examination of the scene.
The Old Lag is a story in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke in which the distinctive crescent-shaped wear pattern in a boot impression from soft earth becomes a key clue identifying the culprit.
Part I of "The Old Lag" in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke ("The Changed Immutable"), following an ex-convict whose attempt to reform is entangled in a crime that Thorndyke investigates.

A Wastrel's Romance is a story in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke in which the age of a tattoo — determined by the depth of ink migration below the skin — establishes a crucial timeline in the investigation.
Part I of "A Wastrel's Romance" in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke ("The Spinsters' Guest"), in which a mysterious lodger's arrival at a spinsters' household sets a complex case in motion.
The Adventure of the Beryl Coronet is Adventure XI in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes, in which a banker's son is suspected of stealing a segment from a royal coronet entrusted to his father.
The Adventure of the Blue Carbuncle is Adventure VII in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes, in which Holmes traces a stolen gemstone from a battered hat and a Christmas goose to a hotel theft.
The Boscombe Valley Mystery is Adventure IV in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes, in which Holmes clears a young man accused of his father's murder beside a Herefordshire pool.

A Case of Identity is Adventure III in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes, in which Holmes exposes a stepfather who disguised himself as a suitor to prevent his stepdaughter from marrying and controlling her income.
The Adventure of the Copper Beeches is Adventure XII in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes, in which Violet Hunter is hired under suspicious conditions by Jephro Rucastle near Winchester.

The Adventure of the Engineer's Thumb is Adventure IX in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes, in which a hydraulic engineer survives an assassination attempt at a counterfeit press and brings his case to Watson.
The Five Orange Pips is Adventure V in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes, in which young John Openshaw receives the Ku Klux Klan's death warning of five orange pips before meeting a mysterious end.
The Man with the Twisted Lip is Adventure VI in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes, in which a respectable man's double life as a beggar is uncovered when he apparently disappears.

The Adventure of the Noble Bachelor is Adventure X in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes, in which a lord's new American bride vanishes from her wedding reception, her prior marriage providing the solution.
The Red-Headed League is Adventure II in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes, in which Holmes exposes John Clay's bank tunnel scheme concealed behind a fake clerical employment agency.
A Scandal in Bohemia is Adventure I in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes, in which Irene Adler outwits Holmes and the King of Bohemia to keep a compromising photograph.

The Adventure of the Speckled Band is Adventure VIII in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes, in which Dr. Grimesby Roylott uses a trained swamp adder to murder his stepdaughter and is killed by his own weapon.

Chapter I: The Millionaire's Daughter, the opening chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which the Duke of Charmerace meets Germaine Gournay-Martin and Sonia Kritchnoff is introduced alongside M. Gournay-Martin.

Chapter II: The Coming of the Charolais, the chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which the suspicious Charolais family arrives and Lupin's schemes around the Gournay-Martin household begin to unfold.
Chapter III: Lupin's Way, the chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which Lupin's methods are pitted against the detective Guerchard and Holmlock Shears at the Duke of Charmerace's instigation.

Chapter IV: The Duke Intervenes, the chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which the Duke of Charmerace steps in to mediate between Lupin's schemes and the Gournay-Martin household, with Sonia Kritchnoff and the Charolais in play.

Chapter IX: M. Formery Opens the Inquiry, the chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which the examining magistrate M. Formery begins the official investigation at the Gournay-Martin mansion with the Charolais in attendance.
Chapter V: A Letter from Lupin, the chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which Lupin sends a provocative letter warning the Gournay-Martin household of his intentions, drawing in the Charolais gang.
Chapter VI: Again the Charolais, the chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which the Charolais family's involvement in Lupin's scheme deepens around the Gournay-Martin mansion.

Chapter VII: The Theft of the Motor-Cars, the chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which Lupin's gang, including the Charolais, steals motor-cars from the Gournay-Martin estate as part of Lupin's wider scheme.
Chapter VIII: The Duke Arrives, the chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which the Duke of Charmerace returns to the Gournay-Martin mansion as Guerchard and M. Formery press their investigation.
Chapter X: Guerchard Assists, a chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which the detective Guerchard joins the investigation at the Gournay-Martin mansion alongside M. Formery.
Chapter XI: The Family Arrives, a chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which the Charolais family and the Gournay-Martin household converge at the mansion during Guerchard's investigation.
Chapter XII: The Theft of the Pendant, a chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which a pendant is stolen from the Gournay-Martin household while Guerchard and M. Formery investigate.

Chapter XIII: Lupin Wires, the chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which Lupin sends telegrams to confuse and outmanoeuvre Guerchard and M. Formery while Sonia Kritchnoff remains under suspicion at the Gournay-Martin mansion.

Chapter XIV: Guerchard Picks Up the True Scent, the chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which the detective Guerchard begins to suspect the Duke of Charmerace's true identity as the trail of clues leads closer to Lupin.

Chapter XIX: The Duke Goes, the chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which the Duke of Charmerace departs as Guerchard and M. Formery close in and the confrontation with Lupin reaches its penultimate stage.

Chapter XV: The Examination of Sonia, the chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which Guerchard and M. Formery interrogate Sonia Kritchnoff as part of the investigation into the Duke of Charmerace and Lupin.

Chapter XVI: Victoire's Slip, the chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which Lupin's loyal old servant Victoire inadvertently reveals information to Guerchard, threatening to expose the Duke of Charmerace's secret identity.
Chapter XVII: Sonia's Escape, a chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which Sonia Kritchnoff escapes during Guerchard's pursuit of Lupin through the Duke of Charmerace's mansion.

Chapter XVIII: The Duke Stays, the chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which the Duke of Charmerace remains at the Gournay-Martin mansion despite Guerchard's suspicions, playing out his masquerade as the endgame approaches.
Chapter XX: Lupin Comes Home, the chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which Lupin returns to the Gournay-Martin mansion as the endgame between him and Guerchard approaches.
Chapter XXI: The Cutting of the Telephone Wires, a chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which Lupin disrupts communications as part of his duel with detective Guerchard.
Chapter XXII: The Bargain, a chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which Lupin negotiates terms with his adversaries Guerchard and the Gournay-Martin household.

Chapter XXIII: The End of the Duel, the climactic closing chapter of the Arsene Lupin novel in which the final confrontation between Lupin (as the Duke of Charmerace) and Guerchard is resolved, allowing Lupin and Sonia to escape.
Part I, Chapter I of A Study in Scarlet introduces Dr. John H. Watson as the first-person narrator, describing his medical degree and his wounding at the Battle of Maiwand in Afghanistan.

Part I, Chapter II: The Science of Deduction is the chapter of A Study in Scarlet in which Watson recalls Holmes's early Baker Street days and Watson first reads Holmes's 'Book of Life' article in a magazine.

Part I, Chapter III: The Lauriston Garden Mystery, the chapter of A Study in Scarlet in which Holmes and Watson visit 3 Lauriston Gardens, Holmes deduces the murderer's height, gait and origin from the crime scene, and Lestrade and Gregson compete for credit.

Part I, Chapter IV: What John Rance Had to Tell is the chapter of A Study in Scarlet in which Constable Rance recounts his discovery of Drebber's body and his encounter with a drunk whom he allowed to slip away (Jefferson Hope in disguise).

Part I, Chapter V: Our Advertisement Brings a Visitor is the chapter of A Study in Scarlet in which Holmes places a newspaper advertisement for a found plain gold wedding ring to lure the murderer back, successfully drawing in Mrs Sawyer.

Part I, Chapter VI: Tobias Gregson Shows What He Can Do, the chapter of A Study in Scarlet in which Gregson presents his evidence about the murder of Joseph Stangerson at Halliday's Private Hotel, with Wiggins and the Baker Street Irregulars also appearing.
Part I, Chapter VII: Light in the Darkness is the chapter of A Study in Scarlet in which Jefferson Hope, captured, reveals his identity and his motivation for the two killings.
Part II, Chapter I: On the Great Alkali Plain is the chapter of A Study in Scarlet that opens the American backstory, describing the vast desert and the wagon train rescued by Brigham Young's people.
Part II, Chapter II: The Flower of Utah is the chapter of A Study in Scarlet in which Lucy Ferrier grows up within the log-house helping her adopted father John Ferrier.

Part II, Chapter III: John Ferrier Talks with the Prophet is the chapter of A Study in Scarlet in which Brigham Young rides to Ferrier's door and delivers the ultimatum that Lucy must marry a Mormon within a month.

Part II, Chapter IV: A Flight for Life is the chapter of A Study in Scarlet in which John Ferrier and Jefferson Hope flee with Lucy through mountain passes, Ferrier eventually overtaken and killed by the Danite Band.

Part II, Chapter V: The Avenging Angels is the chapter in A Study in Scarlet's American backstory section depicting the Mormon enforcement squad's pursuit of Ferrier and Jefferson Hope through the mountains.

Part II, Chapter VI: A Continuation of the Reminiscences of John H. Watson, M.D., the chapter of A Study in Scarlet set in late September in which Watson narrates Jefferson Hope's capture, confession, and sudden death in custody.

Part II, Chapter VII: The Conclusion is the final chapter of A Study in Scarlet in which the principals appear before the magistrates and Holmes explains his full chain of reasoning to Watson and Lestrade.

First Episode: The Blonde Lady, Chapter I: Number 514, Series 23, the opening chapter of The Blonde Lady in which a mysterious coded message draws Arsene Lupin and Inspector Ganimard into the blonde lady affair.

First Episode: The Blonde Lady, Chapter II: The Blue Diamond, the chapter of The Blonde Lady in which a blue diamond stolen from the d'Imblevalle household becomes the focus of Holmlock Shears's investigation and Lupin's counter-moves.

First Episode: The Blonde Lady, Chapter III: Holmlock Shears Opens Hostilities, the chapter in The Blonde Lady in which the English detective Shears, accompanied by Wilson, begins his active pursuit of Lupin across Paris.

First Episode: The Blonde Lady, Chapter IV: A Glimmer in the Darkness, the chapter in The Blonde Lady in which Shears and Wilson follow a new lead in the blonde lady investigation as Lupin and Ganimard compete to control events.

First Episode: The Blonde Lady, Chapter V: Kidnapped, the chapter in The Blonde Lady in which Holmlock Shears is kidnapped by Lupin's agents, temporarily removing the English detective from the field.

First Episode: The Blonde Lady, Chapter VI: The Second Arrest of Arsene Lupin, the chapter in The Blonde Lady in which Lupin is arrested for the second time by Inspector Ganimard, with Shears and Wilson present.

Second Episode: The Jewish Lamp, Chapter I, the opening chapter of the second episode in The Blonde Lady in which Shears and Wilson are engaged to recover the stolen Jewish lamp from the d'Imblevalle house on the Rue Murillo.

Second Episode: The Jewish Lamp, Chapter II, the chapter in The Blonde Lady in which Holmlock Shears closes in on the solution to the Jewish lamp theft, only for Lupin to demonstrate he has already recovered the item he truly sought.

Chapter I: The Arrests, the opening chapter of The Crystal Stopper in which Gilbert and Vaucheray are arrested and Arsene Lupin is drawn into the struggle against the blackmailer Daubrecq and the police chief Prasville.

Chapter II: Eight from Nine Leaves One, the chapter of The Crystal Stopper in which Lupin calculates that only one more name is needed to complete Daubrecq's blackmail list, as Gilbert and Vaucheray's fates converge.

Chapter III: The Home Life of Alexis Daubrecq, the chapter of The Crystal Stopper in which Lupin investigates the private routines and residence of the blackmailer Daubrecq, seeking the hiding place of the crystal stopper.

Chapter IV: The Chief of the Enemies, the chapter of The Crystal Stopper in which Lupin and Clarisse Mergy identify Alexis Daubrecq as the principal adversary holding the list of twenty-seven corrupt politicians.

Chapter IX: In the Dark, the chapter of The Crystal Stopper in which Lupin and Clarisse Mergy operate covertly to track the hidden crystal stopper through Daubrecq's apartments while Vaucheray's execution looms.
Chapter V: The Twenty-Seven, the chapter of The Crystal Stopper in which the secret list of twenty-seven compromised deputies is established as the core of Daubrecq's blackmailing power.

Chapter VI: The Death-Sentence, the chapter of The Crystal Stopper in which Gilbert receives his death sentence and Clarisse Mergy's desperate efforts to find the crystal stopper before his execution become the novel's driving force.

Chapter VII: The Profile of Napoleon, the chapter of The Crystal Stopper in which Lupin searches Daubrecq's residence and discovers a Napoleonic profile clue that conceals the hiding place of the crystal stopper.

Chapter VIII: The Lovers' Tower, the chapter of The Crystal Stopper in which Lupin and Clarisse Mergy trace the crystal stopper to a secret location known as the Lovers' Tower as the execution date approaches.

Chapter X: Extra-Dry?, the chapter of The Crystal Stopper in which Lupin uses a champagne-related code to narrow his search for the crystal stopper while Prasville and Daubrecq continue their antagonism.

Chapter XI: The Cross of Lorraine, the chapter of The Crystal Stopper in which the Cross of Lorraine symbol provides a crucial clue to the hiding place of the crystal stopper in Lupin's race against Gilbert's execution date.

Chapter XII: The Scaffold, the chapter of The Crystal Stopper in which Gilbert faces the guillotine and Lupin and Clarisse race desperately to recover the crystal stopper from Daubrecq in time to save him.
Chapter XIII: The Last Battle, the climactic chapter of The Crystal Stopper in which Lupin confronts Daubrecq and Prasville in a final struggle for the crystal stopper and Gilbert's life.
The Epilogue of The Valley of Fear discloses the fates of the principal characters, noting that John Douglas was lost overboard at sea and that Professor Moriarty's long reach was suspected.

Story I: The Arrest of Arsène Lupin is the opening story of The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin, Gentleman-Burglar, in which a wireless message warns passengers of La Provence that Lupin is aboard and Ganimard boards to arrest him.

Story II: Arsène Lupin in Prison is the second story of The Extraordinary Adventures, in which Lupin, jailed awaiting trial, continues directing burglaries from his cell, embarrassing the authorities.
Story III: The Escape of Arsène Lupin is the third story of The Extraordinary Adventures, in which Lupin escapes from the Santé Prison and vanishes from under Ganimard's surveillance.
Story IV: The Mysterious Traveller is the fourth story in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin, in which a mysterious passenger on a train becomes entangled with Lupin's operations.

Story IX: Sherlock Holmes Arrives Too Late, the ninth story in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsene Lupin in which Holmlock Shears arrives in France to apprehend Lupin but is consistently outmanoeuvred.
Story V: The Queen's Necklace is the fifth story in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin, recounting the theft of the Count de Dreux-Soubise's historic necklace.
Story VI: The Seven of Hearts is the sixth story in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin, in which a series of playing cards bearing the seven of hearts serve as mysterious warnings.
Story VII: Madame Imbert's Safe is the seventh story in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin, in which Lupin empties Madame Imbert's safe only to discover the securities are counterfeit.
Story VIII: The Black Pearl is the eighth story of The Extraordinary Adventures, in which Lupin manoeuvres to acquire the black pearl stolen by Victor Danègre from the murdered Countess.
The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans is a His Last Bow story in which Holmes recovers stolen submarine blueprints whose theft led to Arthur Cadogan West's murder on the London Underground.
The Adventure of the Devil's Foot is a His Last Bow story in which Holmes investigates a baffling death and madness among the Tregennis family in Cornwall, tracing the cause to a rare African poison.

The Adventure of the Dying Detective, a story in His Last Bow in which Holmes feigns a fatal illness to lure the murderer Culverton Smith into confessing, with Watson and Inspector Morton standing by.

His Last Bow: The War Service of Sherlock Holmes, the title story of the His Last Bow collection in which Holmes, operating undercover as Altamont, unmasks German spy Von Bork on the eve of the First World War.

The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax is a story in His Last Bow in which Holmes and Watson track a missing wealthy English spinster endangered by the criminal duo of Holy Peters and Dr. Shlessinger.

The Adventure of the Red Circle is a His Last Bow story in which Holmes unravels the mystery of a silent lodger, ultimately linked to the Italian criminal society the Red Circle and a New York fugitive.
The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge is a His Last Bow story in which Holmes investigates a mysterious country house connected to the fugitive South American dictator Don Juan Murillo.
Chapter One: The Shot is the opening chapter of The Hollow Needle, in which Isidore Beautrelet hears a shot and begins the investigation that leads him to Arsène Lupin's greatest secret.

Chapter Two: Isidore Beautrelet, Sixth-form Schoolboy, the chapter of The Hollow Needle in which the young amateur detective Beautrelet first investigates the mysterious case that will lead him to Lupin and the hollow needle.

Chapter Three: The Corpse, the chapter of The Hollow Needle in which Beautrelet discovers a body connected to Lupin's activities, drawing him deeper into the mystery of the Etretat needle and Lupin's secret.

Chapter Four: Face to Face, the chapter of The Hollow Needle in which Beautrelet and Arsene Lupin meet directly for the first time, beginning their cat-and-mouse confrontation over the secret of the hollow needle.

Chapter Nine: Open, Sesame!, the chapter of The Hollow Needle in which Beautrelet and Lupin discover or force open the entrance to the hidden chamber inside the Etretat needle, revealing its long-guarded treasure.
Chapter Five: On the Track is a chapter of The Hollow Needle in which Beautrelet closes in on the clues pointing to the secret of the Hollow Needle and Lupin's hiding place at Étretat.

Chapter Six: An Historic Secret, the chapter of The Hollow Needle in which Beautrelet uncovers archival evidence proving that the hollow needle of Etretat has concealed a royal secret since ancient times, with Lupin having known this all along.

Chapter Seven: The Treatise of the Needle is a chapter of The Hollow Needle in which Beautrelet discovers and deciphers the ancient treatise that explains the historic secret of the hollow rock formation.

Chapter Eight: From Caesar to Lupin, the chapter of The Hollow Needle in which Beautrelet and Ganimard trace the ancient secret of the hollow needle from Julius Caesar through French royal history to Lupin's mastery of it.

Chapter Ten: The Treasures of the Kings of France, the climactic chapter of The Hollow Needle in which Beautrelet and Lupin reach the hidden chamber inside the needle and discover the immense royal treasure accumulated there since Caesar's time.

A Message from the Deep Sea is a story in John Thorndyke's Cases in which Thorndyke identifies deep-sea sand from the Eastern Mediterranean floor in a murdered man's wound, providing key evidence about the killer's origin.

The Aluminium Dagger is the story in John Thorndyke's Cases in which Thorndyke proves that the victim was killed by an aluminium dagger bearing the inscription 'TRADITORE' fired as a projectile from a rifle across thirty yards.
The Anthropologist at Large is a story in John Thorndyke's Cases in which Thorndyke deduces from a hat's extreme roundness (cephalic index 95.6) that the burglar of Isaac Löwe's house was Japanese.

The Blue Sequin is a story in John Thorndyke's Cases in which Thorndyke determines that Miss Grant's death was caused by a steer's horn protruding from a cattle-truck striking her at a railway siding.

The Man with the Nailed Shoes, a story in John Thorndyke's Cases set in a remote East Coast village in which Dr. Thorndyke and Jervis investigate a mysterious death, deducing the truth from the distinctive nail pattern on the victim's shoes.

The Mandarin's Pearl is a story in John Thorndyke's Cases in which Thorndyke exposes how Raggerton created ghostly Chinese apparitions by dressing in disguise in a closet adjacent to a mirror angled to reflect into the room.

The Moabite Cipher is a story in John Thorndyke's Cases in which a hidden message is revealed by water activating a chemical treatment on the paper, exposing the location of jewel loot at 416 Wardour Street.
The Stranger's Latchkey is a story in John Thorndyke's Cases in which Thorndyke exposes Percy Haldean's plot to abduct six-year-old Freddy Barnard from Bradham Wood to prevent Freddy from inheriting.
The Adventure of the Cardboard Box is Story II in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, in which severed ears sent in a cardboard box to a Croydon spinster expose a double murder of jealousy.
The Crooked Man is Story VIII in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, in which Holmes clears a colonel's wife of suspicion in her husband's death by uncovering a decades-old betrayal in India.
The Final Problem is Story XII in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, in which Holmes confronts Professor Moriarty and both apparently fall to their deaths at Reichenbach Falls in Switzerland.
The 'Gloria Scott' is Story V in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, in which Holmes recounts his earliest case, involving a convict ship's dark secret revealed through a cipher message.

The Greek Interpreter is Story X in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, in which Holmes and his brother Mycroft become involved when a Greek interpreter is abducted and compelled to translate for kidnappers.
The Musgrave Ritual is Story VI in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, in which Holmes solves an old family catechism as a treasure map, recovering a lost crown at the cost of a butler's life.
The Naval Treaty is Story XI in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, in which Holmes recovers a stolen secret naval document from a young Foreign Office clerk's own household.
The Reigate Squires is Story VII in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, in which Holmes, recuperating in Surrey, uncovers the Cunningham family's robbery and murder scheme from a torn letter fragment.
The Resident Patient is Story IX in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, in which a doctor's mysterious backer is found hanged, exposing a gang of thieves from the old Worthingdon bank robbery.

Silver Blaze is Story I in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, in which Holmes finds the missing racehorse and clears the trainer's murder by identifying 'the curious incident of the dog in the night-time'.
The Stockbroker's Clerk is Story IV in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, in which a young clerk is lured away from a legitimate position by a sham firm so an impersonator can take his place.
The Yellow Face is Story III in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, in which Holmes draws the wrong conclusion about a cottage neighbour and a mysterious yellow-masked figure, a rare acknowledged error.

Mr. Justice Raffles, E.W. Hornung's fourth Raffles novel in which A.J. Raffles takes it upon himself to punish the predatory moneylender Dan Levy for exploiting vulnerable victims including one whom Bunny Manders knows.

Part I, Chapter I of The Valley of Fear, in which Holmes and Watson receive a coded warning from Fred Porlock about Moriarty's network, followed by Inspector MacDonald's arrival with news of the Birlstone murder.
Part I, Chapter II of The Valley of Fear, in which Holmes and Watson discuss the Moriarty network with Inspector MacDonald, and Colonel Sebastian Moran is identified as Moriarty's chief of staff.
Part I, Chapter III of The Valley of Fear presents the crime scene investigation at Birlstone Manor, where Holmes, Watson, and Inspector MacDonald examine the body identified as John Douglas.
Part I, Chapter IV of The Valley of Fear, in which Inspector MacDonald, Watson, and Sergeant Wilson examine the murder scene at Birlstone Manor with Ames the butler and Cecil Barker.
Part I, Chapter V of The Valley of Fear, in which Holmes questions Mrs. Ivy Douglas, Cecil Barker, and Mrs. Allen the housekeeper as the investigation of the Birlstone murder deepens.

Part I, Chapter VI of The Valley of Fear, in which Holmes, Watson, and Cecil Barker confront Mrs. Ivy Douglas with new evidence as White Mason and the Sussex Constabulary look on, pushing the investigation toward its solution.
Part I, Chapter VII of The Valley of Fear, in which Holmes, Watson, and Inspector MacDonald question Ames the butler and Cecil Barker as the Birlstone murder investigation reaches its climax.

Part II, Chapter I of The Valley of Fear, the opening chapter of the Vermissa flashback in which John Douglas arrives in the Pennsylvania coalfields as Jack McMurdo and encounters Boss McGinty, Jacob Shafter, and Mike Scanlan.

Part II, Chapter II of The Valley of Fear, set in Vermissa Valley, tracing John Douglas's earlier life as Jack McMurdo among the Scowrers, involving Boss McGinty, Jacob Shafter, Ettie Shafter, and Jonas Pinto.
Part II, Chapter III of The Valley of Fear depicts Jack McMurdo's deepening involvement with Boss McGinty and the Scowrers' Lodge 341, attending meetings and witnessing their violent methods.

Part II, Chapter IV of The Valley of Fear, set in Vermissa Valley, in which Captain Marvin of the police, Boss McGinty, and Mike Scanlan feature in the Scowrers' escalating violence against the mine operators.
Part II, Chapter V of The Valley of Fear shows McMurdo participating in Scowrers operations, including confrontations with Chester Wilcox and reveals of the lodge's wider criminal network.

Part II, Chapter VI of The Valley of Fear, the chapter in Vermissa Valley in which Brother Morris delivers information to Boss McGinty about the infiltration of the Scowrers, with John Douglas as McMurdo navigating the increasing danger.
Part II, Chapter VII of The Valley of Fear depicts the confrontation at the lodge in which McMurdo reveals himself as Birdy Edwards, exposing Boss McGinty and bringing the Scowrers to justice.
A Jubilee Present, a story in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles and Bunny plan a theft from the British Museum as a bold Jubilee-season enterprise.
An Old Flame is a story in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman involving Raffles and Bunny in an escapade centred on a bath-chair and a past acquaintance of Raffles.

No Sinecure, a story in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles and Bunny become entangled with the character Mr. Maturin in an operation that proves far more complicated than anticipated.

The Fate of Faustina, a story in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles and Bunny travel to Italy and rescue the young woman Faustina from the schemes of Count Corbucci.

The Knees of the Gods is the final story of Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles and Bunny join the Imperial Yeomanry and sail for South Africa, ending with Raffles's death at Spion Kop.
The Last Laugh is a story in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman set in Bloomsbury Square, one of the London escapades before Raffles enlists for the Boer War.
The Wrong House is a story in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles and Bunny burgle the wrong premises by mistake, leading to complications they must resolve.

To Catch a Thief, a story in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman in which Inspector Mackenzie pursues Raffles and Bunny, with Lord Ernest Belville involved in the circumstances that bring hunter and hunted together.

The Adventure of the Abbey Grange, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes investigates the apparent murder of the abusive Sir Eustace Brackenstall, with Captain Crocker and Lady Brackenstall as key figures.

The Adventure of Black Peter, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes investigates the harpooning of the brutal retired sea captain Peter Carey, eventually unmasking the harpooner Patrick Cairns.

The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes and Watson burgle the blackmailer Milverton's house, witness his murder by an unidentified noblewoman, and refuse to identify her to Lestrade.

The Adventure of the Dancing Men, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes deciphers coded messages in dancing-figure symbols to unmask the American criminal Abe Slaney and solve Hilton Cubitt's murder.

The Adventure of the Empty House, the opening story of The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes reappears after three years, lures Colonel Moran into a trap, and is reunited with Watson and Inspector Lestrade.

The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes investigates the stabbing of Willoughby Smith, revealing that Professor Coram's nihilist wife Anna was responsible.

The Adventure of the Missing Three-Quarter, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes investigates the disappearance of Cambridge rugby player Godfrey Staunton, finding him nursing his secretly married dying wife.
The Adventure of the Norwood Builder, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes proves the builder Jonas Oldacre faked his own death to frame the young solicitor John Hector McFarlane.

The Adventure of the Priory School, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes investigates the disappearance of a pupil from Dr. Huxtable's school, implicating the Duke of Holdernesse's illegitimate son James Wilder.

The Adventure of the Second Stain, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes recovers a stolen state document for Lord Bellinger, exposing Lady Hilda Trelawney Hope's complicity with Eduardo Lucas.

The Adventure of the Six Napoleons, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes traces the smashing of six plaster Napoleon busts by the Italian criminal Beppo, who had hidden the black pearl of the Borgias inside one of them.
The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes protects music teacher Violet Smith from the schemes of Bob Carruthers and the villainous Jack Woodley.

The Adventure of the Three Students, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes investigates the theft of an examination paper at a university, with the scholarship student Gilchrist as the culprit.

Chapter I: My Learned Brother, the opening chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which Dr. Jervis, emerging from the Temple, encounters Dr. Thorndyke and is drawn into the Hornby diamond theft case alongside Nathaniel Polton.
Chapter II: The Suspect is the chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which the thumb-print of Reuben Hornby is identified in blood in the empty diamond safe, making him the principal suspect.

Chapter III: A Lady in the Case, the chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which Miss Juliet Gibson visits Thorndyke to plead on behalf of the accused Reuben Hornby, providing personal testimony and emotional stakes for the investigation.

Chapter IV: Confidences is the chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which Thorndyke returns from their walk unusually thoughtful and silent, his face bearing concentration as he works through the fingerprint evidence.

Chapter IX: The Prisoner is the chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which Thorndyke visits Reuben Hornby in custody and tells him he has convinced himself of his innocence and has good hopes of convincing the world.

Chapter V: The Thumbograph, the chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which Thorndyke examines a red cloth thumbograph book containing inkpad thumb-prints, using it as a forensic control to analyse the disputed print from the crime scene.

Chapter VI: Committed for Trial is the chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which the magistrate commits Reuben Hornby for trial at the Central Criminal Court over the theft of diamonds worth £25,000–£30,000 from John Hornby's safe.

Chapter VII: Shoals and Quicksands is the chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which Thorndyke pursues the financial motive, noting that a mine slump occurred days before the robbery and that Walter Hornby held a large stake in the affected mines.

Chapter VIII: A Suspicious Accident is the chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which Jervis is nearly pushed under a vehicle by a man who lunges against him and trips him in the street, signalling that the investigation has become dangerous.

Chapter X: Polton is Mystified, the chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which Polton is puzzled by a precise technical drawing resembling a chess piece produced by Thorndyke as part of his scientific reconstruction of the forged thumb-print.

Chapter XI: The Ambush is the chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which a shot from a pneumatic walking-stick air-gun is fired in John Street, shattering a window as an attempt to stop Thorndyke's investigation.

Chapter XII: It Might Have Been is the chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which Anstey visits Thorndyke's chambers and Thorndyke declares the prosecution will receive a great surprise, promising to secure an acquittal.

Chapter XIII: Murder by Post is the chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which a poisoned cigar containing a chalky alkaloid — a 'Trichy' in Thorndyke's slang — is sent through the post as an assassination attempt.

Chapter XIV: A Startling Discovery, the chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which Jervis notices that every small 'e' in Mrs. Hornby's typed evidence paper has a characteristic break, revealing Walter Hornby as the typist and implicating him in the forgery.

Chapter XV: The Finger-Print Experts, the chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which Thorndyke cross-examines prosecution fingerprint experts in court, demonstrating that the probability of two individuals sharing an identical single-finger print is negligible.

Chapter XVI: Thorndyke Plays His Card, the chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which Thorndyke presents his expert evidence in court and states definitively that the thumb-print on the stolen diamond safe was not made by the prisoner Reuben Hornby.

Chapter XVII: At Last is the concluding chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which the jury returns a verdict of not guilty without retiring, the judge noting that Thorndyke has probably averted a serious miscarriage of justice.

A Costume Piece, the second Raffles story in The Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles and Bunny break into the house of the diamond merchant Rosenthall while in disguise, Bunny's first active participation in burglary.

Gentlemen and Players, a story in The Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles steals a lady's necklace at a country-house cricket match, exploiting his status as a distinguished cricketer to move undetected among the guests.
Le Premier Pas is a story in The Amateur Cracksman in which W. F. Raffles's jemmy features prominently, the story exploring the early stages of Raffles's criminal career.
Nine Points of the Law, a story in The Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles and Bunny steal a disputed Velasquez painting on behalf of a client, navigating the legal ambiguity of contested ownership.

The Gift of the Emperor, a story in The Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles steals an enormous pearl given by the Kaiser and hides it inside a cartridge during a cruise, outwitting passengers and crew alike.

The Ides of March, the opening story of The Amateur Cracksman in which the debt-ridden Bunny Manders discovers that his old school friend Raffles is a gentleman-burglar, and Raffles first recruits him as a confederate.

The Return Match, a story in The Amateur Cracksman in which the escaped convict Crawshay returns to seek Raffles's help, creating a tense situation in which Raffles must decide whether to assist a fellow criminal.
Wilful Murder, a story in The Amateur Cracksman in which the blackmailer Angus Baird is found murdered and Raffles and Bunny must navigate their own potential complicity in the affair.

The Blue Cross, the opening story of The Innocence of Father Brown in which the master-thief Flambeau attempts to steal Father Brown's precious blue cross reliquary, but the priest outwits him and leads the detective Valentin to his capture.
The Eye of Apollo, a story in The Innocence of Father Brown in which Father Brown and Flambeau expose the sun-worshipping prophet Kalon as responsible for the death of his follower Joan Stacey.
The Flying Stars is a story in The Innocence of Father Brown in which Flambeau disguises himself as a harlequin to steal the Flying Stars diamonds during a family Christmas entertainment.

The Hammer of God is a story in The Innocence of Father Brown in which Father Brown investigates a man found crushed by a hammer blow and reveals the unexpected culprit and the moral blindness that drove the crime.

The Honour of Israel Gow, a story in The Innocence of Father Brown in which Father Brown and Flambeau investigate strange occurrences at a Scottish castle following the death of Lord Glengyle, solved through Father Brown's lateral thinking.

The Invisible Man, a story in The Innocence of Father Brown in which Father Brown reveals that James Welkin, a postman, committed the murder of the inventor Isidore Smythe by being socially invisible.
The Queer Feet, a story in The Innocence of Father Brown in which Father Brown deduces that Flambeau has stolen the silver of the exclusive Twelve True Fishermen club at a London hotel.
The Secret Garden is a story in The Innocence of Father Brown in which Father Brown and Aristide Valentin investigate a decapitation murder in a locked Parisian garden during a dinner party.
The Sign of the Broken Sword, a story in The Innocence of Father Brown in which Father Brown and Flambeau uncover the dark secret behind a celebrated general's battlefield monument.

The Sins of Prince Saradine, a story in The Innocence of Father Brown in which Father Brown and Flambeau visit an island estate where Prince Saradine's violent past catches up with him through the vengeance of Antonelli and Marco.
The Three Tools of Death, a story in The Innocence of Father Brown in which Father Brown and Inspector Gilder investigate the death of a man apparently killed by three different means.

The Wrong Shape, a story in The Innocence of Father Brown in which Father Brown and Flambeau investigate the suspicious death of the poet Leonard Quinton, whose Indian hermit guest and the shape of a letter prove decisive.
The Actor and the Alibi is a story in The Secret of Father Brown in which Father Brown exposes a murderer among a theatrical company whose alibi collapses under his scrutiny.
The Chief Mourner of Marne is a story in The Secret of Father Brown in which Father Brown uncovers the true identity of a reclusive penitent mourned by Hugo Romaine and General Outram.
The Man with Two Beards is a story in The Secret of Father Brown in which Father Brown unravels a murder mystery at Beechwood House involving a disguise and a deceptive identity.
The Mirror of the Magistrate is a story in The Secret of Father Brown in which Father Brown helps magistrate James Bagshaw solve a murder, exposing how appearances of guilt are misleading.

The Red Moon of Meru, a story in The Secret of Father Brown in which Father Brown investigates the theft of a sacred ruby from Lord Mounteagle's collection, exposing the truth behind the Master of the Mountain's claims.
The Secret of Father Brown is the frame story of the collection, in which Father Brown explains his method to Carlotta Brownson by confessing he solves crimes by imagining himself as the murderer.
The Secret of Flambeau, the concluding story of The Secret of Father Brown, in which Father Brown explains how Flambeau the master thief was reformed and became his friend and ally.
The Song of the Flying Fish is a story in The Secret of Father Brown involving an exotic crime tied to Colonel Varney and a mysterious Arab stranger, unravelled by Father Brown's insight.

The Vanishing of Vaudrey, a story in The Secret of Father Brown in which Father Brown investigates the mysterious disappearance of Vaudrey, with suspects including Dr. Abbott, Evan Smith, and John Dalmon.
The Worst Crime in the World is a story in The Secret of Father Brown in which Father Brown investigates a crime at a castle involving the Musgrave family and a question of inheritance.

The Absence of Mr Glass, a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which Father Brown exposes the rational explanation behind the seemingly sinister disappearance of Mr Glass from James Todhunter's lodgings.

The Duel of Dr Hirsch, a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown set in France in which Father Brown exposes the truth behind an apparent duel involving pacifist scientist Dr. Paul Hirsch and Colonel Dubosc.

The Fairy Tale of Father Brown is a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which Father Brown and Flambeau pursue a crime in a Central European setting involving a general and mysterious mining experts.

The God of the Gongs, a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which Father Brown and Flambeau investigate a murder connected to a boxing event organised by Lord Pooley, with the Italian fighter Malvoli implicated.

The Head of Caesar, a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which Father Brown and Flambeau investigate the theft of a rare coin from Colonel Carstairs's collection, involving his children Arthur and Christabel.
The Man in the Passage, a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which Father Brown resolves a backstage mystery involving Aurora Rome, Captain Cutler, and the actor Isidore Bruno.

The Mistake of the Machine, a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown set in America in which Father Brown and Flambeau expose the limits of a lie-detection device used by Greywood Usher against Ireton Todd's daughter's suitor.
The Paradise of Thieves, a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown set in Italy in which Father Brown solves a robbery involving the Harrogate family and the double-dealing Ezza/Montano.

The Perishing of the Pendragons is a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which Father Brown and Flambeau sail to a decaying estate and uncover the dark secret behind the Admiral Pendragon's family curse.

The Purple Wig, a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which journalist Francis Finn and Father Brown investigate the legend of the Duke of Exmoor's hereditary purple wig, which conceals a surprising truth.
The Salad of Colonel Cray, a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which Father Brown solves a strange series of food thefts involving Colonel Cray, Dr. Oliver Oman, and Audrey Watson.

The Strange Crime of John Boulnois, a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which American journalist Calhoun Kidd and Father Brown investigate the apparent shooting of the celebrity James Dalroy at the home of quiet scholar John Boulnois.
Chapter 1 of The Hound of the Baskervilles, which introduces Dr. Mortimer's visit to Baker Street with the manuscript account of the ancient Baskerville curse and the death of Sir Charles.

Chapter 10: Extract from the Diary of Dr. Watson, the chapter of The Hound of the Baskervilles in which Watson's private diary entries describe his encounters with Mr. Frankland, Mrs. Laura Lyons, and the mysterious figure on the moor.

Chapter 11: The Man on the Tor, the chapter of The Hound of the Baskervilles in which Watson tracks down the mysterious figure on the moor and discovers it is Sherlock Holmes, who has been secretly investigating from a stone hut.
Chapter 12: Death on the Moor, the chapter of The Hound of the Baskervilles in which Holmes and Watson discover Selden the escaped convict dead on the moor, complicating the hunt for Stapleton.

Chapter 13: Fixing the Nets, the chapter of The Hound of the Baskervilles in which Sherlock Holmes rejoins Watson on Dartmoor and closes the trap on Stapleton, coordinating with Beryl Stapleton, Inspector Lestrade, and Mrs. Laura Lyons.

Chapter 14: The Hound of the Baskervilles, the climactic chapter in which Holmes, Watson, and Lestrade stake out the moor, witness the giant phosphorescent hound pursue Sir Henry, and shoot it before it can kill him.

Chapter 15: A Retrospection of The Hound of the Baskervilles is Holmes's closing reconstruction of Stapleton's plot, explaining how the phosphorescent hound was used and what became of the principal players.
Chapter 2: The Curse of the Baskervilles, the chapter in which Dr. James Mortimer reads aloud the 1742 family manuscript recounting the legend of the spectral hound unleashed upon Hugo Baskerville.

Chapter 3: The Problem, the chapter of The Hound of the Baskervilles in which Holmes and Watson question Dr. Mortimer about the legend and circumstances of Sir Charles Baskerville's death, and Holmes accepts the case.

Chapter 4: Sir Henry Baskerville, the chapter in which the heir Sir Henry Baskerville arrives in London and meets Holmes and Watson, having already been warned off Dartmoor by an anonymous letter and found a boot stolen.

Chapter 5: Three Broken Threads, the chapter in which Holmes's three lines of inquiry all run cold, demonstrating the difficulty of the Baskerville investigation before Watson is dispatched to Dartmoor.
Chapter 6: Baskerville Hall, the chapter in which Watson and Sir Henry Baskerville arrive in Devonshire accompanied by Dr. Mortimer, meeting the gloomy hall and its servants including James Desmond.
Chapter 7: The Stapletons of Merripit House, the chapter in which Watson meets the naturalist Jack Stapleton and his supposed sister Beryl on the moor near Merripit House.

Chapter 8: First Report of Dr. Watson, Watson's letter to Holmes describing events at Baskerville Hall including the mysterious candle signal by John Barrymore and the presence of Beryl and Jack Stapleton.
Chapter 9: The Light upon the Moor, Watson's report to Holmes describing a candle signal from Baskerville Hall involving John Barrymore and the escaped convict Selden.
Chapter I: The Science of Deduction is the opening chapter of The Sign of the Four in which Holmes demonstrates his deductive method on Watson's watch before Mary Morstan arrives with her mystery.
Chapter II: The Statement of the Case is the chapter of The Sign of the Four in which Mary Morstan presents the facts of the Morstan pearls and her anonymous invitation.
Chapter III: In Quest of a Solution is the chapter of The Sign of the Four in which Holmes and Watson call on Thaddeus Sholto at Brixton and learn of the Agra treasure.
Chapter IV: The Story of the Bald-Headed Man is the chapter of The Sign of the Four in which Thaddeus Sholto recounts his father Major Sholto's death and confession about the Agra treasure.
Chapter IX: A Break in the Chain is the chapter of The Sign of the Four in which Holmes and Watson lose the trail of the Aurora and Toby the dog tracks through London streets.
Chapter V: The Tragedy of Pondicherry Lodge is the chapter of The Sign of the Four in which Bartholomew Sholto is found dead and the treasure gone, Holmes reading the crime scene.
Chapter VI: Sherlock Holmes Gives a Demonstration is the chapter of The Sign of the Four in which Holmes reads the crime-scene clues aloud to Watson, Athelney Jones, and Mrs. Bernstone.
Chapter VII: The Episode of the Barrel is the chapter of The Sign of the Four in which Holmes and Watson trace the steam launch Aurora through the barrel-maker Mordecai Smith.
Chapter VIII: The Baker Street Irregulars is the chapter of The Sign of the Four in which Wiggins leads the street-boy network to hunt for the Aurora along the Thames.
Chapter X: The End of the Islander is the chapter of The Sign of the Four in which the Thames chase ends with Tonga killed and Jonathan Small captured.
Chapter XI: The Great Agra Treasure is the chapter of The Sign of the Four in which the treasure chest is opened and found empty, the fortune lost to the Thames.
Chapter XII: The Strange Story of Jonathan Small is the closing chapter of The Sign of the Four in which Small recounts his history in India, the Agra treasure pact, and his years in the Andamans.

The Vanishing Man Chapter I is the opening chapter of The Eye of Osiris by R. Austin Freeman in which Dr. Berkeley is accosted in Museum Street and first becomes involved in the Bellingham disappearance case.
Chapter II of The Vanishing Man ("The Eavesdropper"), in which narrator Berkeley overhears a conversation that begins to illuminate the mystery surrounding the disappearance of John Bellingham.
Chapter III of The Vanishing Man ("John Thorndyke"), in which Dr. Thorndyke is formally introduced and begins examining the evidence in the Bellingham disappearance case.

The Vanishing Man Chapter IV: The Will is the chapter of The Eye of Osiris in which John Bellingham's will stipulates that if his body is discovered and deposited in a vault above ground, the estate is to pass to his specified heirs.
Chapter IX of The Vanishing Man ("The Sphinx of Lincoln's Inn"), in which Thorndyke operates from his chambers at the Inner Temple and examines legal and forensic aspects of the case.
Chapter V of The Vanishing Man ("The Watercress-Bed"), a chapter advancing the investigation into John Bellingham's fate through examination of a waterway location significant to the case.
Chapter VI of The Vanishing Man ("Sidelights"), in which Thorndyke and Berkeley gather additional background information and peripheral evidence bearing on the Bellingham mystery.
Chapter VII of The Vanishing Man ("John Bellingham's Will"), in which the legal complications surrounding Bellingham's estate and his contentious will become central to the investigation.
Chapter VIII of The Vanishing Man ("A Museum Idyll"), set partly at the British Museum where the Egyptian collection provides atmospheric and evidential context for the Bellingham disappearance.
Chapter X of The Vanishing Man ("The New Alliance"), in which Berkeley and Thorndyke form a closer working partnership to pursue the Bellingham mystery through its most obscure ramifications.
Chapter XI of The Vanishing Man ("The Evidence Reviewed"), in which Thorndyke systematically re-examines all collected evidence in the Bellingham case, identifying key inconsistencies.
Chapter XII of The Vanishing Man ("A Voyage of Discovery"), in which the investigators follow a fresh lead requiring travel and physical examination of a new location connected to Bellingham's fate.
Chapter XIII of The Vanishing Man ("The Crowner's Quest"), covering the inquest proceedings into the discovered remains and the complex legal questions these raise for the Bellingham case.
Chapter XIV of The Vanishing Man ("The Probate Court"), in which the legal battle over Bellingham's estate comes to the fore, with Thorndyke advising on the forensic evidence presented in court.

The Vanishing Man Chapter XIX: Thorndyke's Analysis is the chapter of The Eye of Osiris in which Thorndyke presents his X-ray photographic analysis, astonishing the solicitor Jellicoe with the resources of modern science.
Chapter XV of The Vanishing Man ("Circumstantial Evidence"), in which the case against the accused appears strong based on indirect evidence, which Thorndyke undertakes to challenge scientifically.
Chapter XVI of The Vanishing Man ("O! Artemidorus, Farewell!"), a pivotal chapter in which the situation appears most dire for the innocent party before Thorndyke prepares his counter-attack.
Chapter XVII of The Vanishing Man ("The Accusing Finger"), in which Thorndyke reveals the true culprit through forensic evidence, dramatically vindicating the accused in the Bellingham case.
Chapter XVIII of The Vanishing Man ("John Bellingham"), the concluding chapter in which John Bellingham's fate and the true circumstances of his disappearance are finally fully explained.

The Vanishing Man Chapter XX: The End is the concluding chapter of The Eye of Osiris in which Thorndyke reflects that the lawyer's error arose from underestimating the scientific expert, with mere bones providing the decisive evidence.
Abdullah Khan is one of the four conspirators who murdered the merchant Achmet for the Agra treasure in The Sign of the Four, warning Jonathan Small that he must either join them or be silenced.

Abe Slaney is the American criminal from Chicago who sends the dancing-men cipher messages to his former fiancée Elsie Cubitt in The Adventure of the Dancing Men, ultimately shooting Hilton Cubitt and being trapped by Holmes.
Achmet is the merchant travelling through Rajputana who is murdered by Jonathan Small and his three Sikh confederates for custody of the Agra treasure in The Sign of the Four.
Admiral Grayson, father of Viola, a retired naval officer mentioned in the Father Brown story 'The Chief Mourner of Marne'.
Admiral Pendragon (Old Pendragon), a retired officer who never quite reached the rank of admiral, inhabiting an isolated property in the Father Brown story 'The Perishing of the Pendragons'.
Admiral Sinclair, a naval officer at whose house the Bruce-Partington submarine plans were examined, appearing in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans'.
Agatha is Milverton's housemaid whom Holmes courts under a cover identity in order to gather intelligence about Appledore Towers in The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton.
Alec Cunningham is the murderous son of old Cunningham who shoots William Kirwan to suppress the secret of their theft in The Adventure of the Reigate Squire.
Alexander Holder is the senior partner of the banking firm Holder & Stevenson who brings the beryl coronet to Baker Street for safekeeping in The Adventure of the Beryl Coronet.
Alfred is the butler at Appledore Towers, Charles Augustus Milverton's residence, in The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton.
Alfred Calverley, Fred's neurotic and dissolute cousin in Dr. Thorndyke's 'John Thorndyke's Cases', who conspired with Raggerton to drive the victim to suicide.
Alfred Hartridge is the murdered man at the centre of The Aluminium Dagger case in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Alice Armstrong, daughter of the murdered man, who came staggering into the garden in the Father Brown story 'The Three Tools of Death'.

Alice Charpentier is Madame Charpentier's daughter in A Study in Scarlet who was treated shamefully by Enoch Drebber at the boarding house, her mother's testimony helping Gregson build his initial case.
Alice de Mun is a character in The Hollow Needle who assists Isidore Beautrelet and is romantically linked to Arsène Lupin.

Aloysius Garcia is the man found murdered near Wisteria Lodge in Esher at the opening of The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge, his death connected to the former dictator Don Juan Murillo hiding in the area.
Altamont, the undercover alias adopted by Sherlock Holmes as an Irish-American agent, used during his intelligence work recounted in 'His Last Bow'.
Ames, the butler of Birlstone Manor in The Valley of Fear, a loyal servant who assists Holmes and Inspector MacDonald in examining the murder scene and identifying the victim.
Anatole Dufour, an alias of the murderer Victor Danègre, who fled to Buenos Aires after committing his crime in the Arsène Lupin story 'The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin'.
Andrews is a young Scowrer member of Merton County Lodge 249 in The Valley of Fear, described as little more than a boy, who participates in the violent lodge action in Part II, Chapter V.
Angus Baird, a man found dead on the floor in the Raffles story 'Wilful Murder' from 'The Amateur Cracksman'.

Anna is the Russian nihilist wife of the man hiding as Professor Coram who accidentally kills the professor's secretary Willoughby Smith when she stabs him while searching for incriminating documents in The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez.
Anstey is the barrister who assists Dr. Thorndyke in a legal capacity in one of the Thorndyke stories.

Anthony is Stapleton's aged manservant at Merripit House in The Hound of the Baskervilles who assisted in the crime of luring Sir Charles to the Yew Alley gate and later flees when Stapleton's scheme unravels.
Antoinette Bréhat, a young woman engaged as a companion by Clotilde Destange, whose disappearance figures in the Arsène Lupin story 'The Blonde Lady'.
Antonelli, a young man who confronts Prince Saradine by name in the Father Brown story 'The Sins of Prince Saradine'.
A mysterious figure draped in an outlandish sea-blue scarf wound about his head, appearing enigmatically in the Father Brown story 'The Song of the Flying Fish'.

Aristide Valentin is the head of the Paris police and most famous investigator in the world who pursues Flambeau across Europe in The Blue Cross, and is later exposed as the murderer in The Secret Garden from The Innocence of Father Brown.
M. Armand Armagnac, a French intellectual who specialized in resistance to militarism, appearing in the Father Brown story 'The Duel of Dr Hirsch'.
Arnold Becker, a criminal who operated under many aliases and was found headless in Father Brown's 'The Secret Garden'.
Arsène Lupin, the gentleman-burglar and master of disguise created by Maurice Leblanc, operates as both thief and unlikely hero across France, outwitting police from Ganimard to Holmlock Shears.

Arthur Cadogan West is the young Admiralty clerk found dead on the Underground in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans from His Last Bow, his death connected to the theft of the submarine plans by Hugo Oberstein and Colonel Valentine Walter.
Arthur Carstairs, an eccentric coin collector who seeks a stolen Roman coin, appearing in the Father Brown story 'The Head of Caesar'.

Arthur Charpentier is the Sub-Lieutenant of Her Majesty's Navy and son of Madame Charpentier's boarding house, arrested by Gregson as a suspect in the Drebber murder in Part I, Chapter IV of A Study in Scarlet.
A.J. Raffles, the amateur cracksman and celebrated cricketer created by E.W. Hornung, leads a double life as a gentleman thief with his partner Bunny Manders across London and abroad.
Ashton Jarvis, an actor in Mandeville's company known for playing villain roles, appearing in the Father Brown story 'The Actor and the Alibi'.

Athelney Jones, the Scotland Yard detective who arrives at Pondicherry Lodge after Bartholomew Sholto's murder in The Sign of the Four, initially confident in his own deductions before Holmes demonstrates the truth.
Aubrey Vernon, a second walking gentleman in Mandeville's theatrical company with a dark, elegant appearance, appearing in the Father Brown story 'The Actor and the Alibi'.
Audrey Watson, the ward and housekeeper of Major Putnam, appearing in the Father Brown story 'The Salad of Colonel Cray'.
Aurora Rome, an actress addressed by her dressers and visitors at her theatrical dressing room, appearing in the Father Brown story 'The Man in the Passage'.
Austin Gilett is a character appearing in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Bannister, a servant who inadvertently left a key in the door, a pivotal figure in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Three Students'.
Mr. Barker is Holmes's professional rival on the Surrey shore, described as Holmes's hated rival in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman, independently investigating the Josiah Amberley case.
Barnard, the lanky, side-whiskered secretary at Beechwood House who is conspicuous among the suspects in the Father Brown story 'The Man with Two Beards'.
Barney Maguire, the man who set the trap for Raffles in the story 'A Trap to Catch a Cracksman' from 'A Thief in the Night'.
Barney Stockdale, the husband of a woman involved in the scheme against Holmes in 'The Adventure of the Three Gables' from 'The Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes'.
Baron Anfredi, a young, grave, pale nobleman who is in reality Arsène Lupin in disguise, appearing in 'The Hollow Needle'.

Baron Cahorn is the owner of the Château de Malaquis who engages Arsène Lupin to protect his castle from a threatened robbery, unaware that Lupin himself is the thief, in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.

Baron Adelbert Gruner is the Austrian murderer and collector in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client from The Case-Book, whose engagement to Violet de Merville Holmes tries to prevent, ending with Kitty Winter throwing vitriol in his face.
Baron Von Herling, the chief secretary of the German legation who meets with Holmes in 'His Last Bow: The War Service of Sherlock Holmes'.

Bartholomew Sholto, the clever son of Major Sholto who discovered the Agra Treasure by precise measurement and was murdered in his locked laboratory at Pondicherry Lodge by Tonga's poisoned dart at Jonathan Small's direction.
Baudru Désiré, the disguise adopted by Arsène Lupin to engineer his escape from the Prison de la Santé, featured in 'The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin'.
Beppo, an Italian criminal who smashed Napoleon busts to recover a hidden pearl, central to the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Six Napoleons'.

Beryl Stapleton, who was in fact Jack Stapleton's wife rather than his sister, held prisoner and manipulated by him while secretly warning Sir Henry Baskerville of danger, and later freed after Stapleton's exposure.
Elizabeth (Betty) Fane, a young female relative of Captain Musgrave who assists Father Brown in 'The Worst Crime in the World'.
Billy is Holmes's page boy in The Valley of Fear, who brings the Porlock cipher letter to Holmes at the opening of Part I, Chapter I.
Billy, the page boy at 221B Baker Street, who showed Count Negretto Sylvius in during 'The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone'.

Blessington, whose real name is Sutton, is the mysterious resident patient at Dr. Percy Trevelyan's Brook Street practice who is hunted down by former confederates from the Worthington Bank robbery in The Adventure of the Resident Patient.
Bob Carruthers, a man who pursued and loved Violet Smith and figures prominently in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist'.
The village doctor in 'The Hammer of God', a tall, black-bearded man called to the scene of the crime in this Father Brown story.
The inspector, a sensible-looking man with a sandy moustache, who investigates the death in the Father Brown story 'The Hammer of God'.
Boscovitch is the anarchist agent carrying the stolen Bruce-Partington submarine plans, referenced in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans.
The plain, elderly man-servant who opens the door at Boulnois's home and conveys his employer's apologies, appearing in the Father Brown story 'The Strange Crime of John Boulnois'.

Brenda Tregennis is the sister of Mortimer, George, and Owen Tregennis who is found dead at the family home after exposure to the Devil's Foot poison, her death the act that drives Dr. Leon Sterndale to revenge in The Adventure of the Devil's Foot.

Brigham Young, the Mormon leader in A Study in Scarlet who rescued the wandering Ferrier party and later demands John Ferrier give his daughter Lucy to one of the elders' sons, setting the tragic events in motion.

Brother Morris, a member of Lodge 341 of the Scowrers in Vermissa Valley in The Valley of Fear, who brings news of a Pinkerton detective to Boss McGinty's attention and later appears at the trial of the Scowrers.
Buller, a cigar merchant from next door who is encountered among the suspects in the Father Brown story 'The Mirror of the Magistrate'.
Calhoun Kidd, a very young but world-weary American journalist who investigates the Boulnois affair in the Father Brown story 'The Strange Crime of John Boulnois'.
Captain Barillon, a famous gentleman-apache of an earlier era who died in a madhouse in terror, mentioned in the Father Brown story 'The Flying Stars'.
Captain Cutler, a celebrated military hero of the siege of Hong Kong, who becomes a suspect in the Father Brown story 'The Man in the Passage'.
