Captain Jack Crocker is the ship's captain in The Adventure of the Abbey Grange who kills the brutal Sir Eustace Brackenstall to protect Lady Brackenstall, to whom he returns Holmes's verdict of 'Not guilty' after Holmes conducts his own private trial.
Captain James Musgrave, a tall and well-dressed young man who appears swiftly and self-assuredly in the Father Brown story 'The Worst Crime in the World'.
Captain Keith (later Lieutenant-Colonel Keith), an officer in the regiment that made a rash assault at the Black River in the Father Brown story 'The Sign of the Broken Sword'.
Captain Marvin, the head of police in the Vermissa Valley who recognizes McMurdo from Chicago but is unable to prove his involvement with the Scowrers until Edwards reveals himself.

Captain Arthur Morstan, Mary Morstan's father and a former officer in an Indian regiment who shared the secret of the Agra Treasure with Major Sholto; he arrived in London to claim his share but died suddenly in Sholto's presence.

Captain Peter Carey, known as Black Peter, is the savage retired sealing master of the Sea Unicorn in The Adventure of Black Peter who is found pinned to the wall of his cabin with a harpoon by Patrick Cairns.
Captain Raggerton, the leading conspirator who, together with Alfred Calverley, schemed to drive a victim to suicide by ruin in Dr. Thorndyke's 'John Thorndyke's Cases'.
Captain Stephen Saradine, the prince's younger brother who appears under that name in the Father Brown story 'The Sins of Prince Saradine'.
Carlotta Brownson, a woman who gave a lecture on 'Thought-Forms' drawing on Father Brown's cases, mentioned in 'The Secret of Father Brown'.
Carrie Evans, also known as Mrs. Norlett, who served as Lady Beatrice Falder's maid under her maiden name and is implicated in 'The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place'.
Carter is the treasurer of Vermissa Valley Lodge 341 of the Scowrers in The Valley of Fear, present at the lodge meetings in Part II, Chapters III and VII.

Cartwright is the young Strand messenger-boy from the case mentioned by Holmes who is sent to Dartmoor to look after Watson's simple wants and serve as a scouting guard during the Baskerville investigation in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
Mr. Carver, a detective who came down to investigate and is lodging at Smith's Farm in the Father Brown story 'The Man with Two Beards'.

Cecil James Barker, of Hales Lodge, Hampstead, a close friend of John Douglas who was first to arrive after the murder at Birlstone Manor and gave evidence to Inspector MacDonald during The Valley of Fear investigation.
Charles Augustus Milverton, the king of blackmailers whom Holmes declares the worst man in London, who is shot dead by a veiled woman whose life he had ruined while Holmes and Watson witness.
Charles McCarthy, a man murdered beside the Boscombe Pool whose death Holmes investigates in 'The Boscombe Valley Mystery' from 'The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes'.

M. Charolais, a disguised Lupin confederate posing as a retired brewer in Arsène Lupin, visits the Gournay-Martin château under false pretences and assists in the theft of the motor-cars and other burglaries.
An elegant chauffeur who drives a very elegant car northward the next day in the Father Brown story 'The Worst Crime in the World'.
Chester Wilcox, a figure who has been 'settled' in the account of the Molly Maguires' activities in 'The Valley of Fear', Part II, Chapter V.
Chief Dudouis is the head of the Paris Sûreté who pursues Arsène Lupin across multiple stories in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin and The Blonde Lady.
Christabel Carstairs, daughter of the late Colonel Carstairs and his famous coin collection, who seeks help in the Father Brown story 'The Head of Caesar'.
Dr. Christopher Jervis, Thorndyke's assistant and narrator of his cases, accompanies him through investigations of murders, burglaries, and forensic puzzles in Freeman's detective series.

Clarisse Mergy, the anguished mother of the condemned Gilbert in The Crystal Stopper, joins Lupin's campaign to recover the crystal stopper from Daubrecq, tracking him from Paris to the Lovers' Tower.

Clotilde Destange is Lucien Destange's daughter, the Blonde Lady herself, who was Arsène Lupin's accomplice, her role exposed by Holmlock Shears when he visits her alone in the drawing-room in The Blonde Lady.
Colonel Adams, father of the young lady with the furry coat, who comes out himself to invite a guest inside in the Father Brown story 'The Flying Stars'.
Colonel Barclay is the commanding officer found dead in a locked room in The Adventure of the Crooked Man, whose past betrayal of Henry Wood provides the motive driving the plot.
Colonel Carstairs, a celebrated collector whose famous Carstairs Collection of Roman coins is at the heart of the Father Brown story 'The Head of Caesar'.
Colonel Clancy, a big Irish bull of a man who apparently died partly of rage during the disastrous battle episode in the Father Brown story 'The Sign of the Broken Sword'.
Colonel Cray of the Royal Artillery, an Anglo-Indian officer whose peculiar behaviour drives the Father Brown story 'The Salad of Colonel Cray'.
Colonel Crutchley, a physician who prescribed a rest cure to a character in the Raffles story 'The Rest Cure' from 'A Thief in the Night'.
Colonel Jules Dubosc, an artillery officer from Belfort who challenges Dr. Hirsch to a duel in the Father Brown story 'The Duel of Dr Hirsch'.
Colonel Emsworth, a stern and forbidding father who conceals a dark secret about his son in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier'.
Colonel Lysander Stark is the false colonel who lures hydraulic engineer Victor Hatherley to an isolated house to operate a coining press in The Adventure of the Engineer's Thumb.

Colonel Sebastian Moran is Professor Moriarty's chief of staff as identified in The Valley of Fear and the man who kills Ronald Adair with an air-gun in The Adventure of the Empty House, where Holmes traps him.
Colonel the Hon. Norman Bohun, the irreligious elder brother of the Reverend Wilfred Bohun, who is found dead in the Father Brown story 'The Hammer of God'.
Colonel Pound, a taciturn military club member who voices a dismissive opinion at the Twelve True Fishermen's club in the Father Brown story 'The Queer Feet'.
Colonel Ross, the owner of the racehorse Silver Blaze whose disappearance and the death of its trainer Holmes investigates in 'Silver Blaze'.

Colonel Sebastian Moran, Professor Moriarty's chief of staff and the world's second most dangerous man, a big-game hunter who uses an air-gun to attempt Holmes's murder after the professor's death at Reichenbach.
Colonel Valentine Walter is the traitor in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans from His Last Bow who steals the secret submarine plans and passes them to Hugo Oberstein.
Colonel Varney, a retired invalid officer inhabiting a small villa adjacent to the scene of events in the Father Brown story 'The Song of the Flying Fish'.
The Comet, a sensationalist newspaper that ran 'The Nobleman Nobody Knows' as a front-page mystery, personified by journalist Cockspur in the Father Brown story 'The Chief Mourner of Marne'.
Comte de Gesvres is the owner of the Château d'Ambrumésy whose secretary Jean Daval is murdered during Arsène Lupin's visit in The Hollow Needle.
Constable Downing of the Underground, a police officer who assisted in the investigation of the Bruce-Partington Plans case in Sherlock Holmes's 'His Last Bow'.
Constable Walters, a police constable who kept watch in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge'.
Count Corbucci is the villain of The Fate of Faustina from Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman, the antagonist whose hold on Faustina Raffles and Bunny work to break.
Comte de Dreux-Soubise is the French nobleman from whose Paris home Arsène Lupin steals the Queen's Necklace in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
Count Yvon de Lara, a distinguished Oriental traveller and scholar with a French title and a rather Russian face, appearing in the Father Brown story 'The Song of the Flying Fish'.

Count Negretto Sylvius, of 136 Moorside Gardens NW, is the villain in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone from The Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes who steals the Mazarin Stone (Crown Diamond) and is trapped by Holmes.
Comtesse de Dreux-Soubise is the wife of the Comte de Dreux-Soubise and is implicated in the theft of the Queen's Necklace in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
Cousin Oliver, a relative who accompanied Audrey Watson to a musical service, mentioned incidentally in the Father Brown story 'The Salad of Colonel Cray'.
Cowper, a man who found employment at his trade in Cleveland after learning of Lucy's fate, mentioned in the backstory of 'A Study in Scarlet'.

Crawshay is a convict who twice clashes with Raffles: first setting a trap for him in A Trap to Catch a Cracksman from A Thief in the Night, and earlier returning for a rematch in The Return Match from The Amateur Cracksman.

Culverton Smith, a Sumatran planter and amateur bacteriologist in The Adventure of the Dying Detective who murdered his nephew Victor Savage with a poison-spring ivory box and was lured by Holmes's feigned illness into confessing before Inspector Morton.
Cyril Morton, an electrical engineer who is engaged to Violet Smith and whose relationship is resolved at the end of the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist'.
Cyril Overton, captain of the Cambridge University rugby team, who comes to Holmes seeking the missing player Godfrey Staunton in 'The Adventure of the Missing Three-Quarter'.
Baron d'Imblevalle is the Belgian nobleman living in Paris from whose household Arsène Lupin steals the Jewish lamp in The Blonde Lady.
Dan Levy is the merciless moneylender who is the villain of Mr. Justice Raffles, the novel in which Raffles takes the law into his own hands to punish Levy's extortion of his victims.
Daniel Devine, a dark and exquisitely dressed friend of Simon Bankes with a somewhat foreign-looking beard, appearing in the Father Brown story 'The Man with Two Beards'.

Alexis Daubrecq, the villainous deputy and blackmailer in The Crystal Stopper, holds power over twenty-seven corrupt politicians through a list hidden in a crystal stopper and uses it to resist Lupin's every assault.
Daulat Ras, an Indian student at St. Luke's College who is one of three scholarship candidates in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Three Students'.
De Quincey, the English essayist whose account of the murderer Mr. Williams is vaguely recalled by a character in the Father Brown story 'The Mirror of the Magistrate'.
Doctor Harel is a doctor character appearing in the mystery corpus.
Dolores, the devoted Peruvian maid of Mrs. Ferguson, appearing in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire'.

Don Juan Murillo (The Tiger of San Pedro), the tyrannical former ruler of San Pedro hiding in England as the Marquess of Montalva in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge, whose past atrocities drive Aloysius Garcia's conspiracy against him.
Dorak, a Bohemian intermediary operating in the Commercial Road, connected to the strange events in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Creeping Man'.
Dost Akbar, one of the Four, who promised to lead Achmet to a side postern and was involved in the murder recounted in 'The Sign of the Four'.
Douglas Maberley is the young man whose love affair with Isadora Klein leads to his death, the case investigated by Holmes in The Adventure of the Three Gables from the Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes.
Dr. Abbott, a broad-backed man who sits patiently fishing on a river bank and is a figure of quiet authority in the Father Brown story 'The Vanishing of Vaudrey'.
Dr. Burdock, a rising biologist with a resolute chin and German-style swept-back hair, one of the guests in the Father Brown story 'The Song of the Flying Fish'.
Dr. Burrows is a doctor character appearing in a Dr. Thorndyke story.
Dr. Cooper is a doctor character in the mystery corpus.

Dr. Grimesby Roylott of Stoke Moran is the violent stepfather and antagonist in The Adventure of the Speckled Band who uses a trained swamp adder to murder one stepdaughter and intends to silence Helen Stoner to retain control of the family income.
Dr. Hanshaw is a doctor character appearing in a Dr. Thorndyke story.
Dr. James Erskine Harris, a bull-necked, good-tempered physician with a small moustache who is consulted in the Father Brown story 'The Wrong Shape'.
Dr. Shlessinger, who also used the alias Dr. Horsom, a villain who abducted Lady Frances Carfax and is unmasked in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax'.
Dr. Huxtable, the principal of the Priory School, who comes to Holmes in distress over the disappearance of a pupil in 'The Adventure of the Priory School'.

Dr. Leon Sterndale is the great lion hunter and African explorer in The Adventure of the Devil's Foot who poisons Mortimer Tregennis with the Devil's Foot root in revenge for Tregennis having poisoned and killed Brenda, Sterndale's secret love.
Dr. Leslie Armstrong, the famous Cambridgeshire physician whose secretive behaviour puzzles Holmes in 'The Adventure of the Missing Three-Quarter'.
Dr. James Mortimer, the rural physician who brings the Baskerville manuscript to Holmes in The Hound of the Baskervilles, introduces the case and accompanies Sir Henry to Devonshire.
Dr Mull, the Duke of Exmoor's librarian, who accompanies a journalist investigating the Purple Wig legend in the Father Brown story 'The Purple Wig'.
Dr. Norbury is a doctor or expert character appearing in a Holmes story.
Dr Oliver Oman, a scientific man who is also a devout churchgoer and escorts Audrey Watson to a musical service in the Father Brown story 'The Salad of Colonel Cray'.
Dr Orion Hood, a celebrated rationalist and literary scholar who is outwitted by Father Brown in 'The Absence of Mr Glass'.
Dr. Paul Berkeley M.B., the recently qualified physician who narrates the Dr. Thorndyke novel 'The Vanishing Man (The Eye of Osiris)', lodging at 31A Woburn Square.
Dr Paul Hirsch, a French scientist of pacifist conviction who refuses to fight Colonel Dubosc's duel in the Father Brown story 'The Duel of Dr Hirsch'.

Dr. Ray Ernest is the young unmarried physician who played chess with Josiah Amberley and became his wife's lover, murdered with her by Amberley in a gas chamber in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman.
Dr. Richards, a physician summoned to examine Sherlock Holmes after he fell ill during the events of 'The Adventure of the Dying Detective'.
Dr. Rowe is a doctor character in the mystery corpus.
Dr. Simon, a typical French scientist with glasses and a pointed brown beard who discovers the headless body in the Father Brown story 'The Secret Garden'.

Dr. John Evelyn Thorndyke, the medico-legal expert created by R. Austin Freeman, applies scientific and forensic methods to solve crimes including murders, diamond thefts, and mysterious disappearances.
Dr. John H. Watson, army surgeon turned chronicler, is Sherlock Holmes's loyal companion and narrator of his cases, providing both practical assistance and the reader's point of view.
Dr. Wood, a brisk and capable general practitioner who is consulted during the investigation in 'The Valley of Fear', Part I, Chapter III.
Draper is a character in a mystery story in the corpus.
Draper (local) is a local draper character in a mystery story in the corpus.
Duc de Valognes, a French nobleman who quietly offers to act as second in the duel in the Father Brown story 'The Duel of Dr Hirsch'.
The Duchess of Mont St. Michel, a black-eyed and opulent guest at Valentin's garden party in the Father Brown story 'The Secret Garden'.
The Duke of Charmerace is Arsène Lupin's aristocratic disguise in the Arsène Lupin novel, allowing him to operate inside the Gournay-Martin household while evading detective Guerchard's suspicions.
The Duke of Chester, the pleasant, flat-haired, freckled young vice-president of the Twelve True Fishermen's club in the Father Brown story 'The Queer Feet'.
The Duke of Exmoor, later revealed by Father Brown to be Isaac Green, the lawyer, in the Father Brown story 'The Purple Wig'.
The Duke of Holdernesse, the greatest and most powerful peer in England, who secretly employs Holmes to find his missing son in 'The Adventure of the Priory School'.
C. Auguste Dupin, Edgar Allan Poe's fictional detective mentioned by Holmes as a predecessor, though Holmes considers his own methods more rigorous than Dupin's imaginative reasoning.

The Earl of Thornaby is the aristocratic president of the Criminologists' Club in Raffles's story of the same name from A Thief in the Night, unwittingly hosting and being robbed by the very thief the club studies.
Edgar Allan Poe, the American author cited as the originator of detective fiction's ratiocinating method, as discussed in the Father Brown story 'The Secret of Father Brown'.
Edith Curtis is the young woman wrongly suspected and arrested by Inspector Badger in The Red Thumb Mark who is ultimately proven innocent through Dr. Thorndyke's investigation.
Edith Grant is a character in the mystery corpus.
Edith Presbury, Professor Presbury's daughter, who is engaged to his secretary Bennett and witnesses her father's alarming behavioural changes in 'The Adventure of the Creeping Man'.

Eduardo Lucas, also known as Henri Fournaye, is the society figure murdered in his London lodgings by his Creole wife in The Adventure of the Second Stain, his death entangling Holmes in the missing diplomatic letter case.
Edward I of England, a king cited as governing England well, mentioned in passing in the Father Brown story 'The Worst Crime in the World'.
Edward II of England, cited as an imbecile king who fawned upon Gaveston and whose reign brought infamy, mentioned in the Father Brown story 'The Worst Crime in the World'.
Edward III of England, cited as a king who covered England with glory, mentioned in the Father Brown story 'The Worst Crime in the World'.
Edward Nutt, the industrious editor of the Daily Reformer who receives Francis Finn's reports on the Purple Wig affair in the Father Brown story 'The Purple Wig'.
Edward Stopford is the client in The Moabite Cipher from John Thorndyke's Cases who brings Dr. Thorndyke a cryptography problem.
Effie Munro is Grant Munro's wife who harbours a secret about her mixed-race daughter from her first American marriage in The Adventure of the Yellow Face.
An Egyptian official who visited England some years after a quarrel with Vaudrey and was violently attacked by him, a figure in the backstory of the Father Brown story 'The Vanishing of Vaudrey'.

Elder Drebber is the Mormon patriarch whose son Enoch Drebber, along with Stangerson, claims the hand of Lucy Ferrier, their demand backed by Mormon law providing the backstory of Jefferson Hope's revenge in A Study in Scarlet.

Elder Stangerson is the Mormon elder whose son Joseph Stangerson, along with Drebber, seeks to marry Lucy Ferrier; his son becomes Jefferson Hope's second victim in the Lauriston Gardens revenge plot in A Study in Scarlet.

Elsie Cubitt, née Patrick, is Hilton Cubitt's wife and a former member of a Chicago crime family in The Adventure of the Dancing Men, whose past entanglement with Abe Slaney draws her former fiancé to Norfolk and leads to murder.

Emilia Lucca is Gennaro Lucca's devoted wife in The Adventure of the Red Circle, whose mysterious messages to Holmes via newspaper advertisement reveal her husband's entrapment by the murderous Red Circle secret society.

Enoch J. Drebber, a vicious Mormon elder from Cleveland, Ohio whose corpse found at 3 Lauriston Gardens triggers the investigation in A Study in Scarlet, avenged by Jefferson Hope for wrongs done to John and Lucy Ferrier.
Espado (known as 'The Vulture'), a yellow-faced Spanish criminal and well-known figure on both sides of the Channel who appears in the Father Brown story 'The Sign of the Broken Sword'.
Ethel Harrogate, a radiant and sociable young English woman travelling in Italy whose family is targeted in the Father Brown story 'The Paradise of Thieves'.
Etta Todd, the daughter of a New York millionaire, who figures in the mistaken-identity plot of the Father Brown story 'The Mistake of the Machine'.
Eugenia Ronder, the veiled lodger, a once-famous circus performer disfigured by a lion, who confesses her role in her husband's death in 'The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger'.
Evan Smith, a tall, fair-haired young secretary working for Vaudrey who appears harassed by events in the Father Brown story 'The Vanishing of Vaudrey'.
Evans Pott is a sly, little grey-haired rat of a man serving as county delegate of the Scowrers in The Valley of Fear, Part II, Chapter V, participating in lodge business at Vermissa Valley.
Ezza, who calls himself Montano and styles himself 'The King of Thieves', a flamboyant criminal in the Father Brown story 'The Paradise of Thieves'.
Father Brown, the unassuming Catholic priest and amateur detective created by G.K. Chesterton, solves murders and moral mysteries through psychological insight rather than physical evidence.
Father Gelis is a priest character in a Father Brown story.
Faustina, a woman whose fate hinges on the actions of Raffles and Bunny in the story 'The Fate of Faustina' from 'Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman'.
Felix Davey, an alias under which Arsène Lupin occupied a flat on the Avenue Bougeaud and was arrested by Ganimard in 'The Blonde Lady'.

Fitzroy McPherson is the science master at Harold Stackhurst's coaching establishment near Holmes's Sussex villa who dies in agony on the beach, the victim of a lion's mane jellyfish in The Adventure of the Lion's Mane.

Flambeau, the giant Gascon criminal turned private detective, begins as Father Brown's adversary in Chesterton's stories and becomes his trusted friend, accompanying him on numerous subsequent investigations.
Florian, a famous French acrobat and comic actor recognised by Flambeau, who performs as one of the flying stars in the Father Brown story 'The Flying Stars'.
M. Formery, the examining magistrate in Arsène Lupin, conducts the official inquiry into the Gournay-Martin burglary and is systematically misled and outmanoeuvred by both Lupin and Guerchard.
Francis Boyle, Mr. Smart's new Irish secretary who observes events with mild bewilderment in the Father Brown story 'The Song of the Flying Fish'.
Francis Finn, the journalist who files reports from the field to editor Nutt in the Father Brown story 'The Purple Wig'.
Francis Simmons is a character in the mystery corpus.
François Le Villard, a member of the French detective service who consulted Sherlock Holmes, mentioned in 'The Sign of the Four'.
Frank Harrogate, a curly-haired, sun-burnt, vigorous young man who is the son of the English banker in the Father Brown story 'The Paradise of Thieves'.
Mr. Frankland of Lafter Hall, an eccentric and litigious Devonshire neighbour of the Baskervilles who unwittingly helps Watson locate the mysterious figure on the moor with his telescope.
Fraser is a character in a Holmes story in the corpus.

Fred Calverley is a tall, frail, neurotic young man with wide pupils and taper fingers who came to Dr Thorndyke through solicitor Brodribb with a concern about spiritualism, appearing in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Fred Porlock, a secret informant within Professor Moriarty's organization who sends Holmes encoded warnings, including the cipher message that alerts Holmes to events at Birlstone Manor.
Futashima is a Japanese burglar who resides at 13 Birket Street, Limehouse, and works at Badcock's, featuring as a suspect in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Gaffer Charel is an old knife-grinder who travels all the fairs in the region and provided Isidore Beautrelet with a vital clue in The Hollow Needle (Arsène Lupin).

General de Merville is an old soldier whose daughter Violet becomes entrapped by the villainous Baron Gruner, prompting Holmes's involvement in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client from The Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes.
General Outram, a stolid Anglo-Indian with a bald head and black moustaches who is the English husband of an American woman at the gathering in the Father Brown story 'The Chief Mourner of Marne'.
General Schwartz, who rose from the ranks — originally the private sentry who fired his rifle — and later married Hedwig, in the Father Brown story 'The Fairy Tale of Father Brown'.
General Sir Arthur St. Clare, a celebrated military hero memorialised on a monument, whose secret disgrace is uncovered by Father Brown in 'The Sign of the Broken Sword'.

Gennaro Lucca, a Neapolitan immigrant in The Adventure of the Red Circle who fled the criminal society Red Circle after killing its leader Giuseppe Gorgiano, and whose wife Emilia sought Holmes's help while hiding in London.
A distinguished deputation of geologists and mineralogists from Paris and Berlin who were present at the royal court in the Father Brown story 'The Fairy Tale of Father Brown'.
George Tregennis, one of the brothers who went mad after an evening of cards, central to the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Devil's Foot'.
Georges Devanne is the banker who owns the Château de Thibermesnil, which is robbed by Arsène Lupin, and who later encounters Lupin in Dieppe in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
Germaine Gournay-Martin, the millionaire's daughter and fiancée of the Duke of Charmerace in Arsène Lupin, is a self-important heiress whose household is repeatedly victimised by Lupin's burglaries.
Gervaise Imbert is the wife in the Imbert household whose mansion Arsène Lupin infiltrates by ascending its exterior in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
Gibbs, the village cobbler who discovered the body of Colonel Bohun in the Father Brown story 'The Hammer of God'.

Gilbert, Clarisse Mergy's son and Lupin's associate, is arrested for the murder at Enghien and condemned to the guillotine, with Lupin's entire campaign to recover the crystal stopper aimed at saving his life.
Gilchrist, one of the three scholarship candidates at St. Luke's College who is discovered to have seen the examination paper in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Three Students'.
Giles Carstairs, brother of Christabel, who was quarrelled with by his father and sent to Australia on a small allowance in the Father Brown story 'The Head of Caesar'.
A dark-haired young woman in a canoe who drifts silently past a strange islet and peers intently toward it in the Father Brown story 'The Perishing of the Pendragons'.
Giuseppe Gorgiano, the murderous leader of the Red Circle criminal society in The Adventure of the Red Circle, killed by Gennaro Lucca in self-defence after pursuing the Luccas from Naples to London.
Godfrey Bellingham is John Bellingham's brother, described as a handsome man of about fifty with a frank and pleasing manner, who appears in The Vanishing Man (Dr Thorndyke).

Godfrey Emsworth is a Boer War veteran in The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier who conceals himself at home after contracting ichthyosis, mistaken by his family for leprosy, until Holmes and Sir James Saunders diagnose the true condition.
Godfrey Norton, a lawyer who is the husband of Irene Adler and whose relationship with her complicates Holmes's mission in 'A Scandal in Bohemia'.
Godfrey Staunton, the star three-quarter of the Cambridge rugby team, whose sudden disappearance before a match is investigated by Holmes in 'The Adventure of the Missing Three-Quarter'.

M. Gournay-Martin, the wealthy millionaire art-collector in Arsène Lupin, is the primary victim of Lupin's burglary and pendant-theft, and a baffled witness to the Lupin–Guerchard duel in his Paris house.

Grace Dunbar is the governess in The Problem of Thor Bridge who is framed for the murder of Maria Gibson, ultimately cleared by Holmes's discovery that Maria staged her own suicide on Thor Bridge to destroy the woman her husband loved.
Granby, a sharp but not unfriendly lawyer with greying hair who attends to the affairs of the Musgrave family in the Father Brown story 'The Worst Crime in the World'.

Grandison Chace is the American traveller from Boston in The Secret of Father Brown who visits Father Brown and questions him about his method of understanding criminals, prompting the priest to reveal his secret.
Grant Munro is a hop merchant of Norbury whose wife Effie conceals a secret concerning her mixed-race American daughter, and who consults Holmes as the client in The Adventure of the Yellow Face.
Green, a servant at the household whose slightly odd manner arouses suspicion in the Father Brown story 'The Mirror of the Magistrate'.
Greywood Usher, a cadaverous, careful-spoken ex-detective who serves as second-in-command under the Governor in the Father Brown story 'The Mistake of the Machine'.
Grimm, a very able German detective and old friend of Flambeau's who provides fragmentary information about events at the court in the Father Brown story 'The Fairy Tale of Father Brown'.
Guerchard, the renowned French detective, is Lupin's chief police adversary in the Arsène Lupin novel, eventually deducing that the Duke of Charmerace is Lupin himself but failing to hold him.
Guillaume Berlat, an alias under which Arsène Lupin was known to his circle of society acquaintances on the ship, appearing in 'The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin'.
Guillemard, the keeper of a church who figures in the Raffles story 'The Spoils of Sacrilege' from 'A Thief in the Night'.
The monstrous, streaming-bearded gardener at the Pendragon property, a striking presence who waves warnings from the green ridge in the Father Brown story 'The Perishing of the Pendragons'.

Hall Pycroft is a stockbroker's clerk who consults Sherlock Holmes after being lured away from legitimate employment by a fraudulent job offer, in The Adventure of the Stockbroker's Clerk from the Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.
Mr. Harmer, a guest known by that name at the Blue Dragon inn, appearing in the Father Brown story 'The Song of the Flying Fish'.
Harold Stackhurst, the owner of The Gables coaching establishment and Holmes's neighbour, who brings the case of Fitzroy McPherson's death to Holmes in 'The Adventure of the Lion's Mane'.
Harraway, the Lodge Secretary of the Vermissa Valley Scowrers who oversees initiation rites and organizes the criminal brotherhood's operations under Boss McGinty.
Harris, Mr. Smart's valet, who darkly remarks on the danger his employer faces in the Father Brown story 'The Song of the Flying Fish'.
Harry 'Bunny' Manders, Raffles's old school friend and reluctant accomplice, serves as narrator and sidekick through the burglaries, disguises, and adventures of the amateur cracksman.
Harry Burke, a man who began the free-money movement sincerely but degenerated into sponging off his half-starved sister, mentioned in the Father Brown story 'The Flying Stars'.
Mr. Harry Hartopp, a very rich but shy and silent young gentleman recently arrived from London in the Father Brown story 'The Song of the Flying Fish'.
Harry Murcher is the Holland Grove beat constable mentioned in A Study in Scarlet who chatted with John Rance the night Drebber's body was found at Lauriston Gardens.
Hatty Doran is the American heiress who mysteriously disappears from her own wedding breakfast to Lord Robert St. Simon, making her the central subject of The Adventure of the Noble Bachelor.
Charles Hearn is a gentleman found dead, almost a stranger to the locality, whose death forms the basis of a case investigated by Dr Thorndyke in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Hedwig, a court girl who was picking wild flowers in the wood and later married General Schwartz, appearing in the Father Brown story 'The Fairy Tale of Father Brown'.
Heinrich Arnhold, the timid and pacifist third Arnhold brother who lived as a hermit with two Hungarian servants in the Father Brown story 'The Fairy Tale of Father Brown'.
Helen Stoner, who lives with her menacing stepfather Dr. Grimesby Roylott and seeks Holmes's help after her twin sister's mysterious death in 'The Adventure of the Speckled Band'.
Henriette is the young woman who took the necklace in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin, a fact established through investigation.

Henry Wood, known as the Crooked Man, is a former sergeant in the Royal Mallows regiment who is reduced to a bent and broken physical wreck, and whose shared past with Colonel Barclay is the hidden driver of the mystery in The Adventure of the Crooked Man.

Herbert John Nash is a second fingerprint expert from Scotland Yard who concurs entirely with Singleton's findings but is shown by Dr Thorndyke to be wrong in every conclusion he draws, in The Red Thumb Mark.
Herlock Sholmès, the parodic name given to Holmes's English rival who arrived too late at Thibermesnil in the Arsène Lupin story 'The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin'.
Herr Bleichen is the Austrian consul who previously possessed the Blue Diamond before it disappeared, a fact relevant to the investigation in The Blonde Lady (Arsène Lupin).

Herr Heidegger is the German schoolmaster at the Priory School whose disappearance on the same night as Lord Saltire is a key clue Holmes traces in The Adventure of the Priory School, his body eventually found on the moor.
Hilton Cubitt of Ridling Thorpe Manor, Norfolk, who consults Holmes about mysterious dancing-men cipher messages in 'The Adventure of the Dancing Men'.
Hilton Soames, tutor and lecturer at St. Luke's College, who discovers that his scholarship examination paper has been seen by a student and calls in Holmes in 'The Adventure of the Three Students'.

Holmlock Shears, the fictional English detective in Leblanc's Lupin stories (a thinly veiled Sherlock Holmes), is summoned to Paris to recover stolen jewels and unmask Lupin, yet consistently outmanoeuvred.
Henry Peters, known as Holy Peters, is the missionary impostor in The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax who chloroforms Lady Frances and hides her in a coffin to steal her valuables.

Horace Velmont, an alias used by Arsène Lupin among society, who is noted by acquaintances to bear a striking resemblance to the notorious criminal himself in 'The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin'.
Mr. Hosmer Angel, in reality the stepfather James Windibank in disguise, who woos Mary Sutherland and then vanishes, in the Sherlock Holmes story 'A Case of Identity'.
The dark, motionless hotel proprietor with a glass eye at Seawood, whose stillness Flambeau notes while gazing out to sea in the Father Brown story 'The God of the Gongs'.

Hugo Baskerville is the wild seventeenth-century ancestor whose diabolical bargain with a spectral hound on Dartmoor gave rise to the Baskerville curse, described in the 1742 family manuscript in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

Hugo Oberstein, the German spy operating from Caulfield Gardens in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans who received stolen submarine blueprints from Colonel Valentine Walter and killed Arthur Cadogan West.
Hugo Romaine, acclaimed as the greatest actor of his day, whose golden curls and ivory mask conceal a shattering secret in the Father Brown story 'The Chief Mourner of Marne'.
The two Hungarian servants of Heinrich Arnhold, who wear dull-black gowns and glide out of their cave-like dwelling to support their master in the Father Brown story 'The Fairy Tale of Father Brown'.

Ian Murdoch is the mathematics coach at Harold Stackhurst's establishment and the chief suspect in Fitzroy McPherson's bizarre death in The Adventure of the Lion's Mane, later cleared when the true killer is identified as a lion's mane jellyfish.
Mr. Imlack Smith, a slim, sallow, dressy but quiet bank manager who is one of the guests in the Father Brown story 'The Song of the Flying Fish'.
An Indian hermit in white and yellow robes whom Leonard Quinton insisted on entertaining at his home for months, mentioned in the Father Brown story 'The Wrong Shape'.

Inspector Badger is a brisk Scotland Yard CID inspector with a short sandy moustache who confidently pursues an Italian red herring and wrongly arrests Edith Curtis in The Red Thumb Mark (Dr Thorndyke).

Inspector Baynes of the Surrey Constabulary independently and ably investigates the Garcia murder at Wisteria Lodge in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge, earning Holmes's genuine respect for the quality of his parallel deductions.
Inspector Craven, a lean, iron-grey-haired detective who accompanies Flambeau to Glengyle Castle in the Father Brown story 'The Honour of Israel Gow'.
Inspector Dieuzy is one of two inspectors (alongside Folenfant) who board a train to verify whether Arsène Lupin is among the passengers in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.

Inspector Folenfant is the inspector who carries out detective operations in multiple Arsène Lupin stories, including The Blonde Lady and The Extraordinary Adventures, working as a subordinate to Ganimard.
Inspector Ganimard, the tenacious French detective, pursues Arsène Lupin repeatedly across Paris and beyond, scoring occasional arrests but consistently outwitted by his quarry.
Inspector Gilder, a grizzled and capable senior detective who supervises the investigation at the Armstrong house in the Father Brown story 'The Three Tools of Death'.

Inspector Tobias Gregson, the tall, flaxen-haired Scotland Yard detective described by Holmes as the smartest of the Yarders, who first investigates the Lauriston Gardens murder in A Study in Scarlet and later appears in other cases.
Inspector Lestrade of Scotland Yard, the officially recognised but limited detective, repeatedly calls on Holmes for assistance across the canon from A Study in Scarlet through His Last Bow.
Inspector Alec MacDonald of Scotland Yard, the Scottish detective who leads the official investigation of the Birlstone murder in The Valley of Fear, consulting Holmes and Watson in Parts I and II.
Inspector Mackenzie, the Scotland Yard detective who pursues Raffles and Bunny across several adventures, eventually becoming a persistent nemesis to the gentleman burglar.
Inspector MacKinnon of Scotland Yard works the Josiah Amberley case in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman, guided by Holmes to the gas chamber where Amberley murdered his wife and Dr. Ernest.
Inspector Martin of the Norfolk Constabulary, who arrives at Ridling Thorpe Manor to investigate the shooting in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Dancing Men'.
Inspector Morton of Scotland Yard is hidden by Holmes in the sick room to witness Culverton Smith's confession in The Adventure of the Dying Detective and then emerges to arrest Smith.
Inspector Pinner, who arrived with several police officers and took rapid decisive action to resolve events in the Father Brown story 'The Song of the Flying Fish'.
Inspector Robinson, the Highgate police inspector who takes charge of the funds recovered from the dead man's secretary in the Father Brown story 'The Three Tools of Death'.

Irene Adler, the American opera singer who outwits Sherlock Holmes in A Scandal in Bohemia, becoming the only woman Holmes ever acknowledges as his intellectual equal, forever referred to by him as "the Woman".
Ireton Todd, nicknamed 'Last-Trick Todd', a good host who tracked a suspect to the venue still wearing fancy dress in the Father Brown story 'The Mistake of the Machine'.
Isa Whitney is the brother of the late Elias Whitney, D.D., who becomes an opium addict haunting the Bar of Gold opium den, and whom Watson rescues in The Adventure of the Man with the Twisted Lip.
Isaac Green, the lawyer whose true identity is concealed behind the persona of the Duke of Exmoor, revealed by Father Brown in 'The Purple Wig'.

Isaac Löwe is a great collector and dealer whose house in Howard Street, Piccadilly, functions as both a private museum and a place of business, and whose stolen collection is the subject of a case in John Thorndyke's Cases.

Isadora Klein is the richest and most lovely widow in England in The Adventure of the Three Gables from The Case-Book, who sends agents to steal Douglas Maberley's manuscript novel that exposes her past affair.
Isidore Beautrelet, a sixth-form lycée pupil with remarkable deductive gifts, pursues Arsène Lupin in The Hollow Needle, eventually deciphering the secret of the hollow cliff needle at Étretat.
Isidore Bruno, a celebrated American actor appearing opposite Aurora Rome in the theatre at the centre of the Father Brown story 'The Man in the Passage'.
Isidore Smythe, a small, dark man who runs a business of mechanical servants and lives in terror of threats from John Welkin in the Father Brown story 'The Invisible Man'.
Israel Gow, a gaunt red-haired labourer with dogged features and blank blue eyes, found in sole possession of Glengyle Castle after the earl's death in Father Brown's 'The Honour of Israel Gow'.
An Italian policeman who expresses mixed anger and admiration toward Father Brown after the resolution of the case in 'The Paradise of Thieves'.
An Italian waiter at the Twelve True Fishermen's club who was struck by a paralytic stroke that afternoon, creating the vacancy exploited by Flambeau in the Father Brown story 'The Queer Feet'.
Ivan, the old scarred confidential servant of Valentin, who helps reassure his master in the Father Brown story 'The Secret Garden'.

Mrs. Ivy Douglas, also known in her past as Ettie Shafter, is John Douglas's wife at Birlstone Manor and a key figure in the resolution of The Valley of Fear, ultimately departing for a long voyage after her husband's presumed death.
Jabez Wilson is a pawnbroker with a conspicuous head of flaming red hair who is deceived and exploited by the Red-Headed League in The Adventure of the Red-Headed League.

Jack Ferguson is the crippled elder son of Robert Ferguson who secretly administers curare from tipped arrows into his baby half-brother out of jealousy, the crime discovered and interpreted by Holmes in The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire.
Boss McGinty, the feared leader of Lodge 341 of the Scowrers in Vermissa Valley, dominates the Pennsylvania coal district through violence and corruption until brought to justice and hanged.

Jack Stapleton, the murderous naturalist of Dartmoor, uses a phosphorescent hound and the Baskerville curse to eliminate heirs to the Baskerville estate for his own inheritance in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

Jack Woodley is the rough associate of Bob Carruthers who coerces the defrocked clergyman Williamson into performing a forced marriage on Violet Smith, tried for abduction in The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist.
Jacob Shafter, the Vermissa Valley boarding-house keeper in The Valley of Fear whose daughter Ettie becomes romantically involved with undercover detective Birdy Edwards (McMurdo).
James Bagshaw, an old friend of Wilfred Underhill and a man prone to night-time street rambles, who is one of the witnesses in the Father Brown story 'The Mirror of the Magistrate'.
James Blount, Flambeau's brother-in-law newly arrived from Canada, a big and boisterous yellow-bearded young gentleman-farmer who visits for Christmas in the Father Brown story 'The Flying Stars'.
James Browner, a steward on a Liverpool steamship who commits a crime of passion and sends a severed-ear package in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Cardboard Box'.
James Dalroy, the son of a bankrupt Irish landlord and an attaché of some kind, a witty and unconventional figure in the Father Brown story 'The Strange Crime of John Boulnois'.
Mr. James Desmond, the elderly, amiable next heir to the Baskerville estate, mentioned in 'The Hound of the Baskervilles' as posing no threat to Sir Henry.
James M. Dodd, a big, fresh-faced, sunburned Briton who comes to Holmes worried about his old comrade Godfrey Emsworth in 'The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier'.
James Hardcastle, a correct and distinguished-looking promising politician who is among the guests at the gathering in the Father Brown story 'The Red Moon of Meru'.
James Mair, the real Marquis of Marne who died, a man of political ability who played second fiddle to his brilliant cousin Maurice in the Father Brown story 'The Chief Mourner of Marne'.
James McCarthy is the only son of the murdered Charles McCarthy and is arrested on suspicion of his father's killing, forming the central mystery of The Adventure of the Boscombe Valley Mystery.
James Ryder is the upper-attendant at the Hotel Cosmopolitan who steals the blue carbuncle and conceals it inside a goose in The Adventure of the Blue Carbuncle.
James Stanger is the editor of the Vermissa Herald who is beaten by the Scowrers under Boss McGinty's orders in Part II, Chapter III of The Valley of Fear for his editorials attacking the lodge.
James Todhunter, a man found bound and gagged with a scarf in his own room, who is the apparent victim in the Father Brown story 'The Absence of Mr Glass'.
James Welkin, a squinting man who haunts Laura Hope's neighbourhood and sends threatening letters to Isidore Smythe, central to the Father Brown story 'The Invisible Man'.

James Wilder is the Duke of Holdernesse's illegitimate secretary-son who orchestrates the abduction of his legitimate half-brother Lord Saltire from the Priory School, later fleeing abroad in The Adventure of the Priory School.
Jameson, Mr. Smart's head clerk, who comes up from the office to assist the new secretary Francis Boyle in the Father Brown story 'The Song of the Flying Fish'.
Jean Daspry is a delightful and heedless companion of the narrator who later dies on the frontier six months after the events of The Seven of Hearts, in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
Jean Daval is the secretary of the Comte de Gesvres who is stabbed in the ruins of the old abbey, with his murder initially attributed to Arsène Lupin in The Hollow Needle.

Jefferson Hope, the American frontiersman and avenging cabman, is revealed as the murderer of Enoch Drebber and Joseph Stangerson in A Study in Scarlet, driven by a decades-long vendetta over Lucy Ferrier.
Jephro Rucastle, the jovial and seemingly good-humoured owner of Copper Beeches who conceals his daughter and employs Violet Hunter under sinister pretences in 'The Adventure of the Copper Beeches'.
Arthur Jezzard, a witness at the inquest who testified to having viewed and identified the body of Charles Hearn in Dr. Thorndyke's 'John Thorndyke's Cases'.
Jim Carnaway, a man whose widow received a pension according to the Molly Maguires' accounts in 'The Valley of Fear', Part II, Chapter III.
Joan Stacey, the paler, greyer, and more insignificant younger sister of Pauline Stacey, appearing in the Father Brown story 'The Eye of Apollo'.
John Bankes, the burly and boisterous brother of the principal female character, indifferent to her spiritual interests, in the Father Brown story 'The Man with Two Beards'.

John Barrymore, the butler at Baskerville Hall who was the first to discover Sir Charles Baskerville's body and later secretly signals to the escaped convict Selden on the moor, revealed to be his wife's brother.
John Bellingham is a man of about sixty with a grey beard and wiry figure, an Egyptologist who has vanished without explanation, and the central missing person in The Vanishing Man (Dr Thorndyke).
John Boulnois, an unassuming Oxford academic who published a revolutionary theory of evolution in an obscure journal, at the centre of the Father Brown story 'The Strange Crime of John Boulnois'.
John Clay (alias Vincent Spaulding), a young but already formidable murderer, thief, and forger who masterminded the Red-Headed League scheme in 'The Adventure of the Red-Headed League'.
John Clayton, the cabman whose dark-bearded, sallow-faced fare gave the false name Sherlock Holmes and ordered him to follow Sir Henry Baskerville in 'The Hound of the Baskervilles'.
John Crook, a tall, angular journalist with bristling dark hair and an aggressive red tie who is courting Flambeau's ward in the Father Brown story 'The Flying Stars'.
John Dalmon, a neatly dressed and rather swarthy young man generally supposed to be engaged to Vaudrey's ward, appearing in the Father Brown story 'The Vanishing of Vaudrey'.

John Douglas, also known as Jack McMurdo and Birdy Edwards, is an American-born man at Birlstone Manor whose faked murder and undercover Pinkerton past form the dual-narrative mystery of The Valley of Fear.

John Ferrier, an American pioneer and adoptive father of Lucy, who was saved from death on the Great Alkali Plain by Mormon emigrants and later settled in Salt Lake City before being coerced by Brigham Young over Lucy's marriage.

John Garrideb (Killer Evans) is the American criminal whose real name is James Winter, alias Killer Evans, who constructs the fake Garrideb scheme in The Adventure of the Three Garridebs to clear the London flat where a counterfeiting press is hidden.
John Hector McFarlane, a young solicitor who arrives at Baker Street pursued by the police for the suspected murder of Jonas Oldacre in 'The Adventure of the Norwood Builder'.
John Hopley Neligan Jr., who was searching Peter Carey's papers for securities stolen from his late father in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of Black Peter'.
John Hornby is a gold and silver refiner and bullion dealer whose firm employs his nephew Reuben Hornby, the accused in The Red Thumb Mark (Dr Thorndyke).

John Mason is the trainer at Shoscombe Old Place who comes to Holmes in great distress about Sir Robert Norberton's bizarre behaviour in The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place, suspecting the master is in financial ruin.
John Openshaw is a young man who consults Holmes after receiving the Five Orange Pips, following the mysterious deaths of his uncle and father, in The Adventure of the Five Orange Pips.
John Rance, the constable who discovered Enoch Drebber's body at Lauriston Gardens in A Study in Scarlet and reported the discovery to Scotland Yard.
John Scott Eccles of Popham House, Lee, the solid English gentleman who spent a bewildering night at Wisteria Lodge and finds himself without an alibi in 'The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge'.
John Straker, the murdered trainer of the racehorse Silver Blaze, whose death and the horse's disappearance Holmes investigates in the Sherlock Holmes story 'Silver Blaze'.
John Turnbull Angus, a tall, red-haired young man who is courting Laura Hope and helps expose the mystery of the invisible man in the Father Brown story 'The Invisible Man'.
John Turner, an Australian-accented man who is the true murderer of Charles McCarthy, shielded by Holmes after Holmes learns the full backstory in 'The Boscombe Valley Mystery'.
John Warner, who was sent by Holmes to summon Culverton Smith to Baker Street in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Dying Detective'.
Jonas Oldacre, the builder of Deep Dene House in Sydenham, whose apparent murder and subsequent reappearance form the plot of 'The Adventure of the Norwood Builder'.
Jonas Pinto, a man shot in Chicago whose death is recounted as part of the violent history of the Scowrers in 'The Valley of Fear', Part II.

Jonathan Small, a one-legged English ex-soldier and one of the Four who murdered Achmet for the Agra Treasure, later escaped from the Andaman Islands with Tonga to pursue the treasure and killed Bartholomew Sholto.
Jose, the manservant of Don Juan Murillo who vanished from Wisteria Lodge ahead of the police raid in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge'.

Joseph Stangerson, the quiet private secretary to Enoch Drebber and fellow Mormon elder who was murdered at Halliday's Private Hotel near Euston Station by Jefferson Hope as the second victim in A Study in Scarlet.

Josiah Amberley, a retired art-color merchant who hires Holmes ostensibly to find his missing wife but is ultimately exposed as having murdered her and her lover in the Adventure of the Retired Colourman.
Josiah H. Dunn is the energetic and fearless New England mine manager at Crow Hill who resists the Scowrers' intimidation in Part II, Chapter V of The Valley of Fear.

Judge Jules Bouvier is the examining judge who conducts the formal examination of Arsène Lupin during his imprisonment, described by Lupin himself as a charming fellow, in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
Jules Vibart, described as another conspirator against Murillo, mentioned in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge'.
Juliet Gibson is the client who consults Dr Thorndyke in The Red Thumb Mark, and who later walks from the Old Bailey to Fountain Court with Jervis, where the two declare their love.
Julius K. Brayne, a huge, fat, white-haired American millionaire who is found headless in the garden in the Father Brown story 'The Secret Garden'.
Kalon, a self-styled prophet who has taken the flat above the Stacey sisters and whose sun-worship cult becomes entangled in a death in the Father Brown story 'The Eye of Apollo'.
Kalon's clerk, a respectable young man from Clapham with no connection to his employer, who provides an alibi in the Father Brown story 'The Eye of Apollo'.
The King of Bohemia (Wilhelm Gottsreich Sigismond von Ormstein), who approaches Holmes incognito as Count Von Kramm to recover a compromising photograph from Irene Adler in 'A Scandal in Bohemia'.
Kitty Winter, a woman ruined by Baron Gruner who throws vitriol in his face in an act of revenge in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Illustrious Client'.
La Rothière is one of three foreign spies — alongside Oberstein and Eduardo Lucas — involved in the theft of the Bruce-Partington Plans in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans.

Lady Beatrice Falder is Sir Robert Norberton's recently deceased sister whose death he conceals by having the actor Norlett impersonate her so that he can collect his race winnings before the news of her death becomes public in The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place.
Lady Brackenstall (née Mary Fraser of Adelaide), an Australian-born woman who conceals the true circumstances of her abusive husband's death in 'The Adventure of the Abbey Grange'.

Lady Eva Blackwell is the most beautiful debutante of last season in The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton from The Return of Sherlock Holmes, blackmailed by Milverton over imprudent letters written in a past indiscretion.

Lady Frances Carfax is the last survivor of a noble direct family line who disappears while travelling in Europe, chloroformed by the impostor Holy Peters and nearly buried alive in a coffin in The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax.
Lady Galloway, a slim, silver-haired woman of sensitive and superior bearing, a guest at Valentin's garden party in the Father Brown story 'The Secret Garden'.

Lady Hilda Trelawney Hope is the wife of the Secretary of State who secretly visits Eduardo Lucas's rooms to retrieve a compromising letter, thereby entangling herself in the Second Stain diplomatic case in The Adventure of the Second Stain.
Lady Jerland is a passenger aboard a transatlantic liner whose jewels are stolen by Arsène Lupin in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
Lady Margaret Graham, a pale, pretty girl with an elfin face and copper-coloured hair, daughter of Lord Galloway, appearing at the garden party in the Father Brown story 'The Secret Garden'.
Lady Miriam Marden, a long, languid, and elegant aristocratic woman who sends for Father Brown in the Father Brown story 'The Actor and the Alibi'.
Lady Mounteagle, a prominent and influential hostess at the centre of the jewel theft plot in the Father Brown story 'The Red Moon of Meru'.
Lady Outram, an American-born matron who carries her grey hair gracefully and is the only woman present at the gathering in the Father Brown story 'The Chief Mourner of Marne'.
Lady Pulman, an aristocrat said to possess famous jewellery at the House, mentioned as a potential target in the Father Brown story 'The Man with Two Beards'.
Lady Strongborough is a character mentioned in connection with recovered jewels in The Blonde Lady (Arsène Lupin).

Lal Chowdar is the faithful old Indian servant of Captain Morstan who assisted Major Sholto in the disposal of Morstan's body in The Sign of the Four, dying before the events of the novel with the secret intact.

Lal Rao is the butler at Pondicherry Lodge in The Sign of the Four who is identified by Holmes as the inside confederate who admitted Jonathan Small and Tonga on the night of Bartholomew Sholto's murder.
Laura Hope, a dark, elegant, quick-eyed young woman in black who is courted by both Angus and Welkin in the Father Brown story 'The Invisible Man'.

Mrs. Laura Lyons, the estranged daughter of Mr. Frankland who lived in Coombe Tracey and wrote the letter luring Sir Charles Baskerville to the fatal night meeting, acting under Stapleton's instructions and promise of marriage.
Lawler is an elderly Scowrer member of Merton County Lodge 249 who participates in the violent lodge meeting at Crow Hill Mine in Part II, Chapter V of The Valley of Fear.
Leach, one of the four clever gang members who conspired together, named alongside Pitford, Hearn, and Jezzard in Dr. Thorndyke's 'John Thorndyke's Cases'.
Lecocq, the famous fictional French detective created by Emile Gaboriau, cited alongside Sherlock Holmes as a model of detective fiction in the Father Brown story 'The Secret of Father Brown'.

Lecoq is the fictional French detective in Gaboriau's novels, whom Holmes dismisses as a miserable bungler with only energy to recommend him in A Study in Scarlet, using him as a foil to define his own superior methods.
Leonard Quinton, a pale, slight poet who carefully arranges exotic effects at his home and is found dead there in the Father Brown story 'The Wrong Shape'.
Leonard Wolfe (alias Thomas Barlow), arrested at Eltham while burying a compressed-air rifle in his garden after the murder of Alfred Hartridge in Dr. Thorndyke's 'John Thorndyke's Cases'.

Leonardo is the circus strongman who was Eugenia Ronder's lover and accomplice in the murder of her brutal showman husband Ronder, whose lion completed the killing, in The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger.
Leontine Zalti is the Countess who is murdered in her apartment on the avenue Hoche, serving as the victim in a case investigated within The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
Lestrade, the official Scotland Yard detective used by a character as an illustrative example of the conventional investigator in the Father Brown story 'The Mirror of the Magistrate'.

Leverton is a Pinkerton's American Agency detective who assists Holmes in The Adventure of the Red Circle in tracking the murderous Italian secret society member Giuseppe Gorgiano to his London hiding place.
Lopez is Don Juan Murillo's loyal secretary and henchman in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge who maintains the tyrant's disguise in England after Murillo fled San Pedro.
Lord Amber, a young aristocrat who moved in wild society out of misguided chivalry and ended up paying blackmail to London's worst criminals, mentioned in the Father Brown story 'The Flying Stars'.
Lord Bellinger, twice Premier of Britain, who secretly consults Holmes about the theft of a state document in 'The Adventure of the Second Stain'.
Lord Cantlemere, the sceptical representative of the illustrious client who doubts Holmes's ability to recover the Mazarin Stone in 'The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone'.
Lord Ernest Belville, an English nobleman who engaged Raffles and Bunny to catch a persistent thief in the story 'To Catch a Thief' from 'Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman'.
Lord Falconroy, a celebrated English aristocratic traveller, fresh from England, who appears at the gathering in the Father Brown story 'The Mistake of the Machine'.
Lord Galloway, the choleric, russet-faced English Ambassador to France who is present at Valentin's ill-fated garden party in the Father Brown story 'The Secret Garden'.

Lord Glengyle (Archibald Ogilvie), the recently deceased Scottish eccentric who is described as the nearest approach to a good man ever born at Glengyle in the Father Brown story 'The Honour of Israel Gow'.
Lord Lochmaben, the host at the country house party where Raffles and Bunny are guests in the story 'Out of Paradise' from 'A Thief in the Night'.
Lord Mount-James, the extremely miserly uncle who is Godfrey Staunton's nearest relative, consulted by Holmes in 'The Adventure of the Missing Three-Quarter'.
Lord Mounteagle, a man who took his Oriental interests more seriously than his wife, the owner of the Ruby of Meru in the Father Brown story 'The Red Moon of Meru'.
Lord Pooley, the organiser of the boxing match between Malvoli and Nigger Ned, who is questioned by Father Brown in the story 'The God of the Gongs'.

Lord Robert St. Simon is the English nobleman who calls on Holmes to investigate the disappearance of his American bride Hatty Doran from their wedding breakfast in The Adventure of the Noble Bachelor.
Lord Saltire (Arthur) is the Duke of Holdernesse's only surviving legitimate child who is abducted from the Priory School by his half-brother James Wilder in The Adventure of the Priory School.
Louis Becker, the twin brother of the criminal Arnold Becker, who was also known to the police and was eventually executed, appearing in the backstory of the Father Brown story 'The Secret Garden'.
Louis Valmeras, who was in reality Arsene Lupin in disguise as Baron Anfredi, who held Raymonde de Saint-Veran as his prisoner-wife inside the Hollow Needle.

Dr. H. Lowenstein of Prague is the famous rejuvenation researcher whose serum obtained from the langur monkey is secretly administered to Professor Presbury, causing the professor's alarming ape-like nocturnal behaviour in The Adventure of the Creeping Man.

Lucien Destange is the elderly architect in The Blonde Lady who designed houses with hidden secret passages exploited by Arsène Lupin, who visits Destange posing as his beloved Maxime Bermond to gain access to the architectural records.

Lucy Ferrier, the adopted daughter of John Ferrier in A Study in Scarlet, described as the flower of Utah, who is forced into a Mormon marriage and whose death drives Jefferson Hope's vendetta against Drebber and Stangerson.
Ludovic Imbert is the man whose life Arsène Lupin saves, and who asks Lupin his name in return for the rescue, in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
Ludwig Arnhold, the genuinely heroic eldest Arnhold brother who died sword in hand during the capture of the city in the Father Brown story 'The Fairy Tale of Father Brown'.
Luke P. Tanner, a Pennsylvanian figure cited by a character as more 'progressive' than Walt Whitman, mentioned in the Father Brown story 'The Secret Garden'.
Mad Joe, the village idiot who was the nephew of the blacksmith and the only early-morning worshipper at the church in the Father Brown story 'The Hammer of God'.
Madame Andermatt is the woman who authored the letter concealed inside a cigar, used to pass intelligence in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.

Madame Charpentier is the proprietress of the Torquay Terrace boarding house in A Study in Scarlet where Enoch Drebber lodged; she is seized with terror when Gregson questions her about Drebber's movements.
Madame Grunov, a dominant, sensationally dressed woman with yellow hair who is the wife of Mr. Grunov in the Father Brown story 'The Worst Crime in the World'.
Madame Renaud is the wife of the prison inspector who assists Arsène Lupin in a train compartment, enabling his escape in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
Maggie MacNab, a young woman who alarms Father Brown with the claim that James has been murdered in the Father Brown story 'The Absence of Mr Glass'.
Magnus, a sinister figure clad entirely in black including black gloves who appears dramatically on a ridge in the Father Brown story 'The Three Tools of Death'.
Mahomet Singh, one of the Four who signed the Agra treasure pact and was left to guard the door during the murder of Achmet in 'The Sign of the Four'.

Maître Detinan is an influential radical lawyer who serves as Arsène Lupin's legal counsel, is interviewed by reporters, and firmly maintains lawyer-client privilege when pressured by both Ganimard and Holmlock Shears in The Blonde Lady.
Major Murray, a lean, dark-haired Ulster Protestant who served under General St. Clare and appears in the backstory of the Father Brown story 'The Sign of the Broken Sword'.
Major Putnam, an Anglo-Indian who owns a house with a Maltese cook named Marco, the setting for the Father Brown story 'The Salad of Colonel Cray'.
Major John Sholto, a former Indian Army officer who retired to Pondicherry Lodge, kept the secret of the Agra Treasure hidden from his sons until his deathbed, having betrayed Captain Morstan.
Mr. Mallow, a shy, big, brown-eyed young man who drops a cup and apologises awkwardly at the gathering in the Father Brown story 'The Chief Mourner of Marne'.
Malvoli, an Italian prizefighter who is one of the two combatants in the championship bout at the centre of the Father Brown story 'The God of the Gongs'.
The man with a crooked or false nose, a mysterious absent figure known only as 'Mr Glass', alluded to in the Father Brown story 'The Absence of Mr Glass'.
Marco, a manservant with earrings who opens a large black case at Prince Saradine's command in the Father Brown story 'The Sins of Prince Saradine'.
Marco, the Maltese cook employed by Major Putnam, who is skilled with sauces and served the household in difficult circumstances in the Father Brown story 'The Salad of Colonel Cray'.
Maria Gibson, née Pinto, is the jealous Brazilian-born wife of Neil Gibson who stages her own death on Thor Bridge to frame the governess Grace Dunbar for murder in The Problem of Thor Bridge.
Marie Devine, Lady Frances Carfax's maid, whose sudden marriage provides a clue to the whereabouts of her mistress in 'The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax'.
Marlow Bates, the estate manager at Thor Place whose testimony assists Holmes in 'The Problem of Thor Bridge'.
The Marquess of Montalva, an alias assumed by Don Juan Murillo while being pursued by his political enemies in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge'.
The Marquis of Marne, a reclusive figure hidden in a black hood who is revealed to be Maurice Mair impersonating his dead cousin James in the Father Brown story 'The Chief Mourner of Marne'.
Martha is the elderly housekeeper Holmes plants inside Von Bork's household in His Last Bow, serving the German spy for five years while feeding intelligence to Holmes.

Mrs. Mary Maberley is the widow of Douglas Maberley in The Adventure of the Three Gables whose house is burgled by Isadora Klein's agents seeking to steal the manuscript novel Douglas wrote about his affair with Klein before his death.

Mary Morstan, the calm and resourceful young governess who consults Holmes in The Sign of the Four about mysterious annual pearls she has received, and whose loss of the Agra Treasure she accepts calmly so she can marry Watson.
Miss Mary Sutherland of Lower Brixton Road, a guileless young woman deceived by her own stepfather disguised as her fiance Hosmer Angel in the Sherlock Holmes story 'A Case of Identity'.
Mason the plate-layer, a railway worker who discovered the body of Arthur Cadogan West on the Underground tracks in 'The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans'.
The Master of the Mountain, a world-famous fortune-teller by crystal and chiromancy with a refined, pointed face, who appears at the gathering in the Father Brown story 'The Red Moon of Meru'.
Master Vatinel is the carter identified by a witness as the owner of the vehicle involved in key events in The Hollow Needle (Arsène Lupin).
Maud Bellamy, the young woman secretly engaged to the drowned Fitzroy McPherson, whose grief and courage impress Holmes in 'The Adventure of the Lion's Mane'.
Maurice Blum, a man who began as a sincere anarchist and champion of the poor but degenerated into a spy and informer, mentioned in the Father Brown story 'The Flying Stars'.
M. Maurice Brun, a French intellectual and companion of M. Armagnac, who crosses the Champs-Elysees with him at the opening of the Father Brown story 'The Duel of Dr Hirsch'.
Maurice Mair, the brilliant impersonator who took the place of his dead cousin James Mair as the Marquis of Marne and lived in seclusion, revealed by Father Brown in 'The Chief Mourner of Marne'.
Maxime Bermond, the alias under which Arsene Lupin endeared himself to Clotilde Destange and her household in the story 'The Blonde Lady'.

McMurdo is the ex-prizefighter gatekeeper at Pondicherry Lodge in The Sign of the Four who recognises Holmes from a bout, allows entry, and is later arrested as an accessory to Bartholomew Sholto's murder.
Young Medlicott, a peripheral figure involved in the events of the Raffles story 'A Bad Night' from 'A Thief in the Night'.
Menzies is the great bearded Scottish mine engineer at Crow Hill in Vermissa Valley who witnesses the Scowrers' violence in Part II, Chapter V of The Valley of Fear.
Detective Merton, a young detective who leads Father Brown across the fields to the railway at the conclusion of 'The Three Tools of Death'.
Michael Flood, an unnaturally lean man with a hooked nose who identifies himself in a snappy manner during the investigation in the Father Brown story 'The Mirror of the Magistrate'.
Michael Moonshine, a once-famous criminal operating under many aliases who has recently been released and is suspected of planning a robbery in the Father Brown story 'The Man with Two Beards'.
Michel Beaumont, an alias used by Arsene Lupin during the crystal stopper investigation, under which he presented himself to Clarisse as a journalist.

Mike Scanlan, a member of Lodge 341 of the Scowrers in Vermissa Valley in The Valley of Fear, who befriends the newly arrived McMurdo and introduces him to the criminal fraternity's Molly Maguires-like organisation.
Miles McLaren, one of the three scholarship candidates at St. Luke's College, named in connection with the examination paper theft in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Three Students'.

Milverton's avenger is the unnamed noblewoman who shoots Charles Augustus Milverton dead at his desk in The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton, avenging his blackmail that drove her first husband to his death.

Minna Adler is a German girl from Bremen working as a waitress at the Empire Restaurant on Fenchurch Street who is found murdered with her throat cut while sleeping, serving as the victim in a case from John Thorndyke's Cases.

Miriam Goldstein is a red-haired, short, powerfully built, wild-eyed stencil-cutter for decorators who is accused and arrested in connection with the same case as murder victim Minna Adler in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Miss Barlow, the editor's typist who is instructed to take down a letter to Francis Finn in the Father Brown story 'The Purple Wig'.

Miss Burnet is the governess at High Gable who assists in exposing Don Juan Murillo, the Tiger of San Pedro, and is connected to Victor Durando's revolutionary network in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge.
Miss Dobney, the companion of Lady Frances Carfax, who first brought the case of her employer's disappearance to Holmes's attention in 'The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax'.

Mme. Henri Fournaye is the Creole wife who occupies a villa in the Rue Austerlitz in Paris and murders her husband Eduardo Lucas in his London lodgings on discovering his double life, complicating Holmes's Second Stain investigation.
Mordecai Smith, the owner of the steam launch Aurora, whose boat was hired by Jonathan Small for the Thames chase in 'The Sign of the Four'.

Mortimer Tregennis is the man who comes to Holmes's Sussex villa to report that his brothers and sister were found mad or dead after an evening at home, and who is himself later poisoned by Dr. Leon Sterndale in The Adventure of the Devil's Foot.
Moser, a figure briefly mentioned in the general background of the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax'.
Mr. Atkinson, a young man in a billycock hat who is told by the doctor to leave the dying man's room in the Father Brown story 'The Wrong Shape'.
Mr. Audley, the amiable, elderly president of the Twelve True Fishermen's club who still wears Gladstone collars in the Father Brown story 'The Queer Feet'.

Mr. Brodribb is a portly, elderly, old-school solicitor who is both a Weybridge neighbour of Alfred Calverley and a long-standing friend of Thorndyke, through whom he introduces clients to the doctor in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Mr Glass, a mysterious and apparently absent individual whose name is overheard through a door and whose existence puzzles Dr. Hood in the Father Brown story 'The Absence of Mr Glass'.
Mr. Grunov, a short man with a big beard and broad, sleepy-eyed face who passes as Madame Grunov's husband in the Father Brown story 'The Worst Crime in the World'.

Mr. Hurst is John Bellingham's cousin, described as a florid, good-looking businessman who owns a house in Eltham and appears among the parties involved in the disappearance case at the centre of The Vanishing Man (Dr Thorndyke).
Mr. Jellicoe, the solicitor and agent for John Bellingham, a small, spare, mummy-faced man of dry and precise manner who appears in Dr. Thorndyke's 'The Vanishing Man'.
Mr Justice Monkhouse, a judge with a reputation for humorous remarks who presides over the trial in the Father Brown story 'The Man in the Passage'.
Mr. Lawley is a visitor received rather stiffly by Thorndyke in The Red Thumb Mark, one of the Dr Thorndyke stories.
Mr. Lever, the Jewish owner of the hotel who made nearly a million pounds from it, mentioned in the Father Brown story 'The Queer Feet'.

Mr. Marchmont is a solicitor and friend of Thorndyke who waited at Hartridge's flat, informed Thorndyke about Wolfe, and brought handwriting evidence, and who also introduces the distressed client Solomon Löwe to Thorndyke in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Mr. Maturin, the occupant of the rooms targeted in the Raffles story 'No Sinecure' from 'Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman'.

Mr. Melas is a professional Greek interpreter who is forcibly abducted and compelled to translate for a captive Greek man, serving as the client at the centre of The Adventure of the Greek Interpreter in the Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.
Mr Patrick Butler K.C., the barrister who defended the prisoner and was deceptively underestimated as a mere socialite in the Father Brown story 'The Man in the Passage'.
Mr. Roundhay, the vicar at Poldhu Bay who alerts Holmes to the death of Brenda Tregennis in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Devil's Foot'.

Henry James Singleton is the Scotland Yard fingerprint expert who testifies at trial that the incriminating thumbprint belongs to the accused Reuben Hornby, only to be proven wrong by Thorndyke's analysis in The Red Thumb Mark.
Mr. Warren, the landlord of 221 Great Orme Street whose mysterious lodger draws Holmes into the case in 'The Adventure of the Red Circle'.
Mr. Williams, the notorious murderer cited by De Quincey as having slaughtered two entire families almost in silence, recalled by a character in the Father Brown story 'The Mirror of the Magistrate'.
Mrs. Allen is the buxom and cheerful housekeeper at Birlstone Manor House in The Valley of Fear, questioned by Holmes and Watson during their investigation in Part I, Chapters III and V.

Mrs. Amberley is the good-looking woman twenty years younger than Josiah Amberley who eloped with Dr. Ray Ernest and was murdered with him by her husband in a gas chamber in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman.
Mrs. Anthony, a woman with a slight Italian accent who lives at Prince Saradine's island retreat in the Father Brown story 'The Sins of Prince Saradine'.
Mrs. Bankes, a thin, dark woman of acid elegance married to Simon Bankes, appearing in the Father Brown story 'The Man with Two Beards'.
Mrs. Barnes, the magnificent red-gold-haired wife of the blacksmith, found sobbing on a bench at the scene of the crime in the Father Brown story 'The Hammer of God'.
Mrs. Eliza Barrymore, the housekeeper of Baskerville Hall whose secret nightly signalling to her escaped convict brother Selden, a fugitive on the moor, is discovered by Watson.
Mrs. Bernstone is the housekeeper at Pondicherry Lodge who had served Bartholomew Sholto in joy and sorrow for ten years and finds his body at the opening of The Sign of the Four.
Mrs Boulnois, a beautiful and formerly successful actress to whom the reserved scholar John Boulnois is devoted, whose presence attracts scandal in 'The Strange Crime of John Boulnois'.
Mrs. Cecil Forrester, the employer for whom Mary Morstan worked as a governess and who waited up for her return in 'The Sign of the Four'.
Mrs. Ferguson, the Peruvian-born wife of Robert Ferguson, a dark and passionate woman whose seemingly vampiric behaviour Holmes investigates in 'The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire'.

Mrs. Arabella Hornby is the wife of John Hornby who purchased a Thumbograph toy several months before the central events of The Red Thumb Mark, a detail that proves significant to Thorndyke's investigation.

Mrs Hudson, Sherlock Holmes's long-suffering landlady at 221B Baker Street who announces callers, admits visitors, and assists Holmes in the Dying Detective ruse, referred to variously as Mrs Turner in early adventures.
Mrs MacNab, an honest but sharp-tempered widow who is a member of Father Brown's flock and whose household situation prompts the investigation in 'The Absence of Mr Glass'.
Mrs. Mandeville, the theatrical manager Mandeville's quiet and pale-faced wife, a background figure in the Father Brown story 'The Actor and the Alibi'.
Mrs. Fraser is Holy Peters's wife and criminal accomplice in The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax, helping to chloroform and conceal Lady Frances in a coffin.
Mrs. Porter, the caretaker of the empty house, mentioned in the Sherlock Holmes story involving the disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax.
Mrs. Quinton, a heavy-faced woman with golden hair who appears in The Wrong Shape (Father Brown).
A housekeeper whose unexpected tip proves crucial to solving The Man with Two Beards (Father Brown).
Housekeeper to Mr. Peregrine Smart, noted as a witness in The Song of the Flying Fish (Father Brown).
Mrs. Sands, a powerful-looking woman with tight red hair who serves as Mandeville's wife's maid/dresser in The Actor and the Alibi (Father Brown).
Mrs. Sawyer, a disguise persona adopted in A Study in Scarlet to recover the incriminating wedding ring left at the Lauriston Gardens murder scene.
Mrs. Smith is a woman who writes in a letter that she is beginning to feel frightened about him, referenced in Chapter VIII of The Sign of the Four.
Mrs. Warren, a lodging-house keeper whose mysterious tenant prompts her to bring the matter to Sherlock Holmes in The Adventure of the Red Circle.
The huge mulatto cook employed at Wisteria Lodge, a sinister figure in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge (Sherlock Holmes).
Mr. Mundon Mandeville, a big, bullish theatrical manager who features in The Actor and the Alibi (Father Brown).
An unnamed dyed-whiskered man found murdered under a bandstand, wearing a purple scarf, in The God of the Gongs (Father Brown).
Murphy, a gipsy horse-dealer camped on the moor who provides local intelligence in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
Muscari, a flamboyant young Tuscan poet and romantic figure who becomes entangled in a robbery in The Paradise of Thieves (Father Brown).

Mycroft Holmes, Sherlock's elder brother, a government official of extraordinary intellect and sedentary habits who audits records for various government departments and occasionally assists on cases of national importance.
