Nathan Garrideb, an eccentric collector living at 136 Little Ryder Street who is duped by a counterfeiting scheme in The Adventure of the Three Garridebs.

Nathaniel Polton, Dr. Thorndyke's resourceful laboratory assistant who maintains the scientific equipment and the green case, appearing across both John Thorndyke's Cases and The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke.
An unnamed black cook wearing a white uniform at a waterfront establishment in The God of the Gongs (Father Brown).
Neil Gibson, the 'Gold King,' a wealthy and domineering American mine-owner entangled in the tragedy at Thor Bridge (Sherlock Holmes).
Commandant Neil O'Brien, a slim swaggering officer of the French Foreign Legion who appears in The Secret Garden (Father Brown).
Miss Nelly Underdown, an English passenger aboard La Provence who attracts Arsène Lupin's attentions and later allows a Kodak to fall into the sea.

Neville St. Clair is a respectable family man who secretly earns his living disguised as the professional beggar Hugh Boone, and whose mysterious disappearance Holmes investigates in The Adventure of the Man with the Twisted Lip.
Nicholas Carter, a fictional American detective mentioned alongside Sherlock Holmes and Lecocq as a famous literary investigator in The Secret of Father Brown.
Nigger Ned, a prize-fighter who confronts Father Brown's party at the waterfront in The God of the Gongs (Father Brown).
Nipper Nasmyth, a dangerous man encountered in The Field of Phillipi (Raffles).
Mr. Norlett is the actor hired as Lady Beatrice Falder's husband in The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place, used by Sir Robert Norberton to impersonate his dead sister before race day.
Norman Knight, a good-looking actor who plays the hero in a provincial theatre in The Actor and the Alibi (Father Brown).
Norman Neruda, a celebrated violinist whose Hallé concert Holmes wishes to attend in A Study in Scarlet.

Hugo Oberstein is one of three foreign spies, alongside La Rothière and Eduardo Lucas, who are behind the theft of the Bruce-Partington submarine plans in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans from The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
The Ogilvies, a wicked ancestral family whose portraits hang at the Gow estate, referenced in The Honour of Israel Gow (Father Brown).
Old Antonelli, a man whose father was killed and mother stolen by Prince Saradine, who has pursued vengeance across decades in The Sins of Prince Saradine (Father Brown).
The original (now deceased) Duke of Exmoor, who had a genuine slight ear malformation and was morbidly sensitive about it, as recalled in The Purple Wig (Father Brown).
Opal Bankes, the thin, dark, supposedly psychic daughter of Simon Bankes in The Man with Two Beards (Father Brown).
Oscar Brodski is a diamond merchant of considerable reputation and large business who buys stones principally from mines and who is murdered in a story from The Adventures of Dr Thorndyke.
Oscar Rian, alias Drugger Davis, a notorious criminal publicly identified by a woman in a courthouse vestibule in The Mistake of the Machine (Father Brown).
Osric Orm, one of the 'new poets,' a figure of some local notoriety whose verse is considered steep reading in The Mirror of the Magistrate (Father Brown).
Owen Tregennis, the sole survivor of a strange poisoning at a family gathering, found senseless in The Adventure of the Devil's Foot (Sherlock Holmes).

Parker is a garroter by trade who serves as Colonel Moran's sentinel posted outside Baker Street, and whose presence is noted by Holmes in The Adventure of the Empty House from The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
Parkinson, a dingy theatre dresser familiar to multiple actors in The Man in the Passage (Father Brown).

Patrick Cairns is the harpooner who confesses to Holmes that he killed Captain Peter Carey (Black Peter) by running a harpoon through him in his cabin in The Adventure of Black Peter from The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
Patrick Royce, formerly a well-known figure, who serves as secretary to the murder victim Sir Aaron Armstrong in The Three Tools of Death (Father Brown).
Paul Arnhold, one of three brothers who traitorously went over to the Imperial side and became the Chamberlain in The Fairy Tale of Father Brown.
Paul Petrofsky, a short, pock-marked Jewish packer in the wholesale Turkey-sponge trade, identified as the actual murderer in a Dr. Thorndyke case.
Pauline Stacey, the elder of two sisters, a heiress to a title and great estate who becomes a devotee of the Apollo cult in The Eye of Apollo (Father Brown).
An unnamed lean, black-haired, yellow-faced butler who serves the Admiral in The Perishing of the Pendragons (Father Brown).
Percy Haldean is the brother-in-law identified by the witness Jane through his exact description, including his distinctive stick and pocket-comb, in a case from John Thorndyke's Cases.

Percy Phelps, known as Tadpole Phelps at school, is a junior Foreign Office clerk who enlists Holmes's help after the secret Naval Treaty is stolen from under his care in The Adventure of the Naval Treaty from the Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.

Dr. Percy Trevelyan is a physician practising in Brook Street who consults Sherlock Holmes about a mysterious resident patient whose behaviour he cannot explain in The Adventure of the Resident Patient from the Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.
Mr. Peregrine Smart, a round-faced, round-bodied collector obsessed with a single aquarium ornament and prone to one repeated joke in The Song of the Flying Fish (Father Brown).
Perkins, Sir Henry Baskerville's coachman, tasked with stabling the horses at Coombe Tracey in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
Philip Bankes, the dark-curly-haired, fashion-conscious brother of Simon Bankes in The Man with Two Beards (Father Brown).

Philip Green is the man devoted to Lady Frances Carfax who tracked her across Europe and assists Holmes in the desperate search to find and revive her before she suffocates in the coffin in The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax.
Philip Hawker, a young man whose name is central to a dispute over stolen coins in The Head of Caesar (Father Brown).
Phroso the Phrenologist, a lean, sunburnt detective with an extravagant black moustache who investigates in The Red Moon of Meru (Father Brown).
Pierre Onfrey is a murderer who killed three members of the Lefebvre family and who is encountered aboard a transatlantic liner in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
Pierrot, the alias used by Gennaro in America, mentioned in connection with Hugo Oberstein in The Adventure of the Red Circle (Sherlock Holmes).
Pietro Venucci, a Neapolitan member of the Mafia whose murder is linked to the theft of the Borgia pearl in The Adventure of the Six Napoleons (Sherlock Holmes).
Pitford, a gang member who dined with the murder suspects in the morphia-poisoning case in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Pope Leo XIII, mentioned as the originator of a religious exercise that Father Brown borrowed from a friend in The Secret of Father Brown.
Louis Prasville, chief of police in The Crystal Stopper who received the investigation file from the examining judge and was personally determined to destroy the blackmailer Daubrecq.
A Presbyterian minister who joins the doctor and Catholic priest at the scene of a death in The Hammer of God (Father Brown).
President Olivier, the great Brazilian patriot who issued an ultimatum, referenced as the defender of justice in The Sign of the Broken Sword (Father Brown).
Prince Otto of Grossenmark, an Imperial governor installed to rule a small contested territory in The Fairy Tale of Father Brown.
Prince Paul Saradine, an elderly reclusive nobleman living as 'Mr. Paul' on a houseboat, the guilty party confronted in The Sins of Prince Saradine (Father Brown).

Professor Poppelbaum is a distinguished palaeographer at the British Museum who deciphers a cipher as Hebrew or Moabite script but fails to uncover its true meaning, appearing in The Moabite Cipher story within John Thorndyke's Cases.

Professor Copland is an expert witness who testifies that he is prepared to swear to having extracted over one grain of morphia from the substance in question, appearing in a case from John Thorndyke's Cases.

Professor Coram is the alias of the Russian nihilist Sergius hiding at Yoxley Old Place, whose estranged wife Anna stabs his secretary Willoughby Smith while searching for incriminating documents in The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez.
Professor Crake, a celebrated criminologist to whom Father Brown is introduced in The Man with Two Beards (Father Brown).
Professor Moriarty, 'the Napoleon of crime,' is Holmes's supreme adversary — the organiser of London's criminal network — whose death at Reichenbach Falls is described in The Final Problem.

Professor Presbury is the distinguished Camford University professor in The Adventure of the Creeping Man who secretly injects himself with Dr. Lowenstein's langur monkey serum, causing him to crawl along walls at night and alarming his wolf-hound Roy.
Ralph Randall, a character actor with a humorous hatchet face who specialises in elderly roles in The Actor and the Alibi (Father Brown).
Raymonde de Saint-Véran, a young woman kidnapped by Lupin's men and held captive by Baron Anfredi until rescued in The Hollow Needle (Arsène Lupin).

Reginald Musgrave is a fellow student of Holmes from their college days who later brings him the mystery of the Musgrave Ritual, an ancient family ceremony that conceals a hidden secret, in the Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.
Reuben Hayes is the innkeeper of the Fighting Cock pub who shelters Lord Saltire after his abduction and is implicated in the conspiracy in The Adventure of the Priory School.
Reuben Hornby, Dr. Thorndyke's client accused of diamond theft and cleared by fingerprint evidence in The Red Thumb Mark.
Reuben Hornby (Vanishing Man) is the young man who accosts the narrator Berkeley in the street and introduces himself as the nephew of the missing John Bellingham in The Vanishing Man (Dr Thorndyke).
Rev. Williamson is a defrocked clergyman who is tried alongside Woodley for the abduction of Violet Smith in The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist from The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
Robert Ferguson, a tea broker who consults Holmes about the alarming behaviour of his wife toward their infant son in The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire.

Rodger Baskerville is the younger brother of Sir Charles who fled England with a sinister reputation to South America, and is revealed as the father of Jack Stapleton, making Stapleton a concealed Baskerville heir in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
Rodger Prescott is described as the most famous counterfeiter in the world, a figure mentioned in a story from The Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes.

Ronald Adair is the Honourable young man shot dead in his locked room under inexplicable circumstances by Colonel Moran's air-gun in The Adventure of the Empty House, his murder bringing Holmes back to London from his staged death.
Ronder, an abusive circus showman who is killed by his own lion, whose death is later investigated in The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger (Sherlock Holmes).
Rose Spender, an elderly woman found drugged in a coffin, the intended murder victim in The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax (Sherlock Holmes).
Rosenthall, a wealthy man whose house Raffles regards as a prize target in A Costume Piece (The Amateur Cracksman).
Roy, Professor Presbury's wolf-hound that twice attacks the professor after his behaviour becomes dangerously erratic in The Adventure of the Creeping Man.
Ruby Adams, a pretty young woman with brave brown eyes who plays the Columbine role and witnesses the jewel theft in The Flying Stars (Father Brown).

Ruth Bellingham is Godfrey Bellingham's daughter and the love interest of narrator Berkeley, described as a handsome young woman of about twenty-five with dark hair, in The Vanishing Man (Dr Thorndyke).
Salvator is the master criminal who organised and led the Seven of Hearts conspiracy, whose identity is revealed in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
Sam, the old doorkeeper and sole permanent inhabitant of a provincial theatre during off-hours in The Actor and the Alibi (Father Brown).
Sam Merton, a boxer who is Count Sylvius's associate in the plot to steal the Mazarin Stone in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone (Sherlock Holmes).
Samuel Harrogate, a banker arrested for embezzlement of the Hull and Huddersfield Bank funds, unmasked in The Paradise of Thieves (Father Brown).

Selden, the Notting Hill Murderer, an escaped convict hiding on Dartmoor who is the brother-in-law of Barrymore's wife; his death on the moor briefly confuses Holmes's pursuit of Stapleton in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
Sergeant Coventry is the local police officer who has the Thor Bridge case in hand when Holmes and Watson arrive to investigate the apparent murder of Maria Gibson in The Problem of Thor Bridge.

Sergeant Payne is the detective sergeant who draws attention to the fact that the murderer was wearing a very remarkable pair of shoes that left distinctive footprints at the scene, in a story from John Thorndyke's Cases.
Sergeant Wilson, a local police officer in the Vermissa Valley who assists in investigating the crimes of the Scowrers secret society in The Valley of Fear.

Sherlock Holmes, the world's only consulting detective, solves cases through extraordinary powers of observation and deduction, operating from Baker Street alongside Dr. Watson across Doyle's full canon.
Sherman, a lanky old man who houses and lends Holmes the tracking dog Toby in The Sign of the Four.

Shinwell Johnson is Holmes's underworld informant, described as his agent in the lower classes, who surfaces intelligence on Isadora Klein for the Three Gables case in The Adventure of the Three Gables.
Sidney Johnson, the senior clerk at Woolwich Arsenal who is suspected in the theft of the Bruce-Partington submarine plans in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans.
Signor Rulli, one of the enemies of the Tiger of San Pedro (Murillo), mentioned in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge (Sherlock Holmes).
Signora Maroni (Miss Maroni), a talented young actress of Italian parentage cast in the provincial theatre production in The Actor and the Alibi (Father Brown).
Silas Hickler, a cheerful-seeming man concealing a murderous nature, whose case is handled by Dr. Thorndyke in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke.
Silver Blaze is the celebrated racehorse and winner of the Wessex Cup whose disappearance from King's Pyland stables forms the mystery Holmes solves in the Memoirs story Silver Blaze.
Simeon Barnes, the village blacksmith, described as the largest and strongest man for forty miles, a suspect in The Hammer of God (Father Brown).
Mr. Simon Bankes, a worthy, wealthy suburban householder at whose breakfast table The Man with Two Beards (Father Brown) begins.
Simon, Dr. Hirsch's elderly servant, so grave and medical in bearing that he might be mistaken for a doctor himself in The Duel of Dr. Hirsch (Father Brown).
Sir Aaron Armstrong, an immensely popular and publicly entertaining philanthropist found dead in The Three Tools of Death (Father Brown).
Sir Arthur Travers, the brilliant Crown prosecutor who faces Father Brown in The Mirror of the Magistrate (Father Brown).
Sir Arthur Vaudrey, a distinguished elderly gentleman in a light-grey suit and white hat who mysteriously vanishes in The Vanishing of Vaudrey (Father Brown).
Sir Cecil Fanshaw, a young Cornish squire and enthusiastic admirer of Cornish coastal scenery who introduces Father Brown to the Pendragon story in The Perishing of the Pendragons.

Sir Charles Baskerville is the elderly baronet found dead in the Yew Alley of Baskerville Hall, his death engineered by Jack Stapleton using a phosphorus-painted hound and Mrs. Lyons as lure, triggering the mystery in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
Sir Claude Champion, a publicly celebrated figure who is murdered at his country estate in The Strange Crime of John Boulnois (Father Brown).
Sir Eustace Brackenstall is the brutal baronet at Abbey Grange whose killing by Captain Jack Crocker is covered up by his wife Lady Brackenstall in The Adventure of the Abbey Grange.

Sir George Burnwell is described as one of the most dangerous men in England and an absolutely desperate, ruined gambler who seduces Mary Holder and orchestrates the theft of the beryl coronet in The Adventure of the Beryl Coronet.

Sir Hector Trumpler K.C. is the large, florid, and untidy counsel for the prosecution who delivers the opening speech and cross-examines the defence witnesses in the trial at the centre of The Red Thumb Mark (Dr Thorndyke).

Sir Henry Baskerville, the Canadian-born heir to the Baskerville estate in The Hound of the Baskervilles, arrives in England to claim his inheritance and becomes the target of Stapleton's murderous scheme.
Sir Humphrey Gwynne, also known as Mr. Justice Gwynne, a prominent judge whose garden borders the scene of events in The Mirror of the Magistrate (Father Brown).

Sir James Damery is a well-known society figure who acts as intermediary between Holmes and his client, bringing Holmes the delicate case involving the dangerous Baron Gruner in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client from the Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes.
Sir James Musgrave, a Jacobite martyr of the family line, known only from a portrait in the Musgrave gallery in The Worst Crime in the World (Father Brown).

Sir James Saunders is the famous London dermatologist in The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier who travels to Tuxbury Old Park and diagnoses Godfrey Emsworth's scaling skin condition as ichthyosis rather than leprosy.
Sir James Walter, the chief custodian of the Bruce-Partington submarine plans, found dead of shock following their theft in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans.
Sir John Cockspur, a terrier-faced, grey-haired forceful man, a guest at the reunion that opens The Chief Mourner of Marne (Father Brown).
Sir John Musgrave, the current thin, keen-looking baronet of the Musgrave line in The Worst Crime in the World (Father Brown).
Sir Leopold Fischer, Ruby Adams's godfather who visits on Boxing Day and turns out to be the jewel thief in The Flying Stars (Father Brown).
Sir Leopold Pulman, a recent newcomer to the neighbourhood residing at Beechwood House, mentioned in The Man with Two Beards (Father Brown).
Sir Leslie Oakshott is a famous surgeon referenced in connection with the injuries sustained in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client from the Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes.
Sir Matthew Blake, the able defence counsel who faces Father Brown in The Mirror of the Magistrate (Father Brown).

Sir Peter Pendragon, a naval ancestor of the Pendragon family who built the tower during the Spanish wars and whose wife was said to have been cursed by a dying Spaniard in The Perishing of the Pendragons (Father Brown).
Sir Robert Musgrave, a perfectly honest old Cavalier, known only from a portrait in the family gallery in The Worst Crime in the World (Father Brown).

Sir Robert Norberton is the owner of Shoscombe Old Place in The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place who conceals his sister Lady Beatrice's death until after the Shoscombe Prince race, using an actor to impersonate her.
Sir Roger Musgrave, a treacherous ancestor of William-of-Orange times, condemned as a liar and rascal by family tradition in The Worst Crime in the World (Father Brown).
Sir Walter Cowdray, the heavy but weighty Crown prosecutor in the theatre murder trial in The Man in the Passage (Father Brown).
Sir Wilson Seymour, a man of universal social importance whose eminence is felt rather than explained in The Man in the Passage (Father Brown).
Mr. Smith, an active little bald man with an honest wig introduced to Father Brown as a beekeeper, a character in The Man with Two Beards (Father Brown).
Solomon Löwe is the distressed, ruined client and brother of Isaac Löwe who comes to Thorndyke in despair, bringing a case that Thorndyke agrees to investigate in John Thorndyke's Cases.

Sonia Kritchnoff, a secretary employed by M. Gournay-Martin, is secretly Lupin's confidante and love interest, operating inside the household while assisting his plans and ultimately escaping with him.
General St. Clare's daughter, protected from knowledge of her father's treachery which he took to the grave in The Sign of the Broken Sword (Father Brown).

Young Stamford is Watson's former dresser at Bart's Hospital whose chance encounter with Watson at the Criterion Bar in A Study in Scarlet leads directly to the introduction with Holmes and the sharing of Baker Street rooms.

Stanislas Vorenglade is a political ally of Daubrecq and key figure in his corruption network, listed among the twenty-seven names on the crystal stopper, who appears in the final legal manoeuvres in The Crystal Stopper (Arsène Lupin).

Inspector Stanley Hopkins is the promising young Scotland Yard detective who repeatedly consults Holmes in The Return of Sherlock Holmes, appearing in The Adventure of Black Peter, The Adventure of the Abbey Grange, and The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez.
Staples, the butler who had left Garcia's employ before the murder, a witness in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge (Sherlock Holmes).
Steve Dixie, a negro pugilist who delivers a menacing warning to Holmes in The Adventure of the Three Gables (Sherlock Holmes).

Superintendent Miller is the tall, military superintendent from Scotland Yard whom Thorndyke brings to the inquest with plain-clothes and uniformed officers to stand by the door and prevent the escape of Petrofsky in The Red Thumb Mark.

Miss Susan Cushing is the recipient of a gruesome parcel containing two severed human ears who consults Sherlock Holmes about the disturbing delivery in The Adventure of the Cardboard Box from the Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.

Suzanne Gerbois is the young woman held prisoner by Arsène Lupin as a bargaining tool while he wins the lottery ticket, and who is released by Lupin once he secures the Blue Diamond in The Blonde Lady.
Sybil Rye, the pale, alarmed lady of the household who summons Father Brown when Sir Arthur Vaudrey disappears in The Vanishing of Vaudrey (Father Brown).
Ted Baldwin, a violent member of the Scowrers in Vermissa Valley, is the man whose body is falsely identified as John Douglas's in the Birlstone Manor murder and who ultimately escapes the scaffold.
Thaddeus Sholto, the nervous and hypochondriac son of Major Sholto who contacts Mary Morstan to reveal the secret of the Agra Treasure and his late father's guilty knowledge.
The Growler, one of Lupin's two hired oarsmen who rows the boat across the lake at Monte Carlo in The Crystal Stopper (Arsène Lupin).
The Masher, one of Lupin's two hired oarsmen who keeps watch outside Daubrecq's house in The Crystal Stopper (Arsène Lupin).
An unnamed Spaniard who, dying, cursed the Pendragon family to their faces and swore he would outlive them across generations in The Perishing of the Pendragons (Father Brown).
Miss Theresa Talbot, a vivacious woman with an ugly attractive face and greying hair who accompanies the theatrical company in The Actor and the Alibi (Father Brown).
Theresa Wright, the loyal maid of Lady Brackenstall who knows the truth behind the Abbey Grange murder in The Adventure of the Abbey Grange (Sherlock Holmes).
Tiger Cormac is a Scowrer member of Vermissa Valley Lodge 341 present at lodge meetings in Part II, Chapters III and VII of The Valley of Fear.
Tito Castalotte is the man murdered by Giuseppe Gorgiano of the Red Circle secret society, whose death is connected to the case of Gennaro and Emilia Lucca in The Adventure of the Red Circle.
Tommy Hunter, Flambeau's cousin, a conventionally correct young man who accompanies the party investigating the Red Moon of Meru (Father Brown).

Tonga, the diminutive Andaman Islander brought to England by Jonathan Small who killed Bartholomew Sholto with a poisoned blowpipe dart and was shot dead by Holmes and Watson while fleeing down the Thames in The Sign of the Four.
Trelawney Hope, the European Secretary whose stolen letter threatens international peace in The Adventure of the Second Stain (Sherlock Holmes).
Trevor Bennett, the secretary and assistant to Professor Presbury whose disturbing behaviour he reports to Holmes in The Adventure of the Creeping Man.
Two negro servants in tight yellow uniforms who are the Admiral's only household staff in The Perishing of the Pendragons (Father Brown).
Vaucheray, the confederate of Gilbert who committed the actual murder in The Crystal Stopper, arrested alongside Gilbert and subsequently condemned to death.
Vernon Vaudrey, nephew and heir of Sir Arthur Vaudrey, a suspect when his uncle vanishes in The Vanishing of Vaudrey (Father Brown).
Rev. J. C. Elman, M.A., Vicar of Little Purlington, to whose remote parish Holmes sends Amberley as a decoy in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman.

Victoire, the housekeeper loyal to Lupin in Arsène Lupin, acts as his secret agent inside the Gournay-Martin household, sheltering him, cutting telephone wires on his orders, and ultimately escaping with him.

Victor Danègre is the servant who murders the Countess Zalti and steals the black pearl in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin, later manipulated by Lupin who holds evidence of the crime to force cooperation.
Victor Durando is a revolutionary conspirator associated with opposition to the Tiger of San Pedro, Don Juan Murillo, connected to Miss Burnet's network in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge.

Victor Hatherley is a hydraulic engineer who loses his thumb after being lured under false pretences to a counterfeiting operation by the man posing as Colonel Lysander Stark in The Adventure of the Engineer's Thumb.

Victor Savage is Culverton Smith's nephew killed by Smith to test a secret tropical poison, whose death Holmes feigns illness to investigate, provoking Smith's confession in The Adventure of the Dying Detective.

Victor Trevor is the only friend Holmes made during his two years at college, and it is the dark secret hidden in his father's past that drives the mystery at the heart of The Adventure of the Gloria Scott in the Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.

Victorien Mergy is Clarisse's husband who died in disgrace, leaving his son Gilbert to bear the burden of his dishonour as one of the twenty-seven names on Daubrecq's list in The Crystal Stopper (Arsène Lupin).
A red-haired, irascible but exceptionally capable village surgeon who acts with great promptitude in a medical emergency in The Chief Mourner of Marne (Father Brown).
Viola Grayson, daughter of an admiral, who never married and is the subject of old grief for the Marquis of Marne in The Chief Mourner of Marne (Father Brown).
Violet de Merville, the daughter engaged to the dangerous Baron Gruner, who Holmes tries to protect from a disastrous marriage in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client.
Violet Hunter, a governess who consults Holmes about a suspicious employment offer involving the Copper Beeches in The Adventure of the Copper Beeches.

Violet Smith is the music teacher of Charlington Hall who is stalked by a mysterious cyclist and ultimately abducted and subjected to a forced marriage by Jack Woodley and the defrocked clergyman Williamson in The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist.
Violet Westbury, fiancée of the murdered Arthur Cadogan West whose testimony helps Holmes reconstruct the theft of the Bruce-Partington plans.
Von Bischoff, a murderer cited by Holmes as someone who would have been convicted had the haemoglobin test existed at the time, in A Study in Scarlet.
Von Bork, the German spymaster operating in England before the First World War who is captured by Holmes (posing as Altamont) in His Last Bow, foiling a German intelligence network.
Walter Hornby, Reuben's cousin, articled at the same firm, whose suspicious conduct marks him as a likely thief in The Red Thumb Mark (Dr. Thorndyke).
Walter Pendragon, nephew of the Admiral, whose safe return from his ship that night is the old man's greatest hope in The Perishing of the Pendragons (Father Brown).

White Mason, the quiet, comfortable-looking chief constable of the Sussex Constabulary who leads the local side of the Birlstone murder investigation in The Valley of Fear alongside Inspector MacDonald.
A tall, white-haired Italian priest who dines with a companion at a restaurant table and takes his leave at the start of The Paradise of Thieves (Father Brown).
Wicks, a small bald bespectacled barber startled by Father Brown and Flambeau's sudden appearance at his shop in The Vanishing of Vaudrey (Father Brown).
Wiggins, the street-wise leader of Holmes's Baker Street Irregulars, a band of London urchin spies who assist in investigations for a shilling apiece.
Rev. Wilfred Bohun, a devout, austere clergyman whose brother's crime overshadows events in The Hammer of God (Father Brown).
Wilfred Underhill, a sharp-faced, light-haired amateur interested in detection who assists Father Brown in The Mirror of the Magistrate (Father Brown).

The Willaby Brothers (Arthur Willaby), Scowrers members in Vermissa Valley in The Valley of Fear who participated in the lodge's violent activities and were eventually brought to justice at the trial alongside McGinty.
William Hales of Stake Royal, a local landowner named among the Scowrers' victims in Part II of The Valley of Fear.

Willoughby Smith is the young secretary of Professor Coram who is accidentally stabbed by Anna when she enters Yoxley Old Place to search for the incriminating documents against her nihilist comrades in The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez.
Wilson, the English detective Holmlock Shears's loyal assistant in The Blonde Lady, who accompanies Shears through Paris taking notes and is separated from him during Lupin's counter-operations.
An unnamed poor woman in a courthouse vestibule who stands up screaming and points out Drugger Davis when he is brought in, in The Mistake of the Machine (Father Brown).
ZALADIN, a self-proclaimed World's Greatest Conjurer, Contortionist, Ventriloquist and Human Kangaroo whose act is advertised in The Absence of Mr. Glass (Father Brown).

The Josiah Amberley double murder, in which Amberley gassed his wife and her lover Dr. Ray Ernest in a specially constructed room at The Haven in Lewisham, concealing the bodies in a disused well beneath a dog-kennel.

The crime of blackmail by Arthur Carstairs consists of his extorting money from his own family while living in disguise, having sunk to the basest means of making money including selling off the Collection piece by piece, as revealed in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The apparent suicide of Arthur Carstairs, found dead in a brown dressing-gown with an empty medicine bottle on the floor in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
An attempted assassination of Father Brown by the hotel proprietor, who drops a flat shining dagger and flees in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The attempted murder of Sir Henry Baskerville, orchestrated by Jack Stapleton using a trained and phosphorus-painted hound to terrorize and ultimately kill the new Baskerville heir.
The attempted murder of Walter Pendragon, who is pushed into a dark river by a leaping figure and nearly drowned in The Perishing of the Pendragons (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
The murder of Michael Moonshine and theft of emeralds by John Bankes, concealed within a staged burglary at the hamlet in The Man with Two Beards (The Secret of Father Brown).
The shooting of Lady Mounteagle's secretary Barnard, apparently by an escaping burglar in the garden, in The Secret of Father Brown.
The burglary of Beechwood House and theft of Lady Pulman's jewels, an apparently professional crime investigated in The Secret of Father Brown.
The sacrificial battle of the Black River in Brazil, where General St. Clare ordered eight hundred English soldiers to their deaths to cover his treason in The Innocence of Father Brown.

John Boulnois's lie to an American journalist claiming he had gone to Pendragon Park and shut the door in Champion's face, covering up the true circumstances of Champion's death in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The kidnapping for ransom of an English millionaire by an Italian brigand chief who demands two thousand pounds for his release in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

The Burglary of Rosenthall's House, the adventure in A Costume Piece in which Raffles and Bunny break into the house in disguise, marking Bunny's first active participation in Raffles's criminal enterprises.
The self-inflicted sword wound of Sir Claude Champion, witnessed by his actress companion on the sundial at Pendragon Park, in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The minor theft of a Caesar bronze coin by Christabel from a collection, intended as a love gift for her sweetheart Philip, in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
McMurdo's counterfeiting operation, the fabricated cover story and subsequent arrest that establishes Jack McMurdo's criminal credentials before the Scowrers in The Valley of Fear.
A double murder committed by Dalmon in the United States, a known crime that places him in Vaudrey's power through blackmail in The Secret of Father Brown.
A past murder committed by Dalmon long before the story, which Vaudrey discovered and used to blackmail him in The Secret of Father Brown.
The death of Sir Aaron Armstrong by a fall from a chandelier, whether suicidal or accidental, after his daughter hacked the rope that held him in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The serial drugging and defrauding of barmaids and shop-girls by the criminal known as Drugger Davis, who poisoned his victims with cigarettes or chocolate in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The drugging of the police constable Florian by a harlequin performer amid crowd laughter, allowing a criminal to escape in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The theft of Mrs. Bankes's emerald necklace, discovered missing during the events at the hamlet in The Man with Two Beards (The Secret of Father Brown).
The act of espionage involving a forged document appearing to implicate Dr. Hirsch in treason against France, the central crime in The Duel of Dr Hirsch (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
The elopement of Etta Todd, the millionaire's daughter, with a convict she had helped to escape during a freak dinner she arranged at Pilgrim's Pond in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Dan Levy's Moneylending Extortion is the scheme of merciless financial predation driving the plot of Mr. Justice Raffles, in which Raffles takes extrajudicial action to punish Levy's victims.
The accumulated historical crimes of the Eyre family over generations, including hanging a father, scourging a wife through a village, and burning a church in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Flambeau's twenty-year career of theft, conducted with his own two hands before his conversion to the life of a private detective, described in The Secret of Father Brown.
The murder of Gallup, one of a series of killings including Stein and old man Merton that have alarmed the community in The Secret of Father Brown.

The Garrideb Counterfeiting Scheme is the plan in The Adventure of the Three Garridebs where John Garrideb (Killer Evans) invents the pretext of finding fellow Garridebs to clear the premises at 136 Little Ryder Street so he can access Prescott's hidden counterfeiting press.
An unnamed crime committed by Giles Carstairs in Australia for which his brother blackmailed him from afar in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The theft of Smart's exotic goldfish from their Venetian glass bowl, which apparently vanished as though the bowl had exploded like a bomb in The Secret of Father Brown.
The desecration of Lord Glengyle's grave and removal of his skull by a member of the family obsessed with collecting gold objects stripped of their settings in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The embezzlement of funds from the Hull and Huddersfield Bank by Samuel Harrogate, for which he is publicly arrested in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The suicidal leap of Samuel Harrogate from a high Alpine pass, mirroring his earlier dive from a coach, after his arrest for embezzlement in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The identity fraud by which the military spy Colonel Dubosc impersonated Dr. Hirsch at the latter's Paris house, engineering a treason scandal against an innocent man in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The long-running identity fraud by which Maurice Mair impersonated the dead James Mair as the Marquis of Marne, living as a recluse to conceal his crime in The Secret of Father Brown.
The impersonation of the dead Sir Roger Musgrave by his son, who killed his father, hid the body in armour and then in the moat, and assumed the baronetcy in The Secret of Father Brown.
The financial fraud and feudal usurpation by which Isaac Green exploited old legends to extort thousands from poor English people while posing as a mysterious chieftain in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

Jack Poisoning Baby Ferguson with Curare is the crime in The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire in which Jack, the crippled elder son, introduces curare poison into his infant half-brother using a sharp instrument tipped with the toxin.
The murder of Judge Gwynne, the latest in a string of killings investigated by Father Brown in The Secret of Father Brown.

The Rescue/Kidnapping of Faustina is the crime in The Fate of Faustina from Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman, an abduction in Italy that Raffles and Bunny become entangled in resolving.
The killing of Ludwig Arnhold sword in hand during Prince Otto's capture of the city of Heiligwaldenstein, the one genuine heroic death in the conflict in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

The Concealment of Lady Beatrice's Death is the crime in The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place in which Sir Robert Norberton carries his dead sister's body first to a well-house and then to the church crypt to hide her death until after the race.

Maria Gibson Suicide / Frame-Up of Grace Dunbar is the case in The Problem of Thor Bridge in which Maria Gibson, driven by jealousy, shoots herself on the bridge having arranged the evidence to frame her governess Grace Dunbar for murder.
The murder of old man Merton, one of a series of killings in the same community investigated in The Secret of Father Brown.
The Scowrers' systematic blackmail and terror campaign against mining companies and officials in the Vermissa Valley, the central criminal conspiracy investigated in The Valley of Fear.
The murder of Michael Moonshine, whose small wiry body with its false red beard pushed upward is found by Father Brown in The Man with Two Beards (The Secret of Father Brown).
The Moonshine Murder, considered by the police the most remarkable triumph in the history of detection after Father Brown identifies the true perpetrator in The Secret of Father Brown.

Moriarty's Conspiracy to Murder Douglas is the closing revelation of The Valley of Fear, in which Holmes deduces from the style of the crime that Professor Moriarty arranged for Ted Baldwin to kill John Douglas in England.
The murder of the actress Aurora Rome, who is found dead in her green and gold robes at the foot of the stage with her dead face turned upward in The Man in the Passage (The Wisdom of Father Brown).

The Murder of Angus Baird is the killing Raffles and Bunny discover upon entering Baird's house in Wilful Murder from The Amateur Cracksman, implicating them in a dangerous situation with a blackmailer.
The murder of the millionaire Julius K. Brayne by Valentin, who resolved to eliminate him to prevent Brayne from converting to Catholicism in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The murder of Colonel Norman Bohun by his brother Wilfred, who dropped a hammer from the church belfry onto his dissolute sibling below in The Hammer of God (The Innocence of Father Brown).
The murder of Sir Humphrey Gwynne, which Father Brown investigates at the start of The Mirror of the Magistrate in The Secret of Father Brown.
The murder of the hotel proprietor whose body is found drifting at sea with his left eye gouged out and his right eye closed in peace in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The murder of James Mair by his false friend Maurice, who feigned being shot in a duel and then turned and fired on the remorseful James as he bent over him in The Secret of Father Brown.
The murder of Leonard Quinton by Dr. Valentin Kidd, who stabbed the drugged man with an Oriental dagger while Quinton appeared to be writing a suicide note in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The murder of Major Murray by General St. Clare, who plunged his sabre through the major's body to silence him after the Black River treachery in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The murder of the theatrical manager Mundon Mandeville, found face down with blood spreading from under him in his backstage passage in The Secret of Father Brown.
The murder of Achmet, committed during the Indian Mutiny of 1857 in which the merchant Achmet was stabbed twice by one of the four conspirators while carrying the Agra Treasure for safekeeping.

The murder of Bartholomew Sholto at Pondicherry Lodge, committed by Tonga who shot a poisoned thorn through a blowpipe into the victim while Jonathan Small looked on, as Holmes and Watson deduced from the evidence.
The murder of old Antonelli by Prince Paul Saradine, who drove him to a lonely Sicilian pass and flung him off a cliff to steal his wife, as revealed in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The murder of Pauline Stacey by the cult leader Kalon, who deliberately withheld the lift and let her fall to her death in The Eye of Apollo in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The murder of Sir Charles Baskerville, who was lured to the Yew Alley at night by a letter arranged by Mrs. Laura Lyons at Stapleton's instigation, where he died of fright from the spectral hound.
The invisible murder of the millionaire inventor Isidore Smythe, drowned in a canal below Hampstead in such a way that both murderer and victim seemed to vanish in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The murder of Captain Stephen Saradine in a riverside duel by the avenging Antonelli, who ran the Captain through from behind as they fought in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The murder of a man beneath the Seawood bandstand, stabbed through the heart with a long gold pin bearing a monkey head in The God of the Gongs (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
The murder of Sir Arthur Vaudrey in the barber's chair, his throat cut with a razor by Dalmon, who then concealed the body in The Secret of Father Brown.
The murder of a prison warder hurled from the high north wall of the Sequah Convict Settlement, his brains dashed out on the rocks below in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The theft from the British Museum engineered by Raffles in the Further Adventures, one of his bolder crimes exploiting his social respectability to access the museum's collections.
The killing of three policemen with a bare left fist by the fugitive Nigger Ned after he was overtaken a mile from the Sequah prison, referenced in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The suicide of the original dispossessed duke, who shot himself and died without heirs, leaving his title to be usurped by Isaac Green in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The murder of Prince Otto of Grossenmark, shot by his own sentries acting on forged orders, gagged with his own military sash, in The Fairy Tale of Father Brown (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
The murder of his wife by old actor Parkinson with a stage spear during a family quarrel, after which Parkinson died of grief, referenced in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The patricide by Captain Musgrave, who killed his own father and concealed the body in the castle moat — called the worst crime in the world — in The Secret of Father Brown.
The treason of Paul Arnhold, who surrendered the city of Heiligwaldenstein to its invaders by betrayal, abandoning his brothers' cause in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

The systematic murder of Pendragon heirs over generations by a Spanish avenger, who exploited the burning tower and a false lighthouse signal to lure their ships to wreck in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The attempted poisoning of Colonel Cray by Major Putnam, who substituted an Indian poison in a dining vessel and was undone when Father Brown administered an emetic in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The theft of the Red Moon of Meru ruby by Tommy Hunter from Lord Mounteagle's museum, later recovered and restored by Father Brown's persuasion in The Secret of Father Brown.
The murder of circus owner Ronder by his wife and strongman Leonardo, committed using a lion's cage in Abbots Parva, the dark secret behind the horribly disfigured Mrs. Ronder's veiled existence.

The deliberate sabotage of Pauline Stacey's fountain pen by her sister Joan, who left it unfilled so Pauline could not complete the will she was writing before her death in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Sacrilege and Church Robbery is the crime Raffles commits in The Spoils of Sacrilege from A Thief in the Night, breaking into a church to steal its valuables.
The blackmail of Prince Paul Saradine by Captain Stephen Saradine, who had discovered the truth of the Sicilian murder and extorted his brother over it in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The summary execution of General St. Clare by his own English soldiers, who hanged him from a tree after discovering his treason against the Crown in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The treason of General St. Clare, who sold military secrets to Brazil in exchange for money to cover his debts, leading to the Black River massacre in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The staged burglary at Major Putnam's villa, in which silver including a cruet-stand was hidden in the dustbin as a blind to distract from a poison plot in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The staged duel between Maurice and James Mair, in which Maurice feigned being shot and then coldly shot his fallen friend while James bent over him in The Secret of Father Brown.
The murder of Stein, one of the series of killings in the same community, referenced alongside Gallup and Merton in The Secret of Father Brown.
The suicide of Aristide Valentin, who took pills at his own table and died with an expression of pride greater than Cato's on his face in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Sutton Mystery, a cold case involving a man found strangled near the Epsom Grand Stand, recalled as a baffling precedent in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The theft of Father Brown's sapphire cross by Flambeau, who exploited a train journey and disguise to separate the priest from his valuable relic in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The theft of the Emperor's Pearl, a celebrated Japanese jewel purloined by Raffles from its owner during a social engagement, as recounted in The Gift of the Emperor.
The theft of the three Flying Stars diamonds by Flambeau during a Christmas charade, recovered and restored by Father Brown's intervention in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The theft of a valuable necklace at a cricket weekend, one of Raffles's earliest recorded burglaries in which he exploits his standing as a celebrated cricketer to gain access to a country house.
The theft of the Twelve True Fishermen's celebrated silver fish-knives and forks from the Vernon Hotel, solved by Father Brown in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The theft of a Velasquez painting by Raffles, carried out as part of his scheme involving a society dinner and the exploitation of his access to a gentleman's private collection.
Threatening letters sent to the inventor Isidore Smythe and his secretary Laura Hope, received five times in a fortnight, preceding the Invisible Man murder in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Three earlier attempts on Colonel Cray's life using a razor, a gas-filled room, and an Australian boomerang, made before the poison plot foiled by Father Brown in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
An apparent assault and illegal confinement of Mr. Todhunter, who turns out to have tied himself up as part of an escape-artist practice routine in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The wartime treason of Captain Musgrave's father, discovered by Russian blackmailers and used to extort him in the backstory of The Worst Crime in the World (The Secret of Father Brown).
The blackmail of Dalmon by Sir Arthur Vaudrey, who had discovered a past murder and exploited that knowledge until Dalmon silenced him in The Secret of Father Brown.

The Vitriol Attack on Baron Gruner is the dramatic conclusion of The Adventure of the Illustrious Client from The Case-Book, in which Kitty Winter throws acid in Baron Gruner's face as revenge for his past cruelties to women.
The concealment of Vaudrey's corpse by the barber Wicks, who found the body in his chair, despaired of clearing himself, and disposed of it in the river in The Secret of Father Brown.

Ichthyosis is the scaly skin disease caused by a germ diagnosed by Sir James Saunders in The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier, the correct diagnosis that exonerates Godfrey Emsworth from the feared suspicion of leprosy.
George Devil-Doris, an assumed persona adopted wholesale by Raffles in one of his disguises in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman.
A Norwegian professor persona assumed by Raffles to gain access to a target's house in A Costume Piece (The Amateur Cracksman).
Stapleton's cover identity as a butterfly-hunting naturalist on Dartmoor, used to conceal his true identity and murderous scheme in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
Stapleton's earlier alias 'Vandeleur,' under which he ran a private school in Yorkshire before fleeing to Devonshire and reinventing himself as a naturalist in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
Raffles Enlists in Boer War is the event in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles and Bunny join the Imperial Yeomanry and sail to South Africa.

The Arrest of Arthur Charpentier is the event in A Study in Scarlet in which Inspector Tobias Gregson arrests Sub-Lieutenant Arthur Charpentier as the prime suspect in Drebber's murder before Holmes reveals the true killer.
The murders at Crow Hill Mine, one of the series of violent crimes committed by the Scowrers against mine operators and workers in the Vermissa Valley, documented in The Valley of Fear.

The Death of Bartholomew Sholto is the discovery of his poisoned body at Pondicherry Lodge in The Sign of the Four, the event that launches Holmes and Watson into the chase for Jonathan Small and Tonga.
The death of Selden the escaped convict on the moor, found face-down with features convulsed, a turning-point in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
The death of Sir Charles Baskerville, who collapsed at the gate of the Yew Alley after waiting there at night, the crime at the heart of The Hound of the Baskervilles.

The Death of John Douglas Overboard is the epilogue event in The Valley of Fear in which Watson receives word that Douglas was lost overboard in a gale off St. Helena, revealing that Moriarty's agents caught up with him.
The Eucharistic Congress, a major Catholic gathering in London that Flambeau exploits for cover and confusion during the events of The Blue Cross in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The near-execution of Gilbert, who mounted the guillotine scaffold while Lupin raced to intervene and Prasville arrived at the last moment to stop the execution in The Crystal Stopper.

The Ferriers' Flight from Salt Lake City is the event in A Study in Scarlet in which Jefferson Hope guides John Ferrier and Lucy by night through mountain defiles to escape Mormon territory before Ferrier is overtaken and killed.
The arrest of Jefferson Hope, the dramatic capture of the American avenger of the murdered Lucy Ferrier on Enoch Drebber's cab in Baker Street, achieved with Holmes's assistance.
The death of Jefferson Hope in custody, who died peacefully overnight of a burst aortic aneurism the night before he could be tried, in A Study in Scarlet.

Jefferson Hope's pursuit of vengeance, the years-long chase across two continents in which Hope tracked Drebber and Stangerson through Europe and America to avenge the deaths of John and Lucy Ferrier before cornering them in London.
The legend of Hugo Baskerville, an ancestor whose cruelty unleashed a spectral hound that tore out his throat, founding the Baskerville curse in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
The Indian Mutiny of 1857, the historical uprising during which Jonathan Small and his confederates committed the murder of Achmet and seized the Agra treasure in The Sign of the Four.

The Murder of John Ferrier is the event in A Study in Scarlet in which John Ferrier is found dead with a white face and no wound, killed by the Danite Band for refusing to give Lucy to Drebber or Stangerson.
Queen Victoria's Diamond Jubilee of 1897, during which Raffles exploits the festivities as cover for a nocturnal theft from the British Museum in Raffles: Further Adventures.

The Killing of the Hound is the climax in The Hound of the Baskervilles in which Holmes and Watson fire ten shots between them to bring down Stapleton's phosphorescent hound as it attacks Sir Henry on the Dartmoor moor.
The Lodge initiation of John McMurdo (Birdy Edwards's alias) into the Vermissa Valley chapter of the Scowrers, a secret society modelled on the Molly Maguires, in The Valley of Fear.

Lucy Ferrier's Forced Marriage to Drebber is the event in A Study in Scarlet in which Lucy is led to the altar of the Endowment House a month after returning to Salt Lake City, forced by Mormon law to marry Enoch Drebber against her will.

The Death of Lucy Ferrier is the event in A Study in Scarlet in which Lucy, forced into marriage with Enoch Drebber, fades and dies within a month of the marriage, her brutal husband not disguising his satisfaction at her end.

The Mormon Migration to Utah is the historical backstory in A Study in Scarlet in which the Latter-day Saints, driven from Nauvoo on the Mississippi, make their great westward journey and build their city in the desert.
The occasion on which Holmes and Watson move their joint belongings to 221B Baker Street after agreeing to share lodgings, establishing their famous address in A Study in Scarlet.

The Death of Raffles at Spion Kop is the event in The Knees of the Gods from Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman in which A. J. Raffles is killed in action during the Boer War battle at Spion Kop, South Africa.

The Trial of the Scowrers, the legal proceeding in The Valley of Fear at which Boss McGinty and other leaders of the criminal fraternity in Vermissa Valley were convicted, with McGinty meeting his fate on the scaffold.
The second arrest of Arsène Lupin, made by Ganimard under the alias Félix Davey, a key turning point in The Blonde Lady.

The Beating of Editor James Stanger is the Scowrer act of intimidation in Part II, Chapter III of The Valley of Fear in which the editor of the Vermissa Herald is assaulted for his editorial opposition to the lodge.
Stapleton's disappearance into the treacherous Grimpen Mire on the night the hound is destroyed, leaving no body and only fading footprints as evidence in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
The unmasking and attempted trapping of Birdy Edwards (Pinkerton's undercover detective), which he turns the tables on, exposing and destroying the Scowrers in The Valley of Fear.
The first meeting of Dr. Watson and Sherlock Holmes at St. Bartholomew's Hospital, arranged by Stamford, marking the beginning of their celebrated partnership at 221B Baker Street.
A pocket edition of Boccaccio's Decameron bearing Stangerson's name on the fly-leaf, found in the victim's pocket and used to identify him in A Study in Scarlet.
The changed footstep pattern of Sir Charles Baskerville — switching from a walk to a desperate run — which Holmes reads as proof of terror before death in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
A telegram from the head of the Cleveland police organisation that confirms the identity and background of Enoch Drebber, a key piece of evidence in A Study in Scarlet.
Enoch J. Drebber's visiting cards, printed in gilt letters with his Cleveland, Ohio address, used by Holmes to establish the victim's identity in A Study in Scarlet.
Giant hound footprints found twenty yards from Sir Charles's body, noticed only by Holmes and pointing to the supernatural menace in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
The strong smell of fresh paint in Josiah Amberley's house, identified by Holmes as a cover for the odour of gas used to murder two victims in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman.
A small ointment box containing two pills, found in Stangerson's room; Holmes proves one pill is harmless and the other deadly in A Study in Scarlet.
Two identical pills offered by the murderer Jefferson Hope to his captive victim, one deadly and one harmless, leaving the outcome to chance in A Study in Scarlet.

The Word RACHE Written in Blood is the cryptic clue found on the wall at 3 Lauriston Gardens in A Study in Scarlet — traced in blood-red letters, it is Jefferson Hope's intentional misdirection spelling the German word for revenge.
Raffles's burglar tools, preserved and displayed in the Black Museum at Scotland Yard as relics of the famous criminal in Raffles: A Thief in the Night.
Trichinopoly cigar ash scattered across the floor at the murder scene, used by Holmes to characterise the murderer in A Study in Scarlet.
Two unused seats (B30 and B32) at the Haymarket Theatre on the night of the murders, proving Amberley's alibi was fabricated in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman.
An unfinished message — 'We we—' — scrawled in purple indelible pencil on the gas-chamber wall by a dying victim, found by Holmes in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman.
A plain gold wedding ring found in the Brixton Road and advertised in the newspapers, used by Holmes as a lure in A Study in Scarlet.
The distinctive white Jessamine scent on a cigarette stub, identified by Holmes as conclusive evidence of Stapleton's identity in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
The revealed fact that 'Miss' Beryl Stapleton is actually Stapleton's wife, born Beryl Garcia in Costa Rica, discovered by Holmes in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

Holmes's deduction, from the height of the writing on the wall and the absence of wounds on the victim, that the blood at the Lauriston Gardens murder scene came from the killer's own nose in A Study in Scarlet.

The revelation in The Valley of Fear that the body found at Birlstone was not John Douglas but Ted Baldwin, whose corpse Douglas substituted for his own to fake his death and escape Moriarty's agents.

Bunny and Raffles Were School Friends is the biographical fact established in The Ides of March from The Amateur Cracksman that explains the deep trust and loyalty that draws Bunny into partnership with Raffles.

John Douglas is Birdy Edwards (Pinkerton Detective) is the crucial revelation of The Valley of Fear: the supposed murder victim at Birlstone Manor was in fact Pinkerton undercover agent Birdy Edwards who faked his death to escape the Scowrers.
The established fact that Enoch Drebber was poisoned, not physically attacked, with the same deadly pills that Jefferson Hope also carried in A Study in Scarlet.
Holmes's own description of himself as the world's only consulting detective, to whom other detectives bring their most difficult cases, first stated in A Study in Scarlet.

The fact that Holmes is the anonymous author of the magazine article 'The Book of Life,' which argues that all knowledge of a person can be deduced from observation, established in A Study in Scarlet.
The medical fact, confirmed at Jefferson Hope's examination, that he suffers from a potentially fatal aortic aneurism caused by exposure and hunger in the Salt Lake Mountains, in A Study in Scarlet.

The established fact that the Baskerville hound is a genuine giant dog painted with phosphorescent material to create its terrifying supernatural appearance, revealed in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
Holmes's identification of the initials 'L.L.' on a note found among Sir Charles's papers as belonging to Laura Lyons, a key link in the Baskerville murder chain.
Holmes's deduction that Professor Moriarty arranged for John Douglas to be killed overboard at sea, the dark epilogue to The Valley of Fear.
Holmes's explanation that the word 'RACHE' written in blood at the murder scene means 'revenge' in German, correcting Lestrade's theory about an incomplete name in A Study in Scarlet.
The established fact that A.J. Raffles lives in the Albany, Piccadilly, one of London's most prestigious bachelor residences, first stated in The Ides of March.
Raffles's admission that his very first burglary was committed in Australia, establishing his career's origins in Le Premier Pas (The Amateur Cracksman).
The established fact that Raffles plays cricket for England, giving him social cover and public respect in The Amateur Cracksman.
The established fact that Joseph Stangerson was stabbed through the heart at Halliday's Private Hotel, the second murder in A Study in Scarlet.

Holmes's discovery that Stapleton is the son of Rodger Baskerville, younger brother of Sir Charles, making him the true heir who fabricated the hound legend to murder his way to the estate in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
The phrase 'Valley of Fear' explained by Mrs. Douglas as referring to the terror of life in Vermissa Valley under the Scowrers, which haunted her husband for years in The Valley of Fear.

V.V. 341 = Vermissa Valley Lodge 341 is the fact Holmes deduces in Part I, Chapter VII of The Valley of Fear, decoding the cryptic card found at Birlstone to identify the Scowrer lodge whose members carried out the crime.
Holmes's famous first deduction, that Watson has served as an army doctor in Afghanistan, stated upon their very first meeting in A Study in Scarlet.
A fake telegram sent by Holmes to lure Josiah Amberley to an impossibly remote village, creating a window of time to search the murder house unimpeded in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman.
Baritsu, the Japanese system of wrestling that Holmes used to overpower Moriarty at the Reichenbach Fall in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
Holmes's deduction from beeswing sediment found in only one of two glasses, proving only one person had been drinking, in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
Thorndyke's technique of analysing the defibrination of a blood drop on a non-absorbent surface to determine whether it came from a living body, in The Red Thumb Mark.

The forensic method of concealing a murder by placing Arthur Cadogan West's body on the roof of an Underground train carriage, allowing it to fall onto the tracks at a junction in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans.

Body Substitution (Douglas switches with Baldwin) is the forensic method in The Valley of Fear by which John Douglas kills Ted Baldwin in self-defence and swaps their identities — presenting Baldwin's destroyed face as his own — to fake his death and escape the Scowrers.
Thorndyke's analysis of separated ligamentous bone junctions and the absence of adipocere to establish time and manner of death in The Vanishing Man.

The Book Cipher (Whitaker's Almanac) is the decryption method Holmes applies to Porlock's encoded warning in The Valley of Fear, realising the numbers refer to page, line, and word positions in Whitaker's Almanac.
Thorndyke's examination of ballast grit embedded in a boot's welt and grain to prove that a man had walked two hundred yards along a railway track in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke.
Jefferson Hope's method of becoming a London cabman to shadow Drebber and Stangerson undetected across their hotels and haunts in A Study in Scarlet.
Chloroform is the anaesthetic used by Holy Peters and his wife to render Lady Frances Carfax unconscious before placing her in a coffin in The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax.
The forensic method of burning powdered Devil's Foot root to release a lethal psychotropic gas that causes madness and death, used in The Adventure of the Devil's Foot.

Cricket as Social Cover is the technique in Gentlemen and Players from The Amateur Cracksman by which Raffles exploits his celebrity as an amateur cricketer to gain access to houses and events he intends to rob.
The Dancing Men cipher, a substitution code using small stick figures that Holmes deciphers to solve a murder and prevent another in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
Thorndyke's identification of freshwater diatom species in a drowned victim's lung tissue to determine the location where the drowning occurred, in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke.
Disguise and Impersonation is Raffles's primary technique in A Costume Piece from The Amateur Cracksman, where he uses convincing stage disguise to approach and burglarise Rosenthall's house.
Holmes's disguise as Dr. Hill Barton, an expert on Chinese pottery sent by Watson to gain access to the villain's collection in The Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes.
Thorndyke's identification of a warehouse district from rice-starch, wheat-starch, and spice dust found on a suspect's clothing, placing him in Dockhead in The Vanishing Man.
Thorndyke's demonstration that a thumb-print can be forged using a chromicized gelatine stamp from a photographed genuine print, the central forensic argument in The Red Thumb Mark.
Thorndyke's technique of filling footprints with dry plaster cast to capture exact impressions for comparison, used to trace a killer in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Thorndyke's identification of a Levant-specific Foraminifera species in deep-sea sand found on a victim's shoe, placing her in the Eastern Mediterranean before her death in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Thorndyke's cross-section microscopy of individual hairs, establishing circular section as characteristic of Mongolian ancestry in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Lock Picking is the craft deployed by Raffles in The Ides of March from The Amateur Cracksman and other stories, his professional tools enabling him to pick any lock quickly and silently.
Holmes's stratagem of feigning a fatal tropical illness to lure the murderer Culverton Smith into a gloating confession in The Adventure of the Dying Detective.
The Marsh test for arsenic, applied by Thorndyke to a submitted specimen to confirm the presence of arsenic in a very considerable quantity in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Thorndyke's extraction of over one grain of morphia from stomach contents, presented as expert testimony before a magistrate in John Thorndyke's Cases.

The Old Woman Disguise is Jefferson Hope's impersonation of a woman named Mrs Sawyer in A Study in Scarlet, used to collect the wedding-ring trap Holmes set, allowing Hope to slip past Baker Street before being identified.

Phosphorus Coating of the Hound is Stapleton's technique in The Hound of the Baskervilles of painting his hound's muzzle, hackles, and dewlap with phosphorus to create the fire-breathing supernatural appearance that terrifies the Baskervilles.

Murder by Poison Pills is Jefferson Hope's technique in A Study in Scarlet of forcing his victim Enoch Drebber to choose between two pills — one lethal, one harmless — making the death appear to result from chance rather than murder.

The RACHE Misdirection is the false clue Jefferson Hope inscribes in blood on the wall at 3 Lauriston Gardens in A Study in Scarlet, intended to send investigators chasing a German revenge motive rather than the true killer.

The ingenious suicide method in which a revolver tied to a weighted string is fired across a bridge parapet, the recoil flinging the gun into the water and leaving no weapon at the scene in The Problem of Thor Bridge.

Scent Tracking via Stolen Boot is Stapleton's technique in The Hound of the Baskervilles of providing his hound with Sir Henry Baskerville's scent by stealing one of the baronet's new boots from his London hotel.
Barrymore's candle signal at a darkened Baskerville Hall window, used to communicate with Selden the escaped convict hiding on the moor in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
Thorndyke's spectroscopic examination of a blood smear using a micro-attachment to the microscope, providing definitive identification of the substance in The Red Thumb Mark.
Holmes's technique of cutting words from a newspaper printed in a distinctive typeface to construct an anonymous warning message in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

Undercover Detective Work (McMurdo Persona) is the method Birdy Edwards of Pinkerton's uses in The Valley of Fear, infiltrating the Scowrers under the alias Jack McMurdo and eventually exposing the entire lodge.
Shinwell Johnson, Holmes's agent planted in the criminal underworld to gather intelligence on dangerous figures in the lower classes, referenced in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client.
Voodoo rituals practised at Wisteria Lodge by the cook as part of secret religious observance linked to the household's South American origins in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge.
Thorndyke's technique of wetting a surface script to reveal a hidden Roman-capital message written in water-sensitive ink beneath the visible Chinese ink text in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Thorndyke and Polton's portable X-ray apparatus used to examine a mummy case without opening it, revealing a gold dental crown and positively identifying the body in The Vanishing Man.
Financial desperation, the motive that drives Bunny Manders to accept Raffles's proposal of burglary when he is at his wits' end for money in The Ides of March.
Laura Lyons's desire for a divorce and remarriage, exploited by Stapleton who promised to wed her if she lured Sir Charles to his death at the Yew Alley gate in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

Inheritance of Baskerville Estate is Jack Stapleton's motive in The Hound of the Baskervilles: as the concealed son of Rodger Baskerville, he plans to kill Sir Charles and Sir Henry to claim the estate as the last surviving heir.
A personal sense of justice, the motive that drives Raffles to act as self-appointed judge and jury in Mr. Justice Raffles.

Revenge for John Ferrier is one of the two motives driving Jefferson Hope's killings in A Study in Scarlet, cited when Holmes explains that the Danite Band's murder of Ferrier was planned by Drebber and Stangerson.

Revenge for Lucy Ferrier is Jefferson Hope's primary motive in A Study in Scarlet, his vow sworn over Lucy's coffin that his last thought and last prayer before dying would be for the deaths of Drebber and Stangerson.
The thrill and sporting spirit of the chase, Raffles's stated primary motive for continuing his career as a gentleman burglar in Le Premier Pas (The Amateur Cracksman).
The antihero role occupied by Raffles, who is simultaneously an admired gentleman and a practised criminal throughout The Amateur Cracksman.
The narrator role occupied by Harry 'Bunny' Manders, who tells all the Raffles stories from his position as accomplice and admiring friend in The Amateur Cracksman.
A short sabre like a navy cutlass but twice the size, carried by the Admiral as his personal weapon in The Perishing of the Pendragons (The Wisdom of Father Brown).

The Agra Treasure, a chest containing over one hundred and forty-three diamonds of the first water and other jewels valued at no less than half a million sterling in The Sign of the Four, lost to the Thames when Jonathan Small jettisoned it.
The Von Herder air gun, a custom-built silent weapon with a curiously misshapen butt commissioned by Moriarty and used by Colonel Moran in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
An aluminium-hilted Mycenaean-style dagger with the word TRADITORE engraved on its guard and a blade of old foreign steel, the murder weapon in John Thorndyke's Cases.
The yellow dressing-gown with a vivid scarlet lining found on the body of old Sir Aaron Armstrong at the opening of The Hammer of God in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A horn-hilted knife with a red smear, produced by Father Brown and handed to the police sergeant during the investigation in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A scrap of rope found entangled on the corpse in the Quinton garden, apparently caught in a struggle in The Innocence of Father Brown.
An Australian boomerang used as a war-club, identified by Father Brown as the only weapon that can be thrown from a window, curve in the air, and return, in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A loaded automatic pistol found by Father Brown in a library desk drawer and used during the climax of a case in The Secret of Father Brown.
Ted Baldwin's disguise clothes, recovered from the moat at Birlstone Manor, which explain how the body was misidentified and John Douglas faked his own death in The Valley of Fear.
A senseless stick of bamboo with a splintered top, found near a crime scene and examined for clues in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The barber's razor laid down by Wicks just before Dalmon used it to cut Vaudrey's throat in the barber's chair in The Secret of Father Brown.
The 1742 Baskerville family manuscript delivered by Dr. Mortimer, which recounts the curse of Hugo Baskerville and provides historical context for the case in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
Two pairs of crossed battle-axes hung on the walls of the Musgrave Castle fireplace, part of the ancestral arms decorating the hall in The Secret of Father Brown.
A row of sunny beehives at the back of the garden, tended by Mr. Smith the supposed beekeeper who introduces himself to Father Brown in The Secret of Father Brown.

John Bellingham's large blue-glazed Egyptian scarab, which he always carried on his person and which was planted by Jellicoe to create confusion about the identification of remains in The Vanishing Man.
The Beryl Coronet, one of the most precious public possessions of the Empire, set with thirty-nine enormous beryls, stolen and then recovered by Holmes in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes.
A big locked box in the corner of a room, from which something emerges each night according to a strange report in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A black hood worn by Maurice Mair to resemble his brother James as a penitent monk, a disguise that conceals his identity during the long impersonation in The Secret of Father Brown.
The Black Pearl, a jewel stolen from the Countess Zalti's apartment by Victor Danègre and subsequently recovered by Arsène Lupin in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
The Black Pearl of the Borgias, one of the most precious stones in existence, stolen from the Countess of Morcar and recovered by Holmes in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
A second-hand Blickensderfer typewriter with a distinctive broken loop on the small letter 'e', whose characteristic defect allows Thorndyke to trace a forged label in The Red Thumb Mark.
The Bloody Thumb, a penny dreadful that John Boulnois was reading at home on the very evening Sir Claude Champion was killed in The Strange Crime of John Boulnois (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
The Blowpipe is Tonga's weapon in The Sign of the Four, a short round piece of wood clapped to his lips to propel the poison thorn that kills Bartholomew Sholto through his locked window.
The Blue Carbuncle, an intensely blue gemstone smaller than a bean, found inside a Christmas goose crop and traced by Holmes to its rightful owner in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes.

The Blue Cross, a silver reliquary cross set with sapphires and considerable relic value, carried by Father Brown from Essex to a London conference and stolen then recovered from Flambeau in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A length of old blue curtain wrapped round Jameson's head as an improvised turban for his disguise during the events in The Secret of Father Brown.
The Blue Diamond, the jewel stolen by Lupin's blonde accomplice that Ganimard strives to recover in The Blonde Lady (Arsène Lupin).
Blue spectacles worn by the clean-shaven Colonel Dubosc as part of his disguise when impersonating Dr. Hirsch in The Duel of Dr Hirsch (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
Colonel Norman Bohun's round hat lined with steel, originally a Japanese or Chinese helmet that Wilfred recognises at the crime scene in The Hammer of God (The Innocence of Father Brown).

The 'Book of Life' Article is Holmes's essay with the ambitious title arguing that an observant man can learn a great deal from a brief encounter, first discussed in Part I, Chapter II of A Study in Scarlet when Watson reads it in a magazine.
A large brass telescope on a tall tripod tilted towards the river, set up by the Admiral outside the tower for watching for ships in The Perishing of the Pendragons (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
The carbines of the four brigands who cover the coach party from a high ridge, used to enforce the ransom demand in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A big looking-glass smashed during the violent struggle in the room, found alongside an overturned palm tree in The Secret of Father Brown.
A bronze Roman coin bearing the profile of Caesar, whose colour and exact curve of the nose make it the object of Christabel's sentimental theft in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
An old bronze lectern on a small rock platform in the hermitage, bearing a great German Bible, in The Fairy Tale of Father Brown (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
Father Brown's several brown paper parcels, which he is quite incapable of keeping together, referenced in The Blue Cross in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Brown-paper parcels concealing modern English coins rather than ancient Roman ones, part of the Glengyle gold mania plot in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Bruce-Partington submarine plans, top-secret government documents stolen from Woolwich Arsenal and used as bait for a spy ring in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans.
The bullet that killed Prince Otto of Grossenmark, fired by his own sentries on forged orders from Colonel Dubosc, shattering his temple and jaw in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A cardboard box containing two freshly severed human ears sent anonymously to a woman in Croydon, the shocking opening clue in The Adventure of the Cardboard Box (Sherlock Holmes).
The famous Carstairs Collection of Roman coins assembled by Colonel Carstairs, inherited and then endangered by his sons in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Three worn carved symbols on the Pendragon tower doorbeam — a tower, a ship, and a half-man — depicting the legend of the Spanish curse in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A flashing naked cavalry sabre streaked with blood at the point, produced by O'Brien during the confrontation in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A small bottle of Chablis and a plate of almonds and raisins set on a round table outside a hotel, observed by Father Brown as a significant clue in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The great chest of silver plate, the object at the centre of the crime in The Chest of Silver (Raffles: A Thief in the Night).
Chloroform, identified by Father Brown as the means by which Leonard Quinton was rendered unconscious before being murdered in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Tall old-fashioned wine glasses and churchwarden pipes, the old-fashioned drinking paraphernalia that gives Father Brown an initial impression in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Porlock's Cipher Message is the encoded letter sent to Holmes by the informer Fred Porlock in The Valley of Fear, warning of danger at Birlstone and decoded by Holmes using Whitaker's Almanac.
The cocaine bottle kept by Holmes for his seven-per-cent solution, a recurring symbol of his restless and self-destructive mental habits first mentioned in The Sign of the Four.
Colonel Adams's sword hung over the fireplace in his entrance hall, a domestic trophy marking his military career in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Coloured electric lamps strung in the garden, powered by a small plant of electricity from the bungalow, used for garden illumination in The Secret of Father Brown.
A conjuring handbill printed in blue and red advertising a performance, produced from a pocket during a case in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
