Prescott's counterfeiting press, hidden beneath the floor of Nathan Garrideb's house and the true target of the criminal scheme in The Adventure of the Three Garridebs.
Colonel Cray's service revolver, which the long, lean, athletic figure of Cray is seen brandishing when Father Brown first encounters him in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Thorndyke and Polton's portable Crookes tube X-ray apparatus, used to take a skiagraph of a sealed mummy case at the museum in The Vanishing Man.
The missing Crown of Charles I, a small golden coronal recovered from its hiding place by Holmes in The Musgrave Ritual in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.
The silver cruet-stand concealed in a dustbin as part of a staged burglary blind, recovered by Father Brown to expose a poison plot in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The crystal stopper from a liqueur set, inside which Daubrecq has hidden a list of twenty-seven corrupt officials, the object Lupin relentlessly pursues in The Crystal Stopper.
Curare-tipped arrows brought from South America by Mrs. Ferguson, one of which is used to poison the baby, the key evidence in The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire.
A Cyanea capillata, the Lion's Mane jellyfish of extraordinary size whose sting causes the horrifying death that mystifies Holmes in The Adventure of the Lion's Mane.
Cigarettes concealing two potassium cyanide tabloids a quarter-inch from the end, with gold tips and loose wrappers, used as a means of murder in The Vanishing Man.
A straight, long knife or short sword with an ornamental pictured handle, wielded in a menacing confrontation in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A decanter smashed in dead silence onto the bald head of Isaac Green by the Duke, a sudden violent act of justice in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A sample of Eastern Mediterranean deep-sea sand bearing Levant-specific Foraminifera, found on a dead woman's boot and used by Thorndyke to trace her last voyage in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Old Catholic missals curiously cut about and defaced, stripped of their gilt and ornamental gold by the obsessive Lord Glengyle in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Devil's Foot root (Radix pedis diaboli), a rare and deadly West African poison that causes madness and death when burned, central to The Adventure of the Devil's Foot.
Douglas Maberley's unpublished novel manuscript, which contains compromising revelations about Isadora Klein and must be suppressed, driving the plot of The Adventure of the Three Gables.
The pistol used in the staged duel between Maurice and James Mair, which Maurice kept and fired at point-blank range as James bent over the pretended fallen body in The Secret of Father Brown.
A bundle concealing Ted Baldwin's clothing, sunk in the Birlstone Manor moat by Barker and recovered by Holmes, proving the body-substitution scheme in The Valley of Fear.
The Dynamics of an Asteroid, a mathematics treatise of such ascent of thought that no scientific journal would review it, authored by Professor Moriarty, mentioned in The Valley of Fear.
An electric torch produced from his pocket by the American journalist Kidd to illuminate the dark Pendragon tower in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Mrs. Bankes's valuable emerald necklace, discussed openly at dinner as a precious possession before it is stolen during the night's events in The Secret of Father Brown.

Father Brown's fountain pen deliberately left unfilled by his sister Joan Stacey so that Pauline could not complete the will she was writing before her death in The Eye of Apollo (The Innocence of Father Brown).
A tall palm tree with long sweeping leaves fallen full length in its shattered dark red pot, part of the room wreckage found in The Secret of Father Brown.
A wax prosthetic nose forming part of a disguise, observed beginning to spot and warp in warm weather, betraying the actor Hugo Romaine in The Dagger with Wings (The Secret of Father Brown).
Rows of family portraits in dark colours lining the gallery walls of Musgrave Castle in The Worst Crime in the World (The Secret of Father Brown).
Father Brown's large shabby umbrella, constantly falling on the floor, his characteristic accessory that appears throughout The Innocence of Father Brown.
Fingerprints found half-way down the blade of a sword, indicating an unusual grip that points to how the murder of Aurora Rome was committed in The Man in the Passage (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
A specially constructed club with five sharp points mimicking a lion's claw, the true murder weapon disguised as an animal mauling in The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger.
Five dried orange pips sent in an envelope by the Ku Klux Klan as a death warning to Openshaw in The Five Orange Pips (The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes).
Flambeau's unseen children whose belated laughter is heard from the high dark castle above where Father Brown and Flambeau sit talking in The Secret of Father Brown.
A flat shining dagger dropped by the fleeing hotel proprietor on the Seawood esplanade, the intended murder weapon in The God of the Gongs (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
The three Flying Stars, great African diamonds displayed in a nest of orange velvet as a Christmas present, stolen by Flambeau in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A forged spy's note directing an agent to a grey envelope in the War Office Secretary's desk, used to frame Dr. Hirsch for treason in The Duel of Dr Hirsch (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
A garden hose wielded by Father Brown to fight the fire burning in the Pendragon wooden tower in The Perishing of the Pendragons (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
Twenty-four chromicized gelatine stamps on boxwood handles produced by Thorndyke to demonstrate how a fingerprint forgery can be manufactured in The Red Thumb Mark.
An enormous gilt effigy of a human eye surrounded by rays of gold, occupying the space of two or three windows in the Eye of Apollo cult offices in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The accumulated gold objects stripped of their settings by Lord Glengyle — rings, candlesticks, snuff-boxes, pencil-cases — as part of his gold collection mania in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The skull of Lord Glengyle, exhumed from its grave in the churchyard and kept by the family in their castle as part of their eccentric gold-hoarding obsession in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A very long gold pin with a carved monkey head, thrust through a man's heart while appearing to be merely fixed in his scarf, the murder weapon in The God of the Gongs (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
A pair of golden pince-nez spectacles clutched in the dead man's hand, the sole clue that leads Holmes to solve the mystery in The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez (The Return of Sherlock Holmes).
A huge bubble of beautifully blown Venetian glass housing gold fish with ruby eyes, apparently exploded like a bomb during a robbery in The Secret of Father Brown.
A gramophone placed in an adjacent room to simulate Holmes's voice in conversation, enabling him to move undetected while Count Sylvius incriminated himself in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone.
An ancient Greek Mycenaean bronze dagger of the Heroic epoch with a blade still sharp enough to cut in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Green evening coats worn by the Twelve True Fishermen as the distinctive insignia of their exclusive dining club in The Queer Feet (The Innocence of Father Brown).
A great dark green idol with its back turned, outlined against the dying light at the top of a room in Lord Mounteagle's Asian museum in The Secret of Father Brown.
Baron Gruner's brown leather diary of his past conquests, stolen by Holmes for Violet de Merville to read as proof of Gruner's true character in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client.

The Haemoglobin Reagent is the chemical test Holmes excitedly demonstrates at St. Bartholomew's at the opening of A Study in Scarlet, a reagent precipitated by haemoglobin and nothing else, revolutionising the detection of bloodstains.
A harlequin costume made from silver paper out of cigar boxes, worn by Flambeau in his disguise during the Christmas gathering in The Flying Stars (The Innocence of Father Brown).
Short-stalked wild flowers picked by a girl named Hedwig from the wood near Heiligwaldenstein castle, whose short stalks become a significant clue in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The legendary gold supposedly hidden in the rocks around Heiligwaldenstein, the prize that drove Prince Otto to invade the toy kingdom in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Hollow Needle, a sea-rock off the Étretat cliffs concealing the secret treasure-chamber of the Kings of France, the prize at the centre of The Hollow Needle (Arsène Lupin).
Holmes's violin, played carelessly but brilliantly in long nocturnal sessions as a stimulus for thought or a relief from boredom, first described in A Study in Scarlet.
A pair of old heavy horn-rimmed spectacles found beside a body in The Worst Crime in the World (The Secret of Father Brown).
The Hornby rough diamonds, an unusually large parcel of exceptional-value stones stolen from St. Mary Axe, whose theft triggers the wrongful prosecution in The Red Thumb Mark.
Tommy Hunter's glove concealing a hand painted brown to pass as a dark-skinned hand and deceive the witnesses in The Red Moon of Meru (The Secret of Father Brown).
An Indian poison reversible by emetics, described in a book on poisons on Major Putnam's table and used in the plot to kill Colonel Cray in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A long iron garden seat used by Flambeau as a weapon when he abruptly heaves it at opponents in The God of the Gongs (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
The iron treasure chest of Indian workmanship opened aboard ship to reveal the Agra treasure — only to be found empty when the seal was broken in The Sign of the Four.
Two long Italian rapiers with splendid steel hilts, produced by Flambeau for the Christmas charade fencing game in The Flying Stars (The Innocence of Father Brown).
An ivory box fitted with a concealed spring that pricks the opener's finger with a poisoned needle, sent to Holmes as an assassination device in The Adventure of the Dying Detective.
The Jewish lamp stolen from M. d'Imblevalle's home on the Rue Murillo, apparently the sole target but actually a decoy for Lupin's real theft in The Blonde Lady.

A British Officer in Burmah and Brazil, a sort of autobiography published by Lieutenant-Colonel Keith, used by Father Brown to trace the truth about General St. Clare in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Lady Pulman's jewelled tiara and other jewels, stolen in the Beechwood House burglary investigated in The Secret of Father Brown.

Langur Monkey Serum is the experimental rejuvenation substance derived from a langur monkey, obtained by Dr. H. Lowenstein and secretly self-administered by Professor Presbury, causing his ape-like nocturnal behaviour in The Adventure of the Creeping Man.
The lift shaft in the Eye of Apollo building, used by Kalon to murder Pauline Stacey by causing her to step into an empty shaft in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A light brown sack inquired after by Father Brown of the police, significant to identifying the means by which a body was transported in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A bunch of lilies-of-the-valley grotesquely still clutched by Captain Cutler after the violent struggle in The Man in the Passage (The Wisdom of Father Brown).

The L.L. Letter to Sir Charles is the note from Mrs. Laura Lyons (initialled L.L.) that lured Sir Charles Baskerville to the Yew Alley gate on the night of his death, found in John Barrymore's possession in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
Loaded revolvers carried by the courier and the young banker for protection during the mountain journey hijacked by brigands in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A triangle-in-circle brand mark burned on the arm of initiates and found on a victim's body, linking the murder to the Scowrers lodge in The Valley of Fear.
A room at Reed House fitted and filled with looking-glasses at every angle of refraction, creating a disorienting labyrinth of mirrors in The Sins of Prince Saradine (The Innocence of Father Brown).
A considerable hoard of loose diamonds without any settings, part of the eccentric collection of stripped precious objects at Glengyle Castle in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Heaps of loose snuff lying on the mantelpieces without boxes or horns, part of the stripped collection mania of Lord Glengyle at the castle in The Innocence of Father Brown.

Lucy's Wedding Ring is the plain gold ring found at the Lauriston Gardens murder scene in A Study in Scarlet that tells Holmes a woman was present; Holmes uses a newspaper advertisement for it as a trap to draw out Jefferson Hope.
Holmes's large round magnifying glass, used to examine footprints and clues at crime scenes, first produced at the Lauriston Gardens murder in A Study in Scarlet.
The Mazarin Stone, also called the Crown Diamond, a priceless yellow gem stolen by Count Negretto Sylvius and ultimately recovered by Holmes in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone.
Curious little heaps of microscopic steel springs and tiny wheels, found in the eccentric inventor Smythe's rooms and suggesting his mechanical preoccupations in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The empty medicine bottle found on the floor beside the body of Arthur Carstairs, indicating the apparent means of his suicide in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A Ming saucer highly valued by the Baron, used as the pretext for Watson's visit to the villain's house in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client (The Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes).
Sir Henry Baskerville's missing boots — first a new brown one, then an old black one — stolen by Stapleton to give the trained hound a scent to follow in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
John Douglas's missing wedding ring, absent from the fingerless body in the moat at Birlstone, a key anomaly that alerts Holmes to the body-substitution scheme in The Valley of Fear.
A half-sheet of thin notepaper bearing a Moabite/Phoenician script in Chinese ink that conceals a hidden water-activated message in Roman capitals, the clue in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Dr. Mortimer's walking stick, left behind at 221B Baker Street, which Holmes analyses to deduce its owner's profession and career before he arrives, opening The Hound of the Baskervilles.
John Bankes's motor-car, in which he arrives radiantly at his own steering-wheel to visit the hamlet in The Man with Two Beards (The Secret of Father Brown).
Muscari's cutlass, buckled on with boyish gratification under his black cloak before the Alpine journey in The Paradise of Thieves (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
Captain Musgrave's car, offered to take a friend north to Musgrave Moss, the journey that draws characters into the story in The Worst Crime in the World (The Secret of Father Brown).
The Musgrave Ritual, an ancient catechism recited by the Musgrave family for generations, deciphered by Holmes to locate the hiding place of the Crown of Charles I in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.
A mysterious note found in Garcia's pocket at the time of his murder, bearing cryptic instructions that form a central clue in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge.
A small pair of nail scissors picked up by Father Brown at a crime scene, identified as the key implement in a method of murder in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Oberstein's collection of naval signals, spy-craft material recovered in connection with the Bruce-Partington plans theft in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans.
The Naval Treaty document, a secret agreement between England and Italy stolen from the Foreign Office, whose recovery Holmes investigates in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.
Dr. Hirsch's secret formula for a noiseless explosive, carefully guarded by the French Government and the object of the spy's mission in The Duel of Dr Hirsch (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
Oberon's fairy spear, a theatrical prop handed lordly-style like a sceptre to the actor Parkinson, who later uses it to kill his wife in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A large-scale Ordnance Survey map of Dartmoor used by Holmes and Watson to plan their movements and orientate themselves to the moor's geography in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
A curved Oriental dagger inlaid with stones, found hidden in the long grass of the garden and identified by Father Brown as the murder weapon in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A deformed or crooked silver or steel musical instrument resembling a violin, played with a bow, found among a character's possessions in The Secret of Father Brown.
Prince Otto's military sash or scarf, unbound and found crumpled beside him, used as both a gag and the murder weapon by his traitorous sentries in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Pantomime scenery pieces including the sapphire Gates of Bluebeard's Blue Palace and portions of the Enchanted Grove, leaning against a wall backstage in The Secret of Father Brown.
Pauline Stacey's incomplete will written on blue foolscap, leaving her money to the Eye of Apollo cult, cut short when the pen ran dry and she was sent to the lift in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A peacock green robe and high cap of uncommon outline worn by the Master of the Mountain fortune-teller at his purple tent in The Secret of Father Brown.
The Emperor's Pearl, a great gift jewel from a foreign monarch that Raffles steals during a royal visit in The Gift of the Emperor (The Amateur Cracksman).

The Morstan Pearls are the series of very large lustrous pearls sent annually to Mary Morstan beginning six years before the events of The Sign of the Four, their origin ultimately traced by Holmes to the divided Agra treasure.

A brown sixteenth-century map with Tritons and little ships on a curly sea, mistaken by a character for a Pacific chart but actually a map of the Pendragon river and grounds in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The old wooden Pendragon Tower alight with fire, a recurring curse-sign that Father Brown douses with a garden hose in The Perishing of the Pendragons (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
A pepper-pot with a small bullet hole drilled through it by Cray's shot, used by Father Brown to demonstrate the exact angle and trajectory of the revolver fire in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A compromising photograph of a woman accompanied by a letter, the object of Holmes's mission for the King of Bohemia in A Scandal in Bohemia (The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes).
A photograph of Stapleton's former Yorkshire school run under the name Vandeleur, used by Holmes to establish Stapleton's true identity in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
A small box of pills found at Valentin's elbow in his study, the means by which the great detective took his own life in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A scrap of pink society newspaper cutting handed to Father Brown containing a relevant report about events at Pendragon Park in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Pins stuck in a chart while General Outram smokes a long thin black cigar in his study, a visual detail of military habit observed by Father Brown in The Secret of Father Brown.

Playing cards littered across the table and fluttered about the floor of Mr. Glass's room, found by Father Brown and the American investigator in The Absence of Mr. Glass (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
A hypodermic needle projectile — a miniature two-inch brass cylinder with a needle at one end — used to administer poison at a distance in The Red Thumb Mark.
A little glass bottle of poison whose contents cause a heavy face to turn clay-coloured when sniffed, found during an investigation in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Two pills produced by Holmes from a small cardboard box — one deadly, one harmless — used to demonstrate Jefferson Hope's poisoning method to the police in A Study in Scarlet.
The Poison Thorn is Tonga's dart in The Sign of the Four, a long sharp black thorn with a glazed poisoned point fired from a blowpipe through the locked window to kill Bartholomew Sholto.
A Trichinopoly cheroot containing a chalky alkaloid deposit introduced near the small end, identified by Thorndyke as a means of delivering a slow poison in The Red Thumb Mark.
A decanter of port and a wine glass at John Boulnois's elbow while he reads a penny dreadful, establishing his alibi at home in The Strange Crime of John Boulnois (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
A portrait of Paul Arnhold as Chamberlain, showing white hair, black eyebrows, and a pink painted smile, contemplated by a character in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

A post obit agreement by which young Musgrave seeks to raise a large sum against his elderly father's estate in Northumberland, the financial transaction that draws attention to the family in The Secret of Father Brown.
The red, blue, and gold postman's uniform worn by James Welkin as a disguise, making him invisible to witnesses as a habitual presence in The Invisible Man (The Innocence of Father Brown).
Potato sacks used by the barber Wicks to conceal and transport Vaudrey's body from his shop to the river in The Secret of Father Brown.
The Practical Handbook of Bee Culture, Holmes's magnum opus written during his retirement, used as cover for his infiltration of Von Bork's spy network in His Last Bow.
A theatrical property dagger of the scenic kind found beside a body in a backstage passage, examined by Father Brown during his investigation in The Secret of Father Brown.
A pulsometer psychometric machine that measures heartbeat response to spoken words, demonstrated as a truth-detection method in The Mistake of the Machine (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
The purple wig worn by the Duke of Exmoor to conceal some secret disfigurement about which he feels genuine bitterness, central to The Purple Wig (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
The Queen's Necklace, the legendary diamond necklace made for Marie Antoinette and central to the theft that launches Arsène Lupin's career in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
A glass conservatory full of tropical blossoms of unique and almost monstrous beauty adjoining Quinton's house, part of the setting of The Wrong Shape (The Innocence of Father Brown).
The Cure of a Saint, Leonard Quinton's strange manuscript about Eastern mystical healing, which he talks about obsessively in The Wrong Shape (The Innocence of Father Brown).

Leonard Quinton's supposed suicide note in his unmistakable handwriting reading 'I die by my own hand; yet I die murdered!', found on wrong-shaped paper in The Wrong Shape (The Innocence of Father Brown).
Raffles's silver cigarette case containing his Sullivan cigarettes, a characteristic accessory that marks his elegant habits in The Ides of March (The Amateur Cracksman).
Raffles' Jemmy is the crowbar that served A. J. Raffles reliably in his housebreaking, mentioned in Le Premier Pas from The Amateur Cracksman as a valued tool of his criminal trade.
Raffles's criminal relics — his tools and memorabilia — displayed in the Black Museum at Scotland Yard after his death in Raffles: A Thief in the Night.
A large proclamation produced by the brigand captain announcing the capture of an English millionaire and demanding a ransom of two thousand pounds in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

A razor identified by Father Brown as the first of three weapons used in attempts on Colonel Cray's life, leaving only a feathery-light cut indicative of its extreme sharpness in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A long scarlet false beard of the theatrical kind, concealed in a folded paper and used by the disguised Moonshine in his visits to the hamlet barber's shop in The Secret of Father Brown.
The Red Moon of Meru, a stupendously large burning red ruby from the cavern idol at Mount Meru, stolen and recovered in The Red Moon of Meru (The Secret of Father Brown).
A small stove with red glowing eyes like a goblin's, beside which a tiny figure is perched in The Secret of Father Brown.
A red wax candle stuck in a corner to light the gloomy murder room at 3 Lauriston Gardens, a detail noted by Holmes at the scene of Drebber's murder in A Study in Scarlet.
A rank red-hair wig fitted around a face and combined with blue spectacles, the principal elements of Dubosc's impersonation of Dr. Hirsch in The Duel of Dr Hirsch (The Wisdom of Father Brown).

The long well-lit room panelled in light wood and lined with a large number of looking-glasses at Reed House, where Flambeau and Father Brown observe the multiple reflections of Prince Saradine in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A heavy revolver fired twice followed by a cry for help, the weapon discharged in a nighttime incident investigated by Father Brown in The Secret of Father Brown.
A revolver brandished defiantly by John Bankes during the climactic confrontation at the hamlet in The Man with Two Beards (The Secret of Father Brown).
A pistol kept under Mr. Smart's pillow in his bedroom, placed there as a precaution to guard his exotic goldfish in The Secret of Father Brown.
A newspaper advertisement placed by Holmes inviting the finder of a wedding ring to collect it at 221B Baker Street, used to draw out and identify Jefferson Hope in A Study in Scarlet.
A picturesque short cloak clasped with a silver clasp and chain blazing like a star in a flashlight, worn by the actor Hugo Romaine in The Dagger with Wings (The Secret of Father Brown).

Sir Claude Champion's tight crimson Romeo costume glinting with gold, worn at the Pendragon Park open-air performance, in which he died in The Strange Crime of John Boulnois (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
A slender sparkling rapier thrown from the sundial over the pine-wood and flashing in the moonlight, the theatrical sword used as a murder instrument in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

The Rope is the long coil Jonathan Small brings to Pondicherry Lodge in The Sign of the Four, allowing Tonga to climb the side of the building to the locked upper room where Bartholomew Sholto was killed.

Six or seven ropes knotted round a man's elbows and ankles, apparently binding Mr. Todhunter but actually part of his self-trained escape-artist routine in The Absence of Mr. Glass (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
A large revolver found flung away near the centre of the floor at the crime scene in The Hammer of God (The Innocence of Father Brown).
A half-empty open whisky bottle rolled near a large revolver at the crime scene in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A Rudge-Whitworth bicycle found near Birlstone Manor House, linked by MacDonald to Ted Baldwin and used by Holmes to reconstruct the night of the murder in The Valley of Fear.
Sahara King, the circus lion that escapes its cage and is blamed for the death of Ronder the showman, though the truth is darker in The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger.
Prince Saradine's visiting card with a French invitation in green ink directing Father Brown and Flambeau to Reed Island if they should ever become respectable in The Innocence of Father Brown.

The Sawed-off Shotgun is the weapon associated with both the Birlstone Manor murder scene and the Scowrer violence at Crow Hill Mine in The Valley of Fear, identified by Holmes as manufactured by the Pennsylvania Small Arms Company.
Two pairs of round Scottish shields mounted symmetrically on opposite walls of Musgrave Castle's great hall in The Worst Crime in the World (The Secret of Father Brown).
The mummy case of Sebek-hotep, a priest of Amen Ra from the time of Amenhotep III, a flexible cartonnage case large enough to conceal a human body, central to The Vanishing Man.
Shoscombe Prince, Sir Robert Norberton's prize racehorse whose Derby victory saves the family estate from debt in The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place.

The Sign of the Four Card is the torn piece of paper with the words 'The sign of the four' fixed to Bartholomew Sholto's chest after his murder in The Sign of the Four, Jonathan Small's calling card marking his revenge.
The Signal Candle at Window is the light John Barrymore holds against the Baskerville Hall window in The Hound of the Baskervilles, signalling food drops to the escaped convict Selden on the moor.
Etta Todd's lantern swung three times above her head as a deliberate signal in the darkness outside Pilgrim's Pond in The God of the Gongs (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
A gentleman's silk top hat rolled into a corner of Mr. Glass's room, the first clue pointing to a mysterious visitor's identity in The Absence of Mr. Glass (The Wisdom of Father Brown).

The set of pearl-hilted silver fish knives and forks, the celebrated insignia of the Twelve True Fishermen, stolen from the Vernon Hotel and recovered by Father Brown in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A silver model of St. Clare's tomb with a bowed silver head and broken silver sword, displayed on a table in the Sign of the Broken Sword inn in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Six plaster busts of Napoleon systematically smashed by Beppo, one of which conceals the Black Pearl of the Borgias, in The Adventure of the Six Napoleons (The Return of Sherlock Holmes).
The smallest and lightest of the smithy hammers, found with blood on it by Father Brown, the weapon dropped from the belfry by Wilfred Bohun in The Hammer of God (The Innocence of Father Brown).
A small stove on flagstones with red eyes glowing like a goblin, warming the tiny interior of the hamlet's combined shop in The Secret of Father Brown.
A Smith and Wesson revolver, a specific firearm that figures in the violence of the Vermissa Valley Scowrers narrative in Part II of The Valley of Fear.

Smythe's Silent Service, a set of clockwork mechanical domestic servants advertised as a butler that never drinks and housemaids that never flirt, invented by the murder victim in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A diary in common exercise books written by an English soldier, found by the Brazilians and shedding light on the treachery of General St. Clare in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Two half-featured lumps of stone with holes for eyes, South Sea idols flanking the entrance to Lord Mounteagle's Asiatic museum in The Secret of Father Brown.
Lady Frances Carfax's famous Spanish family jewellery, targeted by the criminal Holy Peters and his wife in The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax.
The Speckled Band, a deadly swamp adder snake — the deadliest in India — used as a murder weapon by Dr. Roylott in The Adventure of the Speckled Band (The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes).
A spiked bracelet concealed up the sleeve, a Flambeau-style criminal tool identified by Father Brown as confirming a disguised criminal's identity in The Innocence of Father Brown.
General St. Clare's sword with its tip broken off, found lying beside his recumbent bronze memorial statue with the broken point symbolising his disgrace in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A letter from a foreign potentate in a blue envelope, the stolen state document whose recovery prevents an international crisis in The Adventure of the Second Stain (The Return of Sherlock Holmes).
The steam launch Aurora, the swift river vessel commanded by Jonathan Small in The Sign of the Four, used for the escape down the Thames until captured by Holmes and Watson on the police pinnace.
Prince Otto's steel-lined inner cabin or cupboard erected within his castle room, giving him a fortified private study like a safe or a battleship interior in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The steel handcuffs that click onto Jefferson Hope's wrists when Holmes and Watson trap him in 221B Baker Street, ending A Study in Scarlet.
The stolen silver comprising fish-knives, forks, old cruet-stand, and silver cream-jug, hidden in the dustbin as a blind for Major Putnam's poison plot in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A complete suit of fourteenth-century tilting armour standing like a sentinel beside the large fireplace at Musgrave Castle, used to conceal the baronet's body in The Secret of Father Brown.
The small round hill or dome of a sundial at Pendragon Park, the spot where Sir Claude Champion died by his own hand in The Strange Crime of John Boulnois (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
The screen behind which a principal performer in The School for Scandal remains hidden on stage for a considerable time, an analogy used in The Secret of Father Brown.
A telephone installed in Father Brown's study, used by a servant to summon the priest in a story from The Secret of Father Brown.
A sinister idol in an Indian temple, encountered by a character who descends steps into darkness and glimpses it in the shadows, in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

The Hound is Stapleton's enormous coal-black dog in The Hound of the Baskervilles, daubed with phosphorus to create a supernatural appearance and trained to hunt along a scent, ultimately shot dead by Holmes and Watson on the moor.
A forged fingerprint — a photo-mechanical reproduction of a Thumbograph print — planted as false evidence at the scene of a safe robbery, central to the plot of Dr Thorndyke's The Red Thumb Mark.
Dr Thorndyke's compact portable laboratory, a one-foot-square Willesden-canvas case containing a full set of forensic instruments, carried to crime scenes in The Adventures of Dr Thorndyke.
Anonymous threatening letters written in red ink on stamp paper, sent to a character warning of murder if he visits a woman, appearing in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Three large painted and gilded wooden figures of the Three Kings (the Biblical Magi bearing gifts), displayed as props in a theatrical setting in The Secret of Father Brown.
A small red-cloth-bound novelty book of blank pages used to collect thumb-prints, purchased by Mrs Hornby and later exploited as the source of the forged fingerprint evidence in The Red Thumb Mark.

Toby is the ugly long-haired lop-eared tracking dog owned by Sherman in The Sign of the Four, borrowed by Holmes to follow the creosote trail from Pondicherry Lodge; Holmes rates Toby's help above the whole detective force.
A trap-door built into a theatre stage originally constructed for pantomimes, used as a secret exit in a plot in The Secret of Father Brown.
A small, stout, leather-bound volume on old travels that contains a reference to Voodoo, produced from Father Brown's pocket during an investigation in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A long-healed three-cornered cicatrice on a character's bald head, pointed out as a distinguishing physical mark in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A strong Indian cigar variety sought by a character in an Indian city, whose attempt to purchase them leads to the inciting incident involving the temple monkey in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A hall hung with weapons in the Paris house of Valentin, the head of the Paris police, serving as the setting for a murder in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A boldly worn white hat on the grey head of Sir Arthur Vaudrey, used as a visual identifying detail in The Secret of Father Brown.
A priceless Velasquez portrait of the Infanta, the target of a disputed legal ownership case and eventual Raffles theft in Nine Points of the Law (The Amateur Cracksman).
A voodoo fetish — a pan of blood and a charred bone — found at Wisteria Lodge, evidence of secret South American religious rituals practised by the cook in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge.
The Card with V.V. 341 is the cryptic card found at the Birlstone crime scene in The Valley of Fear, decoded by Holmes as standing for Vermissa Valley Lodge 341 of the Scowrers.
A commodious wagonette-style carriage devised by a modernist courier to transport a party of travellers in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A prison warder's missing gun, taken after the warder was hurled from a high wall and battered to death, a key clue in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
An anonymous letter of common greyish quality addressed to Sir Henry Baskerville at the Northumberland Hotel, warning him away from the moor in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
Watson's revolver, carried at Holmes's request when danger threatens and a symbol of Watson's role as Holmes's armed protector, mentioned from A Study in Scarlet onwards.
Wax candles stuck in bottle necks for lack of candlesticks, noted as a detail of poverty or improvisation in a location described in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A wax bust of Holmes placed in the Baker Street window to deceive watchers and assassins, used to protect him and trap Count Sylvius in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone.
Whitaker's Almanac, the book used as the key to a cipher message sent to Holmes warning of a plot against John Douglas in The Valley of Fear.
Two wine glasses set out on a side-table and a third smashed on the carpet in a star of crystal, clues at a crime scene in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A great goblet filled with Spanish wine by Flambeau and set before Father Brown during a storytelling session in The Secret of Father Brown.
A wineglass set down by Father Brown at a significant moment during a conversation in The Secret of Father Brown.

The Wooden Leg (Jonathan Small) is the timber stump whose impression Holmes reads alongside the boot-mark at Pondicherry Lodge in The Sign of the Four, identifying Small as a one-legged man who lost his limb to a crocodile in India.
Yellow gloves worn and flapped by Sir Arthur Vaudrey as a dandy affectation while walking with his walking-stick in The Secret of Father Brown.
The three Arnhold brothers, celebrated guerrilla patriots to whom Swinburne wrote a poem, who defeated an enemy force in several battles in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Baker Street Irregulars are the street-boy detective network led by Wiggins and organised by Holmes in A Study in Scarlet, described as the Baker Street division of the detective police force.
The Berkshire Regiment, to which Watson was attached as medical officer and with whom he fought at the battle of Maiwand in A Study in Scarlet.
Brickfall and Amberley, an art materials manufacturing firm of which Josiah Amberley was junior partner before retirement in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman.
The Carbonari, the Italian revolutionary secret society that is the parent body of the Red Circle encountered by Holmes in The Adventure of the Red Circle.
Charing Cross Hospital, the institution whose initials on Dr. Mortimer's walking stick prompt Holmes's deduction about its owner in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
A sun-worship cult whose leader calls himself the New Priest of Apollo, active in London and encountered in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Clarion and The New Age, radical newspapers in which Sir Leopold had been dealt with harshly in articles, referenced in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Credit Lyonnais bank, where Arsene Lupin rented a safety-deposit box to store a stolen item in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsene Lupin.

The Criminologists' Club is the secret society of wealthy amateur criminologists presided over by the Earl of Thornaby in A Thief in the Night, which Raffles infiltrates and robs in the story of the same name.
The Daily News, a London newspaper that opined the Drebber murder was a political crime in A Study in Scarlet.
The Daily Reformer, a newspaper whose industrious editor Edward Nutt appears at his desk as the framing device for a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Daily Telegraph, a London newspaper that noted the Drebber murder case was one of the strangest in the history of crime in A Study in Scarlet.

The Danite Band, also called the Avenging Angels, is the Mormon enforcement organisation in A Study in Scarlet that murders John Ferrier and abducts Lucy, its formidable machinery of secret terror surpassing even the Inquisition of Seville.
The Eyres of Exmoor, an old aristocratic family associated with a local legend known to country women as the Devil's Ear of Eyre, in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Fifth Northumberland Fusiliers, the regiment with which Watson sailed for India and served as Assistant Surgeon before being wounded in A Study in Scarlet.
The French Foreign Legion, the military unit to which Commandant O'Brien belongs in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A threatening Nationalist crowd that appears in uproar in a scene set in France in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The French Republican Government, which placed considerable trust in a chemist character for chemical improvements in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Gelder and Co., a firm of sculptors in Stepney from whom a bust involved in a Sherlock Holmes case was commissioned in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
A Voodoo-derived secret society, also called the Monkey, organised outside Jamaica and employing assassins, investigated in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The law firm of a lawyer named Granby, referenced in the background of a case in The Secret of Father Brown.
A steamship company operating services to America, by one of whose vessels Jefferson Hope intended to flee England in A Study in Scarlet.

The Holy Council of Four is the ruling executive body of the Mormon theocracy in A Study in Scarlet whose will, invoked by Brigham Young, enforces the ultimatum that Lucy Ferrier must marry a Mormon within a month.
The Imperial Yeomanry, the volunteer cavalry force that Raffles joins to fight in the Boer War in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman.
The Iron Dike Company, a mining concern whose chief foreman appears as a character in The Valley of Fear.
Italian gendarmes who arrive to rescue captives from brigands in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Ku Klux Klan (K.K.K.), the American secret society whose threatening message causes a character to cry out in horror in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes.
A gathering of the leaders of the legal profession attending a grand dinner, mentioned as context for a character's absence in The Secret of Father Brown.
Lodge 29 of the Ancient Order of Freemen (Chicago), the Scowrers chapter where McMurdo presents his credentials on first arriving in Vermissa Valley in The Valley of Fear.

Lodge 341 of the Eminent Order of Freemen in Vermissa Valley is the Scowrers' chapter led by Boss McGinty that terrorises the Pennsylvania coal district through extortion and murder in The Valley of Fear.

Merton County Lodge 249 is one of the regional lodges of the Scowrers (Ancient Order of Freemen) in The Valley of Fear, from which Andrews and Lawler participate in the violent actions of Part II, Chapter III.
The Mine Constabulary (Mine Police), the private police force that maintains order in the mining district of The Valley of Fear, captained by Captain Marvin.
A monastery founded and generously funded by the false Marquis, who himself lives austerely like a hermit, in The Secret of Father Brown.
The brigand band of Montano, the self-styled King of Thieves, who withstood six military campaigns by the Italian government in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

The Mormon Church (Church of the Latter Day Saints), the religious organization depicted in A Study in Scarlet whose elders, including Brigham Young, Drebber, and Stangerson, persecute John Ferrier and force Lucy into marriage.
Morrison, Morrison, and Dodd, the firm of solicitors whose letter to Holmes opens The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire.
The Paris police force, headed by Valentin, the most celebrated detective in the world, appearing in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Pennsylvania Small Arms Company, a firearms manufacturer referenced in connection with a character in The Valley of Fear.
Pinkerton's Detective Agency, the famous American private detective firm that placed Birdy Edwards undercover among the Scowrers in the Vermissa Valley to gather evidence against them.
The combined police forces of two hemispheres, both of whom hunt Flambeau, as he himself reveals in The Secret of Father Brown.
Princetown Prison, the Dartmoor gaol from which the convicted murderer Selden escapes at the start of The Hound of the Baskervilles.
The Prussian (German) Empire, which gained hegemony relatively late over the small state of Heiligwaldenstein in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Red Circle is the Carbonari-derived Italian secret society in The Adventure of the Red Circle that traps Gennaro Lucca in New York and whose assassin Giuseppe Gorgiano follows him to London.
The Red-Headed League, a bogus organisation invented by John Clay to keep Jabez Wilson occupied while a tunnel was dug to the bank in The Adventure of the Red-Headed League.
Scotland Yard, the London metropolitan detective police force whose inspectors including Gregson, Lestrade, and MacDonald regularly consult Sherlock Holmes on difficult cases.

The Scowrers, operating as the Ancient Order of Freemen in Vermissa Valley, are a violent secret society that extorts and murders in the Pennsylvania coalfields under Boss McGinty's command in The Valley of Fear.
The Second Sight Sisterhood of Indianapolis, an American spiritualist organisation referenced in The Secret of Father Brown.
Smart Society, a pink-paper society journal that a reporter character heartily despised but worked for in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Smart Society, a pink-paper publication devoted to society journalism, referenced in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Surete, the French national detective bureau whose best men investigated Arsene Lupin's crimes and whose chief Dudouis visited Lupin in prison, appearing across multiple Lupin stories.
The Sussex Constabulary is the local police force represented by Sergeant Wilson and Inspector White Mason who assist Holmes with the Birlstone Manor investigation in The Valley of Fear.
An Indian secret society devoted to the Monkey idol, believed by a character to be pursuing him as retribution for entering their temple in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Comet, a newspaper owned by Sir John Cockspur, which ran the mysterious Marquis of Marne as a front-page mystery story in The Secret of Father Brown.
The Echo, a London newspaper that reported on Jefferson Hope's death and the frustration of a sensational trial in A Study in Scarlet.
The Guillotine, a French Nationalist newspaper that weekly caricatured its political opponents with rolling eyes, referenced in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Standard, a London newspaper that commented the Drebber murder was the sort of lawless outrage typical under a Liberal Administration in A Study in Scarlet.
The Twelve True Fishermen, an exclusive London club that holds its annual dinner at the Vernon Hotel in Belgravia, the setting for a theft in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Tyrolean Dairy Company, a fraudulent London business run without dairies, cows, carts, or milk, exposed as a swindle in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Vermissa Herald, a local newspaper in the Valley of Fear mining district whose editor James Stanger opposes the Scowrers in The Valley of Fear.
The Western Sun, a colossal American daily newspaper whose English emissary appears as a reporter character in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Western Sun, a large American daily newspaper that carried society gossip columns about London's fashionable world, in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Ettie Shafter, the identity under which Ivy Douglas (née Ettie Shafter) lived in Vermissa Valley before marrying Birdy Edwards, revealed in Part II of The Valley of Fear.
Jack McMurdo, the undercover identity assumed by Pinkerton agent Birdy Edwards to infiltrate the Scowrers in Vermissa Valley in The Valley of Fear.
John Douglas, the settled English-country-gentleman identity taken on by Birdy Edwards after leaving America, maintained until his death in The Valley of Fear.
104 Berkeley Square, the London house of a character in The Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes.

221B Baker Street, the London lodgings shared by Sherlock Holmes and Dr. Watson, described as comfortable furnished rooms, where Holmes receives clients, conducts experiments, and bases his detective operations throughout the canon.
3 Lauriston Gardens is the empty house off the Brixton Road where Enoch Drebber's body is found at the opening of A Study in Scarlet, the first crime scene Holmes and Watson attend together.
46 Audley Court, a narrow passage leading into a flag-paved quadrangle lined by dingy houses, visited by Holmes in A Study in Scarlet.
5A King's Bench Walk, Temple, the address of Dr Thorndyke's chambers and forensic laboratory in The Vanishing Man.
Abbas Parva in Berkshire, the scene of the showman-murder tragedy whose survivor lives veiled and reclusive, investigated in The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger.
A comfortable middle-class house near Putney with a carriage drive and stable, used by a criminal character as the location of a murder in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Afghan border, where a military character gained early command due to action in accidents of warfare, mentioned in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Afghanistan, the theatre of war where Watson was wounded at Maiwand and from which he returned to London, his background immediately deduced by Holmes in A Study in Scarlet.

Agra Fort is the great Indian fortress whose labyrinthine corridors are where Jonathan Small and his three Sikh confederates murder the merchant Achmet for the Agra treasure, as recounted in The Sign of the Four.
The Albany, a set of fashionable bachelor rooms in London occupied by a self-indulgent character in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Ambrumezy, a Norman chateau with abbey ruins where a body is concealed and where Isidore Beautrelet and Arsene Lupin's paths converge in The Hollow Needle.
The American Exchange in the Strand, where a character named Boscombe worked as secretary in A Study in Scarlet.
The Andaman Islands, a remote British penal colony in the Bay of Bengal where Jonathan Small and Tonga were imprisoned, central to the backstory of the Agra Treasure in The Sign of the Four.
A mountain pass in the Apennines haunted by brigands who still control the ridge in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A passage running along the side of the Apollo Theatre in the Adelphi, London, used as a route by characters in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Appledore Towers, Hampstead, the home of blackmailer Charles Augustus Milverton in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
A handsome, narrow, modern house on the rural skirt of Hampstead, built like a tower and shaken by passing trains on the railway bank below, in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The steep green railway embankment overlooked by a narrow side of Armstrong's Hampstead house, significant to a murder plot in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Australia, to which Squire Eyres exiled his estranged son Giles on a small allowance after a quarrel, in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A bank with an adjoining manager's house, one of the buildings lining a remote village square in The Secret of Father Brown.
The Bar of Gold, a squalid opium den in Upper Swandam Lane behind the Thames wharves, visited by Holmes in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes.
Barton's Crossing, a location in the mining valley setting of The Valley of Fear.
St Bartholomew's Hospital, the famous London teaching hospital where Holmes and Watson are first introduced to each other by Stamford in A Study in Scarlet.

Baskerville Hall is the ancestral Dartmoor seat of the Baskerville family in The Hound of the Baskervilles, where Sir Henry takes up residence under the shadow of the family curse and Stapleton's murderous scheme to claim it.
Beechwood House, the residence of Sir Leopold Pulman, a new-comer to the neighbourhood in The Secret of Father Brown.
The Protestant Cathedral at Belfast, in whose northeast corner cemetery a clue is hidden in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Belfort, the French garrison town where Colonel Jules Dubosc of the Artillery is stationed, a key character in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A Catholic church service of Benediction that Father Brown must attend shortly after a conversation, referenced in The Secret of Father Brown.
Benito Canyon, California, the remote location where Douglas/Edwards planned to hide after escaping his enemies, referenced in the Epilogue of The Valley of Fear.
Birlstone is the Sussex village whose manor house is the scene of the apparent murder in The Valley of Fear, its location identified by Holmes from Porlock's cipher message at the novel's opening.
Birlstone Manor House is the ancient moated Sussex manor of John Douglas where the apparent murder takes place in The Valley of Fear, its drawbridge and moat central to the evidence Holmes evaluates.

The Black Museum at Scotland Yard is the collection of criminal artefacts where Raffles's burglar tools are held as relics after his death, featured in The Raffles Relics story from A Thief in the Night.
The Black River in Brazil, site of a rash military assault by an unsupported regiment mentioned in the backstory of The Innocence of Father Brown.
Bloomsbury Square, a London location visited by Raffles during a night operation in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman.
The Blue Boar, the only inn in the village of Bohun Beacon, located across the cobbled paths from the church, in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Blue Dragon, an inn at a village corner where the stranger Mr Hartopp from London is staying in The Secret of Father Brown.
The Blue Dragon, an inn whose sign is seen outside by characters passing through a village in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Bohun Beacon, a village perched on a steep hill whose tall church spire barely tops the slope, setting of a murder story in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The belfry of the church in Bohun Beacon, affording an unobstructed view over the surrounding plain, significant to a murder plot in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The smithy at the foot of the Bohun Beacon church, perpetually lit with fires and littered with hammers and iron scraps, in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Bond Street, London's fashionable shopping thoroughfare, mentioned in contrast to a humbler context in The Secret of Father Brown.
Boscombe Pool, the body of water beside which Charles McCarthy was murdered, investigated by Holmes in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes.
Boston, Massachusetts, the home city of Mr Grandison Chace, an American traveller in The Secret of Father Brown.
Brackenhurst Mansions, a residential block between Westminster and Victoria where the murder victim Alfred Hartridge had his flat in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Bradford, the constituency where a cousin of the Eyres family is standing for Parliament in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Briony Lodge, Serpentine Avenue, St. John's Wood, the home of Irene Adler from which Holmes attempts to recover the King of Bohemia's photograph in A Scandal in Bohemia.
The British Museum, the target of Raffles's audacious Diamond Jubilee night theft in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman.
Brixton, a south London district traversed by Watson and others en route across London in The Sign of the Four.
Brixton Road, the street off which 3 Lauriston Gardens is located, the scene of Enoch Drebber's murder in A Study in Scarlet.
An interminable avenue of tall houses somewhere in Brompton or Kensington, identified as the address of a character in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Brussels, a city from which Valentin tracked Flambeau to London via the Hook of Holland in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Buckingham Palace, invoked as a place where Father Brown would show his mild imperturbability equally as anywhere else in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Bullock Street, a London thoroughfare down which a detective follows fleeing suspects in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Dr Burdock's house, directly opposite a location visited by Father Brown in The Secret of Father Brown.
A butcher's shop at a hamlet corner, the last place where Sir Arthur was seen before his disappearance in The Secret of Father Brown.
The Cafe Anglais, a Paris restaurant mentioned in a fraudster's account of how a silver fish-service could be obtained there in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Cafe Charlemagne, where a light dinner was laid at the back for a gathering of characters early in the evening in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Cafe Riche, a Paris establishment mentioned in the context of leaving people penniless among bright lights in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Cairo, specifically near an English barracks in its modernized quarter, the setting of a tale told by Count de Lara in The Secret of Father Brown.
Camberwell, the south London suburb where Mary Morstan lodges with her employer Mrs. Cecil Forrester in The Sign of the Four.
Camden Town, a north London district through which Father Brown and Flambeau walk while two policemen nearly fall asleep following them in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A glowing confectioner's shop on a cool blue-twilight corner in Camden Town, passed by characters during a pursuit in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Camford, the fictional composite university where Professor Presbury holds his chair, the setting of The Adventure of the Creeping Man.
The Carlton Club, where Watson met Sir James Damery in The Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes.
Carson City, Nevada, the destination where two fugitive characters hoped to settle in safety in A Study in Scarlet.
Carstairs Street, a London street into which two pursued characters turn, noted by Father Brown in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Castle of Heiligwaldenstein, a vast labyrinthine stronghold whose duke inhabits a single small central room, in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Castle of Marne, a melancholy mansion with four towers glimpsed through grey evening, the home of the reclusive Marquis in The Secret of Father Brown.
The moat of the castle, running as a ribbon of black water covered with dead leaves and scum, described in The Secret of Father Brown.
The woods beyond Heiligwaldenstein castle where Prince Otto of Grossenmark was found lying dead in the darkening twilight in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Caulfield Gardens, the location of Hugo Oberstein's flat, the drop-point for the stolen Bruce-Partington submarine plans in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans.
The Champion Arms, a genuine feudal old-country inn entered by a traveller seeking information in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Champs Elysees in Paris, where two French intellectuals Maurice Brun and Armand Armagnac are crossing at the start of a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Charing Cross, a London landmark near which characters can be heard as a flower-girl laughs in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Madame Charpentier's Boarding House is the Torquay Terrace, Camberwell establishment where Enoch Drebber lodged before his murder, the first address Gregson investigates in A Study in Scarlet.
Château de Charmerace, the ancestral seat of the Dukes of Charmerace, which Lupin infiltrates and makes his base in Arsène Lupin (the novel).
Château de Malaquis, a feudal castle built on a river rock that Arsène Lupin burgles in the opening story of The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
Château de Thibermesnil, a Norman castle hosting a party of guests including military officers, the scene of the Thibermesnil burglary in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
Cheeseman's, a property in Lamberley, Sussex, the home of Robert Ferguson and the scene of the nursing vampire mystery in The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire.
Chicago, the city where Jack McMurdo claims to have come from when he arrives in Vermissa Valley, and to whose Lodge 29 he presents Scowrer credentials in The Valley of Fear.
A prison in Chicago where Father Brown served as Catholic chaplain to his co-religionists, mentioned in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Chisham, the fictional London suburb used by the author as a convenient name for the unnamed suburban setting of a story in The Secret of Father Brown.
The City and Suburban Bank, the target of a robbery by Raffles in The Chest of Silver in A Thief in the Night.
Clapham Junction, a south London railway hub invoked as a cultural marker identifying a character's origin in The Secret of Father Brown.
Cleveland, Ohio is the American city from which Enoch Drebber travelled to London, his address found at the Lauriston Gardens murder scene in A Study in Scarlet.
The cloisters at the end of the left wing of an abbey building, a secluded spot where a character retreats to be alone in The Secret of Father Brown.
Cobhole, Father Brown's old Essex parish which he visits nostalgically during events in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Cobhole in Essex, the parish where Father Brown formerly served as priest, his original place of residence before events of the stories in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Coombe Tracey, the nearest market town to Baskerville Hall on Dartmoor, where Watson hires horses and makes enquiries about Laura Lyons in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
Copenhagen, one of the European cities through which Jefferson Hope tracks Drebber and Stangerson before finally running them to earth in London in A Study in Scarlet.

Copper Beeches is Mr. Jephro Rucastle's country house five miles beyond Winchester in The Adventure of the Copper Beeches, where Violet Hunter is hired under suspicious conditions Holmes ultimately exposes.
The Cornwall coast and its rivers, where Father Brown joins Flambeau and Sir Cecil Fanshaw on a small yacht cruise in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Criterion Bar in London, where Stamford tapped Watson on the shoulder and first mentioned Sherlock Holmes to him in A Study in Scarlet.
Crow Hill Mine is the Vermissa Valley, Pennsylvania colliery where Scowrer violence and murders are carried out in Part II, Chapter V of The Valley of Fear.
Cumnor, a village near Boulnois's cottage in Oxfordshire, bathed in the last light of a summer sunset in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

Dartmoor, the wild Devon moorland and primary setting of The Hound of the Baskervilles, encompassing Baskerville Hall, Grimpen Mire, Lafter Hall, and Merripit House, whose desolate atmosphere pervades the case.
Count de Lara's house, one of three adjacent houses in a hamlet row, the scene of events in The Secret of Father Brown.
The Deaf School, the destination to which Father Brown must return after a conversation, mentioned in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Deep Dene House at the Sydenham end of a Norwood road, the home of Jonas Oldacre in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.

Devonshire is the English county where Dartmoor and Baskerville Hall are located in The Hound of the Baskervilles, the destination to which Holmes, Watson, and Sir Henry travel to confront the hound mystery.
Devonshire, the county from which a letter is postmarked, associated with the Exmoor estate in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Dieppe, a French seaside town where Devanne met the thief Velmont at the casino in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsene Lupin.
The Diogenes Club, the eccentric London club founded by Mycroft Holmes for the most unsociable men in the city, where conversation is strictly forbidden in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.
The Dragon Tower, the oldest and most grotesque tower of Heiligwaldenstein castle, described as homely as an ale-mug in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
13 Duncan Street, Houndsditch, the London address of Sally Dennis, listed as a witness in A Study in Scarlet.
Eagle Canon, a rocky American landscape where mountain birds nested, a location in the Utah wilderness sections of A Study in Scarlet.
A flat stretch of sand on the east coast of Scotland, the site of a pistol duel in The Secret of Father Brown.
The Embankment in London, location of a ramping equestrian statue of General St. Clare, referenced in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Empire Pavilion in Scarborough, where a performance or event is scheduled for Monday at eight o'clock in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Camden House, the empty building directly opposite 221B Baker Street used by Holmes to trap Colonel Moran in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
The Endowment House, the Mormon temple in Salt Lake City where Lucy Ferrier is forced to marry Enoch Drebber against her will in A Study in Scarlet.
The Epsom Grand Stand on the Downs, near which a man was found strangled in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Étretat, the Normandy coastal town whose chalk sea-cliffs conceal the entrance to the Hollow Needle, the ancient royal hiding-place in The Hollow Needle (Arsène Lupin).
Euston Station, London terminus where Jefferson Hope expects to intercept Drebber and Stangerson as they attempt to flee to Liverpool in A Study in Scarlet.
The Exmoor estate of the old Tory Duke of Exmoor, one of the few stiff aristocrats of the old school remaining, in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A rapid tour of British and American industrial cities announced as an itinerary — Birmingham, Manchester, Liverpool, Leeds, Hull, Huddersfield, Glasgow, and Chicago — in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A castle in Spain, the retirement home of Flambeau after he gave up crime, described as an appropriate address for a reformed master thief in The Secret of Father Brown.
Fountain Court in the Middle Temple, a serene legal quarter where Jervis declares his love for Juliet under the plane trees and sparrows in The Red Thumb Mark.
Lodgings in Fulham taken by a character to accommodate his companion during events in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Ghent, a Belgian city from which the Paris police tracked Flambeau through Brussels and then to London in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Gilmerton Mountains, the range that forms the backdrop to the Vermissa Valley coalfields dominated by the Scowrers in The Valley of Fear.
Glasgow, the city where Father Brown had business that he briefly left to meet Flambeau for a holiday in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Glengyle Castle, a grey Scottish castle at the end of a valley, home to the reclusive and eccentric Lord Glengyle in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The churchyard on the hill near Glengyle Castle, where Lord Glengyle's body (or supposed body) had lain, in The Innocence of Father Brown.
16 Godolphin Street, Westminster, the address where a murder connected to a political scandal takes place in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
Graystones, a village containing the Seven Stars inn, where Father Brown and a companion alight during a journey in The Secret of Father Brown.

The Great Alkali Plain is the vast arid North American desert through which the Mormon wagon train travels in the A Study in Scarlet backstory, rescued by Brigham Young before eventually reaching Salt Lake City.
Great Orme Street, the address where the mysterious lodger Emilia Lucca lives, drawing Holmes into The Adventure of the Red Circle.
Greenford, the village to which the Bohun Beacon blacksmith has gone, leaving his smithy unattended in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Grey Cottage near Oxford, the modest home of the philosopher John Boulnois, sought out by journalists in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Grimpen, a Dartmoor village visited by Watson to make enquiries about the hound mystery in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
Grimpen Mire is the vast Dartmoor bog in The Hound of the Baskervilles where Stapleton hides his hound in an old tin mine and into which he finally disappears when his plot is exposed.
Grossenmark, the domain ruled by Prince Otto, who invaded the neighbouring state of Heiligwaldenstein in pursuit of a legendary gold hoard in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A great house in Grosvenor Square, the address of a wealthy family involved in a case in The Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes.
A bungalow in the garden of Mr Justice Gwynne, where a character is sitting and reading signs in stars of red and gold across the garden, in The Secret of Father Brown.
The garden of Mr Justice Gwynne by night, described as a rather singular spectacle and the setting of a dramatic scene in The Secret of Father Brown.
Badsham (Halbury) Junction, the railway location near which Brodski's body is found on the line near Hickler's cottage in The Adventures of Dr Thorndyke.
Hales Lodge, Hampstead, the address of Cecil James Barker, a principal witness and friend of John Douglas in The Valley of Fear.
Halliday's Private Hotel, the establishment where Joseph Stangerson is found stabbed in his room, the site of the second murder in A Study in Scarlet.
A small, isolated hamlet of a single street standing by a river with a row of small shops, the setting of a story in The Secret of Father Brown.
A canal below Hampstead into which Mr Smythe's body is thrown, discovered by neighbours in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Hampstead Heath, a north London open space mentioned as a tram destination in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Hartlepool, the English town where Father Brown served as curate and encountered three women who wore spiked bracelets as penance in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Harwich, the Essex port where a cross-channel boat let loose a crowd of travellers at the start of events in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Heiligwaldenstein, a picturesque toy kingdom in the German-speaking world under Prussian hegemony, setting of a dynastic murder plot in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The hermitage of Heinrich, a thorn-fenced cave on a ridge where one of the three patriot brothers lived in hiding in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
High Gable, the country house rented by Scott Eccles where he encounters the mysterious household that sets off events in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge.
Highgate police station, where a servant deposited his master's money with Inspector Robson after the theft in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Himylaya Mansions in Hampstead, the residential address of a character investigated in the invisible man murder in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A handsome Paris house near the Elysee, the home of the controversial French scientist Dr Hirsch in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Hobson's Patch, a community in Vermissa Valley where the Scowrers commit several outrages in Part II of The Valley of Fear.
The Holborn Restaurant in London, where Holmes and Watson dined pleasantly together in A Study in Scarlet.
Holdernesse Hall in Hallamshire, the great country seat of the Duke of Holdernesse whose young son goes missing in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
Holloway Prison, where Reuben Hornby is detained as prisoner B.31, composedly awaiting his trial in The Red Thumb Mark.
Holmes's Sussex Villa is the retirement home on the South Downs where Sherlock Holmes keeps bees after leaving London, from which he narrates The Adventure of the Lion's Mane in The Case-Book.
Holmgate, the place where a character's sister lives, from which the character has just arrived before visiting the hamlet in The Secret of Father Brown.
Hong Kong, whose siege made the military reputation of Captain Cutler before his celebrated march across China, in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Hook of Holland, the Dutch ferry terminal through which Flambeau passed on his route from Brussels to England in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A quiet hotel near the castle where General Outram's party assembles, only to find the actor Hugo Romaine absent in The Secret of Father Brown.
The Hotel Saint Louis near the Elysee in Paris, to which a character is driven with Dr Hirsch in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Hull and Huddersfield Bank, from whose funds a character is theatrically threatened with arrest for embezzlement in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

India is the colonial posting backdrop in the Holmes and Lupin canons, serving as the country where Watson was wounded, where Captain Morstan served, and where the Agra treasure backstory of The Sign of the Four takes place during the Great Mutiny.
An unnamed Indian city where a character entered the sacred Monkey temple while seeking Trichinopoli cigars, setting off the vendetta in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Indianapolis, Indiana, home of the Second Sight Sisterhood spiritualist organisation referenced in The Secret of Father Brown.
A river island where the hermit-like Dalmon camps alone in a bungalow, discovered by Father Brown in The Secret of Father Brown.
Italy, the country to which Raffles and Bunny pursue the rescue or kidnapping of Faustina in The Fate of Faustina (Raffles: Further Adventures).
Jacobson's Yard, the sixteenth boatyard searched by Holmes, where he discovers the Aurora has been handed over for refit in The Sign of the Four.
Jamaica, the original centre of Voodoo from which the Monkey secret society spread its organisation abroad, cited in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
6A King's Bench Walk, Inner Temple, Thorndyke's house and chambers pointed out to Jervis in The Red Thumb Mark.
SS La Provence, the swift transatlantic steamship aboard which Arsène Lupin is travelling when he is arrested in the opening story of The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
Lafter Hall, the home of elderly amateur litigant Mr. Frankland on Dartmoor, whose telescope surveillance inadvertently aids Holmes in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
Lincoln's Inn, the legal chambers of Mr Jellicoe, scene of the final confrontation in The Vanishing Man.
The Lincolnshire Fens, where Father Brown discovers an old soldier in an almshouse who witnessed the Black River disaster in Brazil in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Little Purlington, a small Essex village on a branch railway line that is not easy to reach, visited by Holmes in The Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes.
136 Little Ryder Street, the London address of Nathan Garrideb, whose house conceals the counterfeiting press sought by the criminal Killer Evans in The Adventure of the Three Garridebs.
Little Sundersley, an exceptionally remote and lonely village on the East Coast of England, the setting of a crime in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Liverpool, the port where Jefferson Hope tracked Drebber as he attempted to sail to America in A Study in Scarlet.
Liverpool Street station, where Father Brown alights confident he has not lost the criminal during a London pursuit in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A local inn standing alone on the main road, described as the only real and Christian inn in the neighbourhood of the hamlet in The Secret of Father Brown.
London, the central setting for Lupin's English escapades involving Holmlock Shears and for Father Brown's arrival for the Eucharistic Congress.

Lord's Cricket Ground is the prestigious London cricket venue where Raffles steals a lady's necklace in Gentlemen and Players from The Amateur Cracksman, using his celebrity as a cricketer as social cover.
Lower Burke Street, Kensington, the location associated with Culverton Smith's activities, referenced during The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax.
Lucknow Mansions in Hampstead, the residential address of a character connected to the invisible man case in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Ludbury, the town where a character's father owned the Red Fish inn and where the character worked serving customers in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Lyceum Theatre on the Strand, where Holmes and Watson meet Miss Morstan at the third pillar on the left in The Sign of the Four.
Maiwand, the Afghan battle in 1880 where Watson was struck by a Jezail bullet and left with a wound that shapes his civilian life in A Study in Scarlet.
Major Putnam's handsome suburban villa with a verandah, past which a character hears a suspicious noise in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Mallowood Abbey, the country seat of Lady Mounteagle, where a successful bazaar is held at the opening of a story in The Secret of Father Brown.
A fortress in Malta, the site of a third sinister incident experienced by characters in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Mandeville's private study at the far dark end of a long passage, where the theatrical manager's distress is evident in The Secret of Father Brown.
Mandeville's theatre, the backstage of which Mr Mundon Mandeville the theatrical manager briskly walks through in The Secret of Father Brown.
Signora Maroni's locked dressing room, from which she defied everyone through the door during events in The Secret of Father Brown.
A favourite restaurant overlooking the Mediterranean (Italian Riviera), where a character dines and reflects in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Meiringen, a Swiss village where Holmes and Watson stayed before the final confrontation at the Reichenbach Falls in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.

Merripit House is Jack Stapleton's bleak former farmhouse on Dartmoor in The Hound of the Baskervilles, where the hound is kennelled in an outhouse and from which Stapleton sets his trap for Sir Henry.
A small ale-house or eating-house tucked into a mews passage between two tall mansions in Brompton or Kensington, frequented in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Miller Hill, a location in the Vermissa Valley mining district in The Valley of Fear.
Monte Carlo, where Daubrecq fled and was pursued by Lupin and Clarisse, who drugged him there with a sedative-laced drink in The Crystal Stopper.
Mount Meru, a legendary sacred peak from Hindu mythology, where a colossal carving of the God of Gods is said to stand in a cavern, referenced in The Secret of Father Brown.
Lord Mounteagle's private museum of Asiatic art, which characters pass through en route to other rooms of his house in The Secret of Father Brown.
Musgrave Castle, an ancient square Norman keep on the Northumbrian border rarely visited by strangers, home of the eccentric Squire Musgrave in The Secret of Father Brown.
Musgrave Moss, the estate of the reclusive old Colonel Musgrave, offered to Father Brown as a destination for a northern road trip in The Secret of Father Brown.
The Natural Philosophy Quarterly, an unreadable academic journal in which John Boulnois published his celebrated philosophical articles in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Nauvoo on the Mississippi, the original Mormon settlement from which the community was driven and then migrated to Utah in A Study in Scarlet.
Netley, the army medical training base in Hampshire where Watson completed the prescribed course for army surgeons before being posted abroad in A Study in Scarlet.
The Nevada Mountains, the route taken by two fugitives crossing the desert in A Study in Scarlet.
The Norfolk Broads, the intended destination of a small boat journey through the little Norfolk rivers in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The North Sea, described as an endless outer wall of blue-green marble visible from the cliffs in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Turkish Bath in Northumberland Avenue, London, where Holmes and Watson meet in The Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes.
The Northumberland Hotel, where Sir Henry Baskerville stays upon arriving in London and receives the anonymous warning letter in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
The Northumbrian coast, where the road runs along a maze of sand dunes and rank sea meadows in The Secret of Father Brown.
The Central Criminal Court at the Old Bailey, past whose grim walls Jervis walks before witnessing the trial scene in The Red Thumb Mark.
General Outram's handsome old-fashioned house in the West End, visited by Father Brown in The Secret of Father Brown.
Oxford, the university city near which the philosopher John Boulnois lives in his little house in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Palmyra, the ancient Syrian city where a sacred gift was bestowed on a character during a journey through the Mormon story in A Study in Scarlet.
The Paradise of Thieves, a hidden hollow in the Apennines used as the stronghold of the brigand Montano, its name known to those who live nearby in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Parc Monceau area of Paris, near which both the d'Imblevalle house and the Countess Zalti's apartment are located, in multiple Lupin stories.

Paris is the city from which Arsène Lupin directs his criminal network in multiple Lupin stories, and where key events such as the lottery office scene in The Blonde Lady and operations in The Crystal Stopper are set.
Park Lane, London, associated in Father Brown's narrative with monstrous dark secrets and evangelical crimes in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Pendragon family's old Elizabethan black-beamed house, briefly glimpsed from the road by passing characters in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Pendragon Park, the estate of Sir Claude Champion, visited by journalists and party guests for a celebrated fete in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
