Pendragon Island and its tower, built by the Admiral's ancestors in the days of Queen Elizabeth I and connected to family legends of an ancient curse, in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Peshawar, the base hospital to which Watson was invalided after being wounded at Maiwand, before his eventual return to England in A Study in Scarlet.
A modern-art picture gallery wandered through by Father Brown at the start of a mystery in The Secret of Father Brown.
A pig-sty on a farm attached to a country house, into which the violent Vaudrey dragged and threw a man in backstory from The Secret of Father Brown.
Pilgrim's Pond, the palatial country estate of the hospitable Mr Todd, where Lord Falconroy is entertained in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Poldhu Bay, the small Cornish hamlet where Holmes is spending a retirement holiday when the Devil's Foot poisonings begin in The Adventure of the Devil's Foot.
Pondicherry Lodge, the walled private estate in Norwood belonging to Major Sholto and later his son Bartholomew, where the Agra Treasure was hidden and Bartholomew Sholto was found murdered.
Port Said, an Egyptian port city where a second sinister incident occurred in a lodging during characters' journey home in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Poultney Square, Brixton, the address where the criminal Holy Peters (Henry Peters) lodges during his London operations in The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax.
Princetown Prison on Dartmoor, from which the murderer Selden has escaped before events in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
The Priory School near Mackleton in the north of England, a private school from which the Duke of Holdernesse's son is kidnapped in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.

Prison de la Santé, the Paris prison where Arsène Lupin is held under the guard of twelve warders between his arrest and trial, and from which he continues to operate in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.
A purple tent belonging to the Master of the Mountain, a world-famous fortune-teller by crystals and chiromancy, at a fair in The Secret of Father Brown.
Putney police station, from which a police officer set out to find a suspect in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Queen Square in Bloomsbury, where John Bellingham kept his private Egyptian collection in a museum that became the scene of his disappearance in The Vanishing Man.
A long, low white-and-pale-green house with a verandah in north London, the home of the Quinton family and setting of a murder in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Reed House on Reed Island, Norfolk, the address of Prince Saradine, where Father Brown and Flambeau arrive seeking him in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Reichenbach Fall in Switzerland, the mountain torrent where Holmes and Moriarty apparently fell to their deaths, referenced in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.

Reichenbach Falls is the Swiss mountain gorge where Sherlock Holmes and Professor Moriarty fall together in The Final Problem from The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, Holmes faking his death to escape Moriarty's organisation.
A Revival Mission in Greenford, attended through the night by a character who uses it as an alibi in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Riga, the Latvian city where a fugitive captain is living under Poste Restante in The Secret of Father Brown.
The Rio Grande, a river in the vast desolate American landscape crossed by characters in A Study in Scarlet.
A river and boathouse adjoining the garden where Father Brown paces while waiting for events to unfold in The Secret of Father Brown.
The River Thames is the waterway Holmes, Watson, Athelney Jones and the police chase the steam launch Aurora down in The Sign of the Four, ending with Tonga's death in the dark ooze of its bottom.
The Rue Clapeyron in Paris, the street surrounded by Ganimard's men and the scene of confrontations involving Holmlock Shears in The Blonde Lady.
Rue Murillo, near the Parc Monceau, the Paris street where M. d'Imblevalle's house stands and from which the Jewish lamp is stolen in The Blonde Lady (Arsène Lupin).
Salisbury Plain, invoked by Father Brown as a vast space that a man's personality could fill or dwarf, in The Innocence of Father Brown.

Salt Lake City, the capital of Utah founded by Mormon settlers under Brigham Young's leadership in A Study in Scarlet, where John Ferrier and Lucy settle and where Jefferson Hope's doomed love for Lucy unfolds.
A stretch of sandhills on the east coast of Scotland with a deep cranny leading to the beach, the site of a duel in The Secret of Father Brown.
Saxe-Coburg Square, a small dingy London square containing the pawnbroker's shop behind which the Red-Headed League plot is conducted in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes.
Scarborough, a Yorkshire seaside resort where characters walk along the sea-front and events occur in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Scotland Yard, the headquarters of the Metropolitan Police, visited by Thorndyke to regularise his position and by characters from The Innocence of Father Brown.
Seawood, a fictional Essex coast seaside resort described as Lord Pooley's social experiment, the setting of a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The large bandstand at Seawood, shaped like a giant mushroom on six legs, a landmark in the seaside resort setting in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A small, flashy seafront hotel at Seawood with a saloon bar, typical of Victorian seaside resorts, in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A gilded and gaudy pavilion in a small brilliantly lit square at Seawood, the venue of a fight scene in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Seine, the Paris river overlooked by high walls and tall poplars at Valentin's house in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Convict Settlement at Sequah in a named American state, where a shout for help triggers events in a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Shoscombe church crypt, the dismal underground chamber where Sir Robert Norberton secretly disposes of his sister's body to prevent discovery of her death before the Derby in Shoscombe Old Place.
Shoscombe Old Place, a Berkshire country estate owned by the late Lady Beatrice Falder where Holmes investigates suspicious behaviour by her brother in The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place.
A precipice in Sicily over which a husband appears to have flung himself in an alleged suicide, a detail from the dark history of Prince Saradine in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Sierra Blanco, a mountain range in the vast desolate American interior traversed by characters in A Study in Scarlet.
The Sierra Nevada mountain range, the western boundary of the great American alkali plain in A Study in Scarlet.

The Sign of the Broken Sword, a roadside inn whose dimly lit sign shows a sabre hilt and shortened blade in false archaic lettering, the setting of a Father Brown story in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Smith's Farm, the lodging of a character named Carver who is connected to events in The Secret of Father Brown.
Soho, the London quarter evoked in a restaurant's atmosphere that seemed to have strayed from there in The Innocence of Father Brown.
South Africa is the theatre of the Boer War where Raffles and Bunny serve in the Imperial Yeomanry in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman, Raffles dying at the Battle of Spion Kop.
Holmes's small farm on the South Downs, five miles from Eastbourne, where he has retired to keep bees when the final story His Last Bow takes place.
The South Kensington Museum (Victoria and Albert Museum), where a character's brother spent much time, mentioned in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
A small seaside watering-place in South Wales where characters lived as children, recalled in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Square Lamartine near the Porte Dauphine in Paris, the location of Daubrecq's house watched by Lupin in The Crystal Stopper.
St. Bridget's Bay, a coastal inlet where the footprints of two men converge and mingle on the shore in John Thorndyke's Cases.
St. Clare's churchyard, where a massive recumbent metal soldier marks the general's monument in starlight, in The Innocence of Father Brown.
St. Dominic's Church and its adjoining presbytery, where Father Brown serves as priest in The Secret of Father Brown.
St. Francis Xavier's Church in Camberwell, the London parish to which Father Brown is attached in The Innocence of Father Brown.
St. Helena, the South Atlantic island off which John Douglas is reported lost overboard in a gale, Moriarty's arranged revenge described in the Epilogue of The Valley of Fear.
The College of St. Luke's, an Oxford college where a scholarship examination theft occurs in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
St. Mary Axe, the location of Hornby's business premises where Inspector Sanderson found bloodstains during the investigation in The Red Thumb Mark.
The small church of St. Mungo, Father Brown's parish in a story in The Innocence of Father Brown.
St Oliver's School, a private school mentioned in a Father Brown story as the setting or background for events involving the clergy detective's investigation.
St. Petersburg, the Russian imperial capital referenced in A Study in Scarlet in connection with Jefferson Hope's tracking of Drebber and Stangerson across Europe.
Stagville, a settlement in the Vermissa Valley mining district of The Valley of Fear.
Stoke Moran, the ancient Surrey manor house of Dr. Grimesby Roylott where the mystery of the Speckled Band unfolds, centred on a secret ventilating shaft connecting the bedrooms.
A private hotel in the Strand, Watson's first London lodging when he arrives after Afghanistan in A Study in Scarlet.
A large, silent, seemingly lifeless suburban labyrinth through whose corners Father Brown and another character repeatedly turn in The Secret of Father Brown.
The Temple Gardens in London, where Flambeau and Father Brown sit at sunset discussing a case in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The River Thames, visible as a sunset-coloured river from a terrace used by characters in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The Albany, a prestigious set of bachelor chambers on Piccadilly where Raffles keeps his rooms throughout The Amateur Cracksman.
The Aquarium, a London entertainment venue to which a character is sent to perform tricks after success in the show business in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The Haven in Lewisham, the house where Josiah Amberley imprisoned and gassed his wife and her lover, the scene of crime discovered by Holmes in the Adventure of the Retired Colourman.

Thor Bridge in Hampshire, the stone bridge over a lake at Thor Place where Mrs. Neil Gibson is found shot, the crime appearing to implicate governess Grace Dunbar in Holmes's last Case-Book investigation.
The Three Gables at Harrow Weald, the home of widow Mary Maberley that is the scene of a plot by Isadora Klein to buy and destroy a revealing manuscript in the Adventure of the Three Gables.

The Tin Mine Island in Grimpen Mire is the hidden location in The Hound of the Baskervilles where Stapleton stables and conceals his phosphorescent hound, itself eventually found dead there after Stapleton flees into the bog.
A hamlet shop that functions as both tobacconist's and barber's, a common dual-trade establishment noted in The Secret of Father Brown.
Tottenham, a north London district reached by Father Brown somewhat mysteriously while carrying all his parcels in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Tredannick Wartha, the remote Cornish hamlet where the Adventure of the Devil's Foot is set, notable for the mysterious deaths of the Tregennis family attributed to an African root poison.
Tufnell Park, a north London neighbourhood which Flambeau has just reached at the start of a pursuit in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Tunbridge Wells, a Kent spa town referenced in connection with a character in The Valley of Fear.
Tuxbury Old Park in Staffordshire, the setting of the Adventure of the Blanched Soldier in the Case-Book, where James Dodd seeks to discover why his friend Godfrey Emsworth has disappeared.

Union House (McGinty's Saloon) is the Vermissa saloon and Scowrer headquarters run by Boss McGinty in The Valley of Fear, where Jack McMurdo first presents himself to the Bodymaster to join Lodge 341.
The University of London, the institution where Dr. Watson took his degree of Doctor of Medicine before proceeding to Netley and subsequently to service in Afghanistan.

Upper Norwood, a south London suburb where Jonas Oldacre has his timber yard, the site of the apparent crime scene in The Adventure of the Norwood Builder, with Holmes proving Oldacre faked his own murder.
The Vale of Health, a popular hollow on Hampstead Heath where characters gather in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Valentin's house in Paris, an old building with high walls and tall poplars almost overhanging the Seine, where a murder occurs in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Colonel Varney's house, outside whose entrance a small strip of pavement has been suspiciously washed clean, observed by Father Brown in The Secret of Father Brown.
Sir Arthur Vaudrey's great house by the river, from which he set out on his last brisk walk in The Secret of Father Brown.

Vermissa, the Pennsylvania coal-mining township at the centre of Part II of The Valley of Fear, home to Lodge 341 of the Scowrers and McGinty's Union House saloon, where John Douglas lived as Jack McMurdo.

Vermissa Valley (Pennsylvania), the coal-mining region in The Valley of Fear where the criminal Scowrers held sway, encompassing the town of Vermissa, Crow Hill Mine, and the surrounding Gilmerton Mountains.
The Vernon Hotel in Belgravia, an exclusive institution hosting the annual dinner of the Twelve True Fishermen, the setting for a silver-theft story in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Vernon Lodge, a house appearing in the Sherlock Holmes corpus that serves as a location connected to one of Holmes's investigations.
Versailles, where Suzanne Gerbois was a college student when she purchased the lottery ticket sought by Lupin in The Blonde Lady.
The Victoria district of London, through whose streets and squares Father Brown wanders during an investigation in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A building in Victoria Street, London, where a character has an office halfway up, in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Vienna, where Prince Saradine lived for a period before events in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Villa Marie, a villa that appears in the Arsène Lupin stories as a location linked to Lupin's adventures or those of his associates.
An old village green in an unnamed hamlet around which a cluster of new houses has grown, described in The Secret of Father Brown.

Von Bork's House, the residence of German spy Von Bork on the Essex coast that Holmes, operating undercover as Altamont, raids on the eve of World War I in His Last Bow to seize the spy's intelligence files.
The Vosges, a French mountain region where a German spy was caught carrying a document with a War Office formula in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Wagga Wagga at 379 Mafeking Avenue, Putney, the address given as a cab destination in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
The War Office in London, whose secretary's desk conceals a grey envelope with a powder formula, mentioned in a spy document in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
Washington D.C., where Lady Outram met the man later known as the Marquis of Marne when he was an attache thirty years before events in The Secret of Father Brown.
Waterloo Bridge in London, crossed by Jefferson Hope on his route to a railway station before his eventual capture in A Study in Scarlet.
Westminster, the London district to which Father Brown dispatches a letter and where various institutions are located in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Westminster Abbey, where a memorial to General St. Clare stands, its presence used to illustrate the hero's false reputation in The Innocence of Father Brown.
Westminster Bridge, crossed by two men at the start of events in The Innocence of Father Brown.
A new pile of flats near Westminster Abbey where a character has his offices, the setting of a story in The Innocence of Father Brown.
The White Hart Tavern, a London landmark near which a body is found on the roadway in A Study in Scarlet.

Widow MacNamara's Boarding House, a lodging establishment in the Scowrers territory that serves as a gathering point for characters within the secret society plot recounted in the backstory of The Valley of Fear.
Wilkinson's Motor Garage, outside which a laughable occurrence (a staged fight) takes place, reported in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

Wisteria Lodge, the house near Esher, Surrey rented by the murdered Aloysius Garcia, whose sinister household and connections to a Central American dictator form the central mystery of The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge.
The Yellowstone River in the north, the upper boundary of the vast desolate American alkali plain described in A Study in Scarlet.

The Yew Alley is the long walk between twelve-foot hedges at Baskerville Hall where Sir Charles Baskerville waits at the gate on the night of his death in The Hound of the Baskervilles, the hound's footprints found at the end of the path.
York College, mentioned in connection with a character who had accumulated out-of-the-way knowledge in A Study in Scarlet.
The Yorkshire moors, traversed swiftly by characters heading north on a road trip in The Secret of Father Brown.
Yoxley Old Place, a country house taken by an elderly man and the scene of a mysterious death in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.
Sherlock Holmes as Detective is the narrative role node anchoring Holmes's function across the Holmes saga, cited at the opening of The Valley of Fear where he is addressed impatiently by Watson.
The narrative role of Sherlock Holmes acting as his own first-person narrator in The Adventure of the Lion's Mane, one of only two Case-Book stories told directly by Holmes rather than by Watson.
Fred Porlock as Informant is the narrative role of the anonymous insider who sends Holmes a coded cipher warning about the coming Birlstone murder in The Valley of Fear.

Narrator (Watson) is the narrative role of Dr. John H. Watson, who transcribes his letters to Holmes from Devonshire in The Hound of the Baskervilles and recounts the framing events of The Valley of Fear.
Christopher Jervis M.D., acting as narrator of Part II and subsequent stories in The Adventures of Dr Thorndyke.
Christopher Jervis M.D., the narrator of all cases in John Thorndyke's Cases.
Christopher Jervis M.D., the first-person narrator of all chapters of The Red Thumb Mark.
Dr Paul Berkeley M.B., the narrator throughout The Vanishing Man, distinct from the usual narrator Jervis.

Birdy Edwards as Undercover Agent is the narrative role of John Douglas / Jack McMurdo, the Pinkerton detective who penetrated the Scowrers in The Valley of Fear and revealed himself with the words 'I am Birdy Edwards!'.

McGinty as Villain is the narrative role of Boss McGinty / Black Jack McGinty, the corrupt saloon-keeper and Bodymaster of Vermissa Valley Lodge 341 who drives the Scowrer terror in Part II of The Valley of Fear.

The Arsene Lupin saga, Maurice Leblanc's series of novels and stories following the French gentleman-burglar Arsene Lupin who baffled Inspector Ganimard, the English detective Holmlock Shears, and all the police of Europe, across The Extraordinary Adventures, The Blonde Lady, The Hollow Needle, and The Crystal Stopper.

The Dr. Thorndyke series, a collection of scientific detective novels and short stories by R. Austin Freeman featuring Dr. John Evelyn Thorndyke, a medico-legal expert at the Inner Temple who solves crimes through forensic science.

Father Brown is the node representing the Father Brown saga in the graph, grouping Chesterton's three collections (The Innocence, The Wisdom, and The Secret of Father Brown) in which the small Essex priest solves crimes through moral imagination.

The Raffles saga, a series of crime stories by E.W. Hornung following A.J. Raffles, the gentleman cracksman and amateur cricketer, and his loyal companion Bunny Manders, published across three collections and one novel.

The Sherlock Holmes saga, Arthur Conan Doyle's series of detective fiction following the cases of the eccentric consulting detective Sherlock Holmes and his companion Dr. Watson, beginning with A Study in Scarlet.
The Gloria Scott, a convict ship that went down in a gale off Falmouth, whose story forms Holmes's earliest remembered case in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.

The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier, a Case-Book story narrated by Holmes in which he investigates why James Dodd's friend Godfrey Emsworth has been hidden away at Tuxbury Old Park, discovering he has pseudo-leprosy.

The Adventure of the Creeping Man, a Case-Book story in which Holmes investigates Professor Presbury of Camford University, who has been secretly injecting himself with a serum derived from langur monkeys, causing animal-like nocturnal behaviour.

The Adventure of the Illustrious Client, a Case-Book story in which Holmes and Watson are engaged by an anonymous client to prevent Violet de Merville from marrying the Austrian murderer Baron Adelbert Gruner, culminating in a vitriol attack on the Baron.

The Adventure of the Lion's Mane, a Case-Book story narrated by the retired Holmes in which the science master Fitzroy McPherson is found dying on a Sussex beach, with Ian Murdoch suspected until a Cyanea capillata jellyfish is identified as the killer.
The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone, a Case-Book story in which Holmes uses a gramophone and a wax dummy to trap Count Negretto Sylvius and Sam Merton and recover the stolen Mazarin Stone crown jewel.

The Adventure of the Retired Colourman, a Case-Book story in which Holmes exposes Josiah Amberley for gassing his wife and her lover Dr. Ray Ernest and concealing their bodies, with Mr. Barker assisting independently.

The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place, a Case-Book story in which Holmes exposes Sir Robert Norberton's concealment of his sister Lady Beatrice Falder's death in order to preserve her identity until his racehorse Shoscombe Prince wins the Derby.

The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire, a Case-Book story in which Holmes investigates Robert Ferguson's Peruvian wife, who appeared to be biting their infant son but was actually sucking poison injected by her jealous stepson Jack.

The Problem of Thor Bridge, a Case-Book story in which Holmes proves governess Grace Dunbar innocent of murdering Neil Gibson's Brazilian wife Maria, who staged her own death to frame the governess out of jealousy.

The Adventure of the Three Gables, a Case-Book story in which Holmes investigates a mysterious offer to buy Mary Maberley's house contents, tracing the scheme to the wealthy widow Isadora Klein who sought to suppress Douglas Maberley's novel.

The Adventure of the Three Garridebs is a story in The Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes in which John Garrideb (Killer Evans) invents a scheme to find non-existent fellow Garridebs as a pretext to access a counterfeiting press hidden beneath a London flat.

The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger, a Case-Book story in which a scarred woman named Eugenia Ronder confesses to Holmes the truth about how her abusive showman husband Ronder was killed by their lion, with the strongman Leonardo complicit.
Alexander Teixeira de Mattos is the English translator of Maurice Leblanc's Arsène Lupin novels, credited on The Blonde Lady and The Hollow Needle in the corpus.
Edgar Jepson is the English translator credited on some volumes of Maurice Leblanc's Arsène Lupin novels in the corpus.
George Morehead is the English translator credited on some of the Arsène Lupin volumes in the corpus.
A Study in Scarlet, the first Sherlock Holmes novel by Arthur Conan Doyle, introduces Holmes and Watson and presents the murder of Enoch Drebber as a tale of revenge rooted in Mormon Utah.

A Thief in the Night, E.W. Hornung's third Raffles story collection featuring A.J. Raffles and Bunny Manders in adventures including A Bad Night, A Trap to Catch a Cracksman, and The Criminologists' Club.

The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes, Doyle's first short-story collection featuring Holmes and Watson, comprises twelve cases including A Scandal in Bohemia, The Red-Headed League, and The Adventure of the Copper Beeches.

Arsène Lupin (novelisation of the play), co-written by Edgar Jepson and Maurice Leblanc, follows the Duke of Charmerace — Lupin in disguise — through a cat-and-mouse investigation with detective Guerchard at the Gournay-Martin household.
The Decameron is Giovanni Boccaccio's 14th-century Italian story collection referenced in the mystery corpus as a cultural touchstone.

The Book of Life is Holmes's monograph on observation and deduction, excerpted in A Study in Scarlet as an ambitious article arguing that a trained observer can infer almost anything from a brief encounter.
The Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes, Doyle's final short-story collection, gathers twelve late adventures including The Blanched Soldier, The Illustrious Client, and The Lion's Mane.
De Jure Inter Gentes is the book of maritime law that Holmes notes was carried by Captain Morstan, as mentioned in The Sign of the Four.

Gaboriau's Lecoq Novels are mentioned in A Study in Scarlet when Watson compares Holmes to the fictional detective Lecoq, works that Holmes dismisses as models of what he considers inferior detection.

His Last Bow, Arthur Conan Doyle's collection of eight Sherlock Holmes stories including The Adventure of the Dying Detective, The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax, and the title spy story set on the eve of the First World War.

John Thorndyke's Cases, a collection of short stories by R. Austin Freeman narrated by Dr. Christopher Jervis and featuring the medico-legal detective Dr. John Thorndyke, including cases such as The Aluminium Dagger and The Mandarin's Pearl.

The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, Doyle's second short-story collection, includes eleven cases such as The Final Problem, The Greek Interpreter, and Silver Blaze, ending with Holmes's apparent death at Reichenbach Falls.
Mr. Justice Raffles is E. W. Hornung's 1909 Raffles novel in which Raffles takes justice into his own hands against the moneylender Dan Levy.

Poe's Dupin Stories are Edgar Allan Poe's detective tales featuring C. Auguste Dupin, mentioned in A Study in Scarlet when Watson flatters Holmes by comparing him to Dupin, a comparison Holmes politely deflects.
Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman, E.W. Hornung's second Raffles collection featuring stories including A Jubilee Present, The Fate of Faustina, and The Knees of the Gods.

The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke (also published as The Singing Bone), R. Austin Freeman's short-story collection narrated by Dr. Jervis and featuring inverted-detective stories including The Case of Oscar Brodski and The Echo of a Mutiny.

The Amateur Cracksman, E.W. Hornung's first Raffles collection introducing gentleman-burglar A.J. Raffles and his narrator Harry 'Bunny' Manders across stories such as A Costume Piece and The Gift of the Emperor.
The Blonde Lady, Maurice Leblanc's novel recounting the duel of wits between Arsene Lupin and the English detective Holmlock Shears across two episodes: The Blonde Lady and The Jewish Lamp.

The Crystal Stopper, a Maurice Leblanc Lupin novel, follows Lupin's desperate effort to recover a crystal stopper concealing the list of twenty-seven corrupt deputies in order to save his condemned associate Gilbert.

The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin, Gentleman-Burglar, Leblanc's first Lupin collection, introduces the charming thief through nine stories including his arrest, prison escape, and clashes with Holmlock Shears.

The Hollow Needle, a Maurice Leblanc Lupin novel, follows the young detective Isidore Beautrelet as he uncovers the centuries-old secret of the hollow cliff needle at Étretat, which is Lupin's ultimate refuge.

The Innocence of Father Brown (1911), Chesterton's first Father Brown collection, introduces the priest-detective through twelve stories including The Blue Cross, The Invisible Man, and The Hammer of God.
The Red Thumb Mark, R. Austin Freeman's first Dr. Thorndyke novel, centres on a disputed fingerprint and a diamond theft that Thorndyke investigates using scientific forensic methods.

The Return of Sherlock Holmes, the third Doyle short-story collection, opens with Holmes's resurrection after Reichenbach and gathers thirteen adventures including the Dancing Men, Charles Augustus Milverton, and the Golden Pince-Nez.

The Secret of Father Brown (1927), Chesterton's fourth Father Brown collection, gathers eight stories including The Chief Mourner of Marne, The Mirror of the Magistrate, and the frame story revealing Father Brown's method.
The Valley of Fear, a Sherlock Holmes novel by Doyle, pairs a locked-room murder at Birlstone Manor with a flashback to a violent Pennsylvanian coal-mining lodge terrorised by the Scowrers.

The Vanishing Man (also known as The Eye of Osiris), R. Austin Freeman's novel in which Dr. Thorndyke assists the narrator Dr. Paul Berkeley in a case involving a missing Egyptologist whose will and identity are disputed.
The Wisdom of Father Brown (1914), Chesterton's second Father Brown collection, presents twelve more cases including The Absence of Mr Glass, The God of the Gongs, and The Fairy Tale of Father Brown.
The Hound of the Baskervilles, a Sherlock Holmes novel by Doyle, investigates the legend of a supernatural hound menacing the Baskerville family on Dartmoor, with Stapleton unmasked as the culprit.

The Sign of the Four, Doyle's second Holmes novel, follows Holmes and Watson as they trace a stolen Agra treasure through a network of intrigue involving a one-legged man and a pygmy archer on the Thames.
La Vie de Bohème is the work mentioned in the Arsène Lupin stories.
